Agent MemorySpec-Driven DevelopmentVerifiability (as the Quality Bar)
illogical
The thought experiment ('if coding became instant') proves too much — it assumes zero implementation cost, but actual AI implementation is not zero-cost. The gap between 'minutes' and 'instant' may matter more than the post acknowledges.
Sam Hatoum's Post
incomplete
The article identifies executive non-perception of relevance as the root cause of failed buy-in, then assigns 100% of the remedy to the seller. The corresponding institutional remedy — executives learning to receive, sponsor allocation, structured 'bring-up' channels — is mentioned (Mankins & Litre) and immediately dropped. This is the conversation's largest hidden asymmetry: it accepts a structural diagnosis but offers only individual prescriptions.
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Cutler's work on institutional overload and operating-system design (TBM); Edmondson on psychological safety as a receiving infrastructure; sociotechnical literature on decision rights.
Managing Up & InfluencePsychological Safety
incomplete
Power, incentives, budget authority, and zero-sum redistribution are absent. The stakeholder map (ally / blocker / fence-sitter) is purely affective; it does not model who controls the resources, whose headcount shrinks, or whose KPIs the idea threatens.
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Pfeffer ('Power'); Bolman & Deal's political frame; March & Simon's bounded rationality and political coalitions; Christensen on incumbent self-protection.
Managing Up & Influence
incomplete
The framework conflates several distinct receiver-behaviours under 'resistance': overload-induced inaction, genuine objection, competing issue-selling, and political vetoing. They demand different responses, but the article treats them as variants of 'we need to dig deeper.' Without a diagnostic, the seller can be told indefinitely to socialize and follow up on what is in fact a hard 'no.'
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Argyris on undiscussables and skilled incompetence; Kahneman/Klein on diagnostic decision-making under ambiguity.
incomplete
The article assumes the seller possesses time, network, political cover, and emotional regulation as preconditions. It does not address how to do issue selling when one or more of those is unavailable — i.e., the typical condition of overloaded mid-level managers.
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Time to Think (Kline) on the conditions ideas need; Stolen Focus on attention as precondition; Fearless Change patterns for resource-poor change agents.
Attention as a Hijackable ResourceManaging Up & Influence
incomplete
Adler's mode of incompleteness: the article reduces idea transmission to tactics and scripts ('three questions,' 'map the stakeholders,' 'aim for worth exploring'). The structural conditions under which those tactics actually work — slack in the executive agenda, trust between layers, a culture that distinguishes loyalty to leaders from loyalty to ideas — are presupposed and never named. The advice is therefore most useful exactly where it is least needed (healthy organizations) and least useful where it is most needed (organizations whose pathology is what makes upward selling necessary in the first place).
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Fearless Change (Manns & Rising) — patterns for getting ideas through resistant organizations; Time to Think (Nancy Kline); Cutler's 'institutionalized overload' essays; Donella Meadows on leverage points (rule-changes, paradigms) vs. parameter-changes.
Trust
illogical
Lewin's force-field model is invoked to justify reducing restraining forces, but the conversation conflates two different uses: (a) reduce the *perception* of loss through framing, and (b) reduce the *actual* loss through redesign. Only (a) is illustrated in the cases. The implication that (a) generally substitutes for (b) is not earned by the cases shown.
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: Original Lewin on planned change; revisionist critiques (Burnes, Cummings & Worley) on what force-field analysis can and cannot do.
Managing Up & Influence
uninformed
The article never engages with the considerable literature on why good ideas reliably fail in organizations independent of framing skill — institutional isomorphism, status quo bias of senior leaders facing reputational risk, principal-agent problems, sunk-cost commitments. The implicit theory of failure here is unidimensional (the seller framed it badly).
Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea — read next: DiMaggio & Powell on isomorphism; Jensen on agency costs; Hambrick on upper-echelons theory.
incomplete
Treats overload as a permanent atmospheric condition rather than a designed outcome. The article tells overwhelmed managers how to coach overwhelmed reports about growth, but never asks why aggregate work has expanded or whether it should be cut. The one-sentence 'lighten their load' is the closest it comes — it is not developed and not staffed with examples.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: John Cutler's 'institutionalized overload' (TBM 412 and related) treats overload as a systems problem with feedback loops and organizational levers — read this against Knight's piece to see what's missing when the manager is the only actor in the frame.
Feedback Loops
incomplete
The organization as a unit of analysis is absent. No discussion of role architecture, promotion criteria, learning budgets, internal mobility programs, or HR systems. The implicit model is: each manager improvises a development culture inside their span of control, regardless of structure.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: Org-design literature on internal labor markets and skill architectures (e.g., Cappelli on talent on demand) — or Cutler on org operating systems — would supply the missing layer.
Orchestration Tax / Human Review Bottleneck
incompleteclosed
The claim that breadth ('stay wide') hedges automation risk while specialization concentrates it is asserted, not argued, and runs against historical evidence that deep expertise often commands a premium during disruption. Which skills genuinely survive — and on what evidence — is not engaged.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: Empirical work on AI exposure by occupation/skill (Eloundou et al., Brynjolfsson et al.) — and any pushback arguing that depth is now scarcer and more valuable.
✓ closed byItchy Brain (Still Burning: Michael Grinich & Kent Beck on AI adoption, enterprise developer tools) — Supplies the requested pushback on the breadth-hedges-automation thesis: Grinich argues the profession normalizes toward medicine/law-style deep specialization with longer apprenticeships, while flagging the opposing barrier-dropping trend as unresolved.
Agent Skills
incomplete
Risk and accountability are silently transferred to the individual. Career uncertainty caused by employer decisions (layoffs, restructurings, AI deployment) becomes a personal learnability problem. There is no symmetric obligation on the employer.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: Critical management scholarship on the 'enterprising self' and responsibilization (e.g., du Gay, Fleming) — and any HBR-adjacent counterpoint arguing for employer-side commitments.
illogical
'Show your own work' is positioned as the bridge between asking impossible things and maintaining credibility. But if the targets are genuinely impossible (Bidwell's own framing), no amount of leader transparency repairs that; transparency without target reduction is theater. The argument elides the difference.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: Anything on psychological safety as a precondition for honest upward escalation (Edmondson) — to see what 'showing your own work' would need to do to actually change the work, not just narrate it.
Psychological Safety
uninformed
No acknowledgment that 'permission to learn' presumes a manager with real slack and political cover to grant time for non-job-essential study. In overloaded, deliverable-tracked teams, that permission is often not in the manager's actual gift — the article assumes managerial discretion that may not exist.
Supporting Your Employees' Career Growth When Everyone Is Overwhelmed — read next: Cutler on power and space in organizations; flow accounting / slack theory (Tom DeMarco).
DORA & Engineering MetricsLearning Culture & Slack
incomplete
The article never canvasses structural alternatives to adding a C-suite role: explicit resilience portfolio for COO; resilience standing committee with rotating chair; embedding resilience accountability in existing role mandates; redesigning the boundaries between CISO/CRO/COO so the gap disappears. The choice between 'new role' vs. 'redesigned roles' is never made an argument — it is assumed.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Donella Meadows on leverage points (a new actor is a relatively low-leverage intervention compared to changing rules, feedback loops, or paradigms); Cutler on accountability culture and DRIs (whether accountability needs a title or a clearer commitment); Yourdon & Constantine on coupling and cohesion applied to org structures — when do you add a module vs. refactor existing modules?
Feedback Loops
incomplete
No theory of how horizontal authority operates in practice. The CResO is said to have 'real authority' to set standards, make operational decisions during crisis, and report to CEO/board — but during a cascading event the CISO is restoring infrastructure, the COO is running operations, the CRO is managing exposure. The article does not explain by what mechanism the CResO's decisions override or align theirs.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Incident command system literature (NIMS/ICS); how military and emergency services handle cross-functional command; product organizations on dual-track authority (functional vs. mission); papers on matrix organizations that survive vs. collapse.
incomplete
The implicit theory of resilience is person-centric (resilience is governed by appointing an accountable person). But resilience could equally be a property of system design — redundancy, loose coupling, graceful degradation, blast radius limits, runbooks, chaos engineering, fast feedback loops. The article never engages the possibility that resilience scales through architecture and practice rather than through a single executive.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Nassim Taleb on antifragility and skin-in-the-game; SRE / Google production engineering on error budgets and blast radius; Resilience Engineering tradition (Hollnagel, Woods) which explicitly argues resilience is a system property, not an organizational role; chaos engineering literature.
Agent SecurityFeedback LoopsModularity & Coupling
uninformed
No engagement with the Resilience Engineering academic tradition (Hollnagel, Woods, Dekker), which has spent 20+ years arguing resilience is the adaptive capacity of a sociotechnical system — not something that can be 'owned' by a single accountable executive. The article reads as if this body of thought does not exist.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Hollnagel, Woods & Leveson, 'Resilience Engineering: Concepts and Precepts'; David Woods' lectures on graceful extensibility.
Sociotechnical Architecture
illogical
The 'companies that adopted the role' evidence chain has a selection-effect problem: companies that publicly appoint CResOs are companies that have had high-visibility cascading failures (CrowdStrike) or operate in high-cascade-risk industries (finance, pharma). The adoption rate does not establish that the role causes better outcomes — it establishes that organizations with the problem buy the solution. The article treats adoption as evidence of efficacy.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Pfeffer & Sutton, 'Evidence-Based Management'; any introductory text on selection bias in management research.
incomplete
Author conflict-of-interest signal in the closing note: the authors thank executives at Syngenta, Novo Nordisk, Nestlé, and 'Group Resilience' — all members of the UNDRR network the article cites approvingly. The article is partly the UNDRR network's case for itself, but this framing is not surfaced.
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Look up Igor Linkov's affiliations and the UNDRR Network's funding and membership economics.
incomplete
No discussion of failure modes for the CResO function itself: accountability laundering (other executives off-load problems to CResO), resilience theater (dashboards proliferate, actual capability does not improve), single-point-of-failure (a strong CResO leaves and capability collapses), incentive misalignment (CResO scored on time-to-recover incentivizes minimizing reported incidents).
The Case for Hiring a Chief Resilience Officer — read next: Goodhart's Law literature; Cutler on metrics that displace work; security industry's experience with CISO accountability since SOX/Sarbanes-Oxley.
incomplete
Geopolitical and regulatory friction is mentioned only as a tailwind for vertical integration; the article does not engage with the very real possibility that Western governments will increasingly block exactly the deals it recommends (CFIUS, EU FDI screening, Five Eyes pressure). The strategic-choice menu assumes a deal-friendly environment that may not exist.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: Recent CFIUS/EU FDI screening case data; analyses of the Newport Wafer Fab, MagnaChip, or Nexperia interventions; Henry Farrell & Abraham Newman on weaponized interdependence.
incomplete
Survivor bias: every named case is a success. No accounting of failed Chinese acquisitions of Western brands, base rates, or selection effects (e.g., Ding's board roles at H&H and JS Global, disclosed, may shape the case mix). Without a denominator, the playbook may be retrofitted to winners.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: M&A failure-rate literature on Chinese outbound deals (e.g., Lenovo-Motorola, HNA, Anbang); academic work by Peter Williamson, Eden Yin.
Craft Community
incomplete
Organizational mechanism inside the acquirer is treated as a black box — 'they bring digital marketing acumen' is asserted, not decomposed. For a reader interested in capability transfer, the actual operating model (governance, talent flows between Hangzhou and Boston, decision rights) is the most interesting question and is unaddressed.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: Cutler-style organizational systems writing applied to cross-border M&A; case studies of JS Global's actual governance of SharkNinja; literature on ambidextrous organizations.
incomplete
The article reframes outsourcing as the original sin but does not engage why the smiling-curve strategy made sense at the time or what changed (capital costs, supplier learning rates, geopolitics). Without that, the prescription to re-vertically-integrate is unsupported by the same kind of analysis the diagnosis received.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: Suzanne Berger 'Making in America'; recent reshoring economics; semiconductor industrial-policy literature.
incomplete
No treatment of the data, IP, and software dimension — i.e., what happens to customer data, embedded software, OTA update infrastructure, and AI/ML models when a connected-product brand changes hands. For 2026 consumer-electronics deals this is arguably the central question.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: Recent DeepSeek / Chinese AI capability reporting; smart-device data sovereignty frameworks; literature on connected-product M&A.
Data Sovereignty
illogical
Conflates two distinct claims under the 'rewiring the smiling curve' phrase: (1) the acquirer creates value at all three nodes, and (2) value at the middle (manufacturing) is no longer low-margin. Only the first is argued; the second is suggested rhetorically.
How Chinese Firms Are Saving Western Brands — read next: Stan Shih's original smiling-curve papers; recent revisions by Mudambi on geography of value.
AI Labor Disruption
incomplete
Treats energy as a procurement-and-deployment problem (route smaller models, sign PPAs, pick Nordic regions) rather than as a strategic constraint that should reshape AI architecture decisions — e.g., whether to deploy agentic loops at all, whether to build retrieval-heavy systems vs. cached responses, whether to build AI features whose value-per-watt is negative.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: Sasha Luccioni and Hugging Face on AI energy accounting; Andy Masley and effective-altruist energy debates; Kate Crawford 'Atlas of AI' on AI's material substrate.
incomplete
The proposed governance body (CIO + CFO + procurement + sustainability) reproduces the assumption that AI is something the IT/procurement axis manages. It does not engage with the possibility that energy-bounded AI is a board-level capital-allocation question on par with major capex, nor that engineering leadership (CTO, head of AI) should own intelligence-per-watt as a product KPI.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: Recent CTO-track writing on AI infra strategy; capital-allocation literature on industrial transitions.
incomplete
Jevons is invoked rhetorically but the article doesn't engage with the implication: if demand will always grow to consume available efficiency gains, then efficiency-focused recommendations (Action 1, Action 2) only delay the bottleneck for the individual firm without changing the aggregate trajectory. The piece offers no view on whether firms should care about aggregate trajectory.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: Climate-economics literature on rebound effects; Vaclav Smil on energy transitions.
incomplete
No discussion of grid externalities — what happens to non-AI users and communities when hyperscalers and incumbents lock up 20-year nuclear PPAs and Nordic colocation. The framing is purely firm-strategic; the political economy is mentioned ('politically contested') but not analyzed.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: Reporting on data-center power disputes in Virginia, Ireland, and the U.S. Midwest; energy-justice literature.
incomplete
The Great Value Loop framework is presented as a recurring pattern but the article does not interrogate whether the energy era differs structurally — physical infrastructure has 20+ year build timelines, hard geographic constraints, and political sovereignty implications that the previous three layers (connectivity, attention, compute) did not.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: History of electrification (Hughes 'Networks of Power'); literature on infrastructure lock-in and path dependence.
Compute & Energy Infrastructure
uninformed
Does not engage with on-device / edge inference as an alternative architectural lever for reducing data-center electricity demand. The five actions all assume cloud-resident inference.
Your Company Needs an Energy Strategy for AI's Next Phase — read next: Apple Intelligence and on-device LLM literature; small-language-model research from Mistral, Microsoft Phi, etc.
Local AI
incomplete
Same system-shift-framed-as-practice-tweak pattern recurring across the user's recent extractions (Cutler, Osmani, Reed, HBR Career-Growth-Overwhelmed). Duffy diagnoses a structural failure of the manager layer to keep pace, then prescribes individual behavior changes that leave organizational architecture, role boundaries, decision rights, and compensation untouched.
Managers Are Struggling to Keep Up with the AI Productivity Boom — read next: Pair with Cutler 'AI Won't Save the Kingdoms We Built' and Osmani 'Orchestration Tax is You' for the syntopical thread. The unanswered question: when is the manager role itself the bottleneck-design, and what would an AI-era org chart actually look like?
Hierarchy & Org ChartsOrchestration Tax / Human Review Bottleneck
incomplete
Treats manager decision-rights over AI-produced output as unproblematic — but if AI generates faster, broader, and (with verification) better output than the manager's review can keep up with, the manager's authority is conventional, not epistemic. The piece never asks whose judgment is actually load-bearing.
Managers Are Struggling to Keep Up with the AI Productivity Boom — read next: Cathy O'Neil on algorithmic accountability; Bainbridge 'Ironies of Automation' for what happens to operator authority when automation outpaces oversight (Paul Reed extract).
Ironies of Automation
incomplete
The AI-as-EQ-coach example is presented as a clever augmentation, but if managers cannot find time for warm communication, that's an org-design failure being masked by a tool. The article never asks whether normalized DM-auditing by AI changes the implicit contract between manager and report.
Managers Are Struggling to Keep Up with the AI Productivity Boom — read next: Surveillance/trust literature; HBR 'On Managing Yourself' for the older norm being replaced.
Burnout & RecoveryTrust
illogical
'Increase frequency to course-correct faster' (daily syncs) is offered as a remedy for manager overload, but adding meetings to an already overloaded manager is the textbook overload-amplifier. Osmani's orchestration-tax argument applies to managers too.
Managers Are Struggling to Keep Up with the AI Productivity Boom — read next: Cutler TBM on institutionalized overload; Osmani 'Orchestration Tax is You'.
Orchestration Tax / Human Review Bottleneck
incomplete
'89% of leaders agree AI has accelerated work' — but the article assumes that acceleration is sustained throughput rather than a transient burst as teams adopt new tooling. No engagement with the possibility that the 'AI productivity boom' is a one-time S-curve, not the new steady state.
Managers Are Struggling to Keep Up with the AI Productivity Boom — read next: DORA reports on AI's effect on engineering throughput; longitudinal AI-adoption studies.
DORA & Engineering MetricsTechnology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
incomplete
The thesis that traditional leadership doesn't work with autonomous teams is stated as observed fact ('vi så i NAV') but never specified. Which behaviors of old-style leadership failed? What were the visible symptoms? Without examples, the claim functions as a slogan rather than an argument — appropriate for a welcome episode, but the show will need to deliver the substance.
0) Velkommen til Heis
Team Autonomy
incompleteclosed
'Leadership that plays on the team' (spiller på lag med teammodellen) is the show's positive claim but is left almost entirely uncashed in this episode. The metaphor is suggestive (cooperative, aligned) but does not yet distinguish this style from related ideas — servant leadership, Team Topologies-style enabling teams, or Cutler-style 'conditions over directives' leadership.
0) Velkommen til Heis
✓ closed by10) Una Aamodt Mathisen om å lede når du ikke har fasiten — Cashes out what 'leadership that plays on the team' means in practice: publicly acknowledging not knowing, a three-phase role (listening, hypothesis-shaped direction, closing the learning loop), direction and autonomy as mutually constitutive, and restraint toward distrust signals.
incomplete
The argument that this kind of leadership requires experience 'from working in teams and with technology' is asserted but not defended. It excludes by implication leaders from non-engineering backgrounds; whether and why that exclusion is justified is not addressed.
0) Velkommen til Heis
incomplete
The 'every company is a technology company' claim is delivered as a closer without supporting argument. It is a common premise in contemporary tech-leadership discourse but the episode does not engage with the well-known counter-readings (that 'tech company' loses meaning when applied universally; that technology intensity is a spectrum, not a binary).
0) Velkommen til Heis
incomplete
The elevator metaphor implies vertical movement between hierarchical levels, but the show's stated commitment is to a team-model world where hierarchical levels are deliberately flattened. There is a latent tension between 'visit leaders on every floor' (which implies floors exist as distinct strata) and 'leadership that plays on the team' (which implies the strata are less salient). The episode does not address this tension.
0) Velkommen til Heis
incomplete
The Jobs analogy is named but not fully cashed out against its disanalogies. Jobs's reality distortion field was famously paired with cruelty, factional purges and product taste applied to consumer aesthetics. Larsen's framing is the opposite — toppdivisjon-ledelse runs on indre motivasjon, not fear; authenticity / easy-to-read-ness is the asset; the product is a public-sector welfare system. The conversation does not name these disanalogies, so the analogy carries more rhetorical work than it earns.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Read Walter Isaacson's Jobs alongside Tracy Kidder's Soul of a New Machine and Marc Andreessen-tradition writing on charismatic founder leadership to surface the disanalogies; for the NAV case specifically, read Cutler on 'institutionalized overload' to see what indre-motivasjon-based mobilisation can hide. Connects to the gap in the Skjærpe ep 17 extract on transformations that fail in the liminal phase.
Intrinsic Motivation & MakingStrategy & Direction-SettingTaste & JudgmentTechnology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
incomplete
The NAV transformation is described entirely from the inside by one of its architects. There is no counterfactual: comparable Norwegian agencies that did not transform similarly, post-Larsen reversion at NAV, what failed within NAV and why. The 'hagiography risk' the hosts flagged is real and unexamined.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Compare Skatteetaten, Statens Pensjonskasse, Helsedirektoratet trajectories during the same period; read the NAV-flow article in the user's library (Optimizing for Fast Flow in NAV) for the inside-but-different-author view; read the Skjærpe ep 17 extract for the immediate-successor's view from inside the system. Look for what the NAV transformation displaced or made impossible (e.g. casualties among legacy fagsystemer staff).
incomplete
The 'common components are discovered, not designed' thesis is asserted with the NAV fakturering case as the load-bearing example, but the inverse failure mode (premature divergence — many verticals each solving the same problem badly because no one promoted the pattern to the platform) is acknowledged in passing ('we have capabilities in the platforms that strictly speaking are only needed one or two places ... it is a little behagelig that it got fixed over there') without being theorised. The position is one-sided.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Read the Wardley Mapping / pioneers-settlers-town-planners tradition for an explicit account of when components should be designed up front (the 'industrialised' part of the map). Read Cutler on 'horizontal team accountability' for how teams refuse to own platform-like problems they should own. The Vipps ep 18 extract may have a relevant view from the perspective of an organisation that did design infrastructure up front.
Platform EngineeringValue Streams & Verticals
incomplete
The 'easy to read' / authentic-leadership claim collides with the strategic-narrative claim. Strong strategic narrative is itself a construction; sustained reality-distortion presupposes some performance discipline. The conversation treats easy-to-read-ness and disciplined narrative as compatible without examining the tension. When does the constructed narrative cross into managed-impression territory, and what is the leader's check on that?
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Erving Goffman's Presentation of Self; Edgar Schein on the discrepancy between espoused theory and theory-in-use; Bent Flyvbjerg on narrative in megaprojects (where the same person both believes and constructs the future). The Cutler 'signals that look like decisions' thread is also relevant — for an easy-to-read leader, even contemplative musings can be read as commitments.
Strategy & Direction-Setting
incomplete
The inngangsbillett method is a personal description, not a teachable account. Larsen says he spends 'many hours a day' on craft and uses the hermeneutic-circle approach, but does not name which key terms, which structural maps, which sources, or how to know when the inngangsbillett is sufficient for a given fagdomene. The 'Fred George reads no books, he talks to authors' punchline gestures at one upper-bound technique but leaves the floor undefined.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Hubert Dreyfus's five-stage skill model (novice → expert) for a more precise grammar of 'how much do I need to know'; Cal Newport's deep work / deliberate practice tradition; the recent harness-engineering and AI-coding research extracted in this repo on what 'understanding a system' actually requires before AI can help you scale that understanding.
Attention as a Hijackable ResourceHands-On Leadership & the Pendulum
uninformed
The conversation invokes Peter Senge's learning organisation as one of the three common denominators of Larsen's narrativer but does not interrogate how durably the organism-organisation framing has held up under real conditions over the 30 years since Fifth Discipline. There is a tradition of critique (e.g. Mintzberg on the messiness of organisational learning; recent operations-research literature on actual learning loops in software organisations) that the conversation does not engage with.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Mintzberg's Rise and Fall of Strategic Planning; Argyris on double-loop learning vs Senge's framing; the more recent DORA / Accelerate research on what kinds of learning actually correlate with performance — and what kinds (e.g. 'just keep adapting') do not.
DORA & Engineering MetricsLearning Culture & Slack
incomplete
The episode ends on Larsen's most quotable problem — moving from project mindset to outcome-driven verdistrøm-mindset — but does not specify what makes this so hard at Telenor specifically. Is it inherited governance? Board reporting cadence? Career incentives for people who have always shipped to scope? The hosts move on. This is the single most actionable place the conversation could have gone deeper.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Cutler on outcome-orientation and operating-system design; Marty Cagan's 'product team vs feature team' distinction; the Una Aamodt-Mathisen ep 10 extract (lede uten fasit) likely has direct material on the same problem at a different scale. The Vidar Moe ep 5 ('det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett') may have a relevant view on operating-model simplification.
Leader RestraintValue Streams & Verticals
illogical
Larsen holds two positions that are in tension: (a) modern leadership happens from the middle, not the top, and (b) the leader must construct a strategic narrative that pulls the organisation toward a 3-4 year endgame. If leadership is from the middle, where exactly is the strategic narrative being constructed and projected from? The conversation treats these as compatible — the strategic leader rides the elevator down from a not-quite-top position — but the geometry is not specified.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: Henry Mintzberg's 'crafting strategy' for an account of emergent strategy from the middle; Roger L Martin's 'play to win' for a counter-position arguing strategy has to be top-set. Worth a syntopical pass against ep 15 (Kari Olrud Moen on strategi tydelig og skråsikker), which from its title suggests the opposite position.
AI Labor DisruptionIntrinsic Motivation & MakingStrategy & Direction-Setting
incomplete
The hagiography risk is structural: Truls worked for Larsen, Audun met him at a formative moment, both are visibly inspired. The format does not include any adversarial pressure. The episode is therefore best read as Larsen's self-presentation lightly facilitated by admirers — strong as primary source, weak as evaluation.
1) Torbjørn Larsen: Det nærmeste vi kommer Steve Jobs — read next: For an evaluation lens, read external accounts of NAV's transformation (audit reports, Riksrevisjonen, Aftenposten / NRK coverage of NAV IT projects during the Larsen era), and look for counter-narratives in the Norwegian public-sector tech community. Compare to Holte ep 2 (the senior public-sector counterpart) for a different view of the same period.
incomplete
The cost side of the information shower is not addressed. Every person who 'prepares and wants to land maximum points in their slot' is paying a cost: compressing their best case, choosing what to leave out, presumably also accepting that most of their points will be among those that 'rinse down the drain.' A senior-leader-centric view of information overload treats it as a load problem on the receiver; a system view would ask what happens to the people producing the information for the shower and what the aggregate organizational effect is when many serious efforts produce one-slot compressions that the receiver does not retain. Holte's metaphor invites this question and the conversation does not pick it up.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
incomplete
The criteria for which 'few central themes' Holte holds are not made explicit. He mentions specific filters: digital representation as a near-term must-solve at NAV offices; the NAV-office counsellor as a long-term focus; user experience as his current energise-zone. But how those particular bets emerged from the shower — what got filtered out, by what test — is not described. This is the operationally most interesting question and the most strategically sensitive; an answer might also be politically costly to give.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
unaddressed
The piece does not address what happens when Holte's filtering is wrong — when a theme that should have made it through the filter did not. The arbeidsavklaringspenger case is named as a failure of broad-enough thinking, but the role of his own information processing in that failure is not interrogated. A self-aware leader could productively unpack the failure mode of accepted forgetting: the information you accept losing may include exactly the information you should have kept.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
incomplete
Public-sector context shapes what Holte can say. As sitting NAV director he must be careful about politically loaded territory (the stat/kommune split at NAV offices, decisions about digital identity, the politics around the user-experience unit). The conversation rides these edges and signals them ('certain politicians are squinting at us') but cannot fully unpack them. This is a structural feature of the format, not a flaw — but it limits how much of Holte's actual filtering criteria can surface.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
incomplete
The 'T-ledelse' reframe of the hosts' heis-fører concept is elegant, but the question of where T-ledelse fits in a 22,000-person organisation with multiple levels — and whether T-ledelse from the very top can substitute for translation roles at the middle — is not resolved. The hosts' own concept (heis-fører at middle levels) and Holte's reframe (T-ledelse from the top) may not be alternatives at all; the conversation does not address whether both are needed.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
AI Labor DisruptionTechnology Leadership (Heis)
irrelevant
The episode does not address the dusjer-i-informasjon dynamic outside the senior-leader context — at lower organisational levels (where 'preparation by counterparties' looks very different), or in private sector (where Holte has no recent experience). The framing is specific to Holte's altitude in Norwegian public-sector leadership, and the conversation honours that specificity rather than over-generalising — which is appropriate for the form, though a syntopical reading across episodes might generalise it.
2) Hans Christian Holte dusjer i informasjon
incomplete
The episode does not address whose work is *invisible* without a title — those without platform, conference speaker credibility, financial cushion, or social safety to speak controversially. Gorman names her own preconditions (cheap living in Drammen, ability to return to retail work) but does not extend the analysis to those whose preconditions do not hold. Title-less leadership in her account is universally legitimated but materially restricted.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read against Cutler on institutional overload and 'who gets to think' / power and space (TBM 413); against work on visibility and credit allocation in tech (especially gendered patterns); and against critiques of 'thought leadership' as a privilege economy. The Bekk/Kodemaker consulting ecosystem also deserves examination as the platform infrastructure that makes Gorman's mode possible at all.
DORA & Engineering MetricsInformal Leadership
incomplete
When is the formal title load-bearing? Gorman gestures at the answer (taking unpopular fights, decisive 'I'm not paying for that' moves) but does not theorize when title-less voice strictly cannot substitute. The carpenter renovation analogy works because the homeowners had authority — Gorman in a team without it cannot replicate that pattern.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read Morten Eikeland (Heis ep 4) 'det finnes alltid et hierarki' as the explicit counter-claim. Also read Una Aamodt Mathisen (Heis ep 10) 'lede uten fasit' as a complementary refusal-of-answer-key mode. The cross-cutting question: which leadership functions can be unbundled from title and which cannot?
Leader Restraint
incomplete
The asymmetry of credit ('formal leaders take recognition when title-less leaders' ideas land') is named as a price Gorman is willing to pay, but not analyzed as a systemic problem. Who benefits from this asymmetry? It serves the formal leadership class — both the leaders who take credit, and the system that does not have to compensate the title-less.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read on credit assignment in collaborative work, on academic citation/recognition norms, on the labor economics of 'thought leadership' (Substack, conferences). Connects to Cutler on 'directly responsible individuals' from the recognition side.
incomplete
The framing of public-sector dysfunction as 'budget growth instead of value delivery' is a strong claim presented anecdotally. The implicit comparison to private sector ('we are on the same team') under-acknowledges that private-sector alignment is also engineered and partial — shareholder vs. employee interests, sales vs. engineering, short- vs. long-term.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read Nils Brede Moe (Heis ep 9) on the AI-paradox and learning culture; read public-sector reform literature (NAV's 'optimizing for fast flow' is on the user's list). Also read industrial-organization work on aligned vs. misaligned incentives across sectors.
Code Was Never the BottleneckLearning Culture & Slack
incomplete
Implicit premise about authority and self-correction ('I trust my own thinking only because I have to argue for it') is asserted as autobiographical but generalized. The argument that formal authority degrades judgment by removing resistance deserves more scrutiny — many formal leaders maintain rigorous self-criticism, and many title-less voices ossify.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read on epistemic humility and authority (Annie Duke on 'Thinking in Bets' framing of belief-updating; Greger Teigre Wedel (Heis ep 12) 'når sjefen har svaret' on the inverse problem). Also Beck on Tidy First / TDD as institutionalized self-correction independent of authority.
Decision-Making PracticesTrust
uninformed
The episode does not engage with how AI coding agents change the picture of who counts as a 'delivery-grounded' practitioner. Gorman's 'basis' criterion is rooted in writing code yourself — but what counts as that basis when much code is now generated and curated? The 'AI replaces ticket-converters' framing is correct but stops short of asking what the basis becomes.
3) Christin Gorman leder uten tittel — read next: Read Heis ep 11 (Vilde Opsal / Anders Sveen AI special) and ep 13 (Tobias Torrissen on code quality + AI); read the user's Kent Beck Tidy First and Claude Code material — what is the 'basis' equivalent when the medium shifts?
Claude CodeCode QualityCoding Agents
incomplete
The thesis 'det finnes alltid et hierarki' is rescued from triviality by Eikeland's broad definition (formell + uformell + kunnskaps- + framing-makt), but the same breadth makes the claim nearly unfalsifiable. Every conceivable group has some asymmetry of influence on some dimension; calling that 'hierarki' wins the argument by definition. The conversation does not engage the harder question of when the unit of analysis is wrong — when treating a temporary, context-dependent, weak asymmetry as a 'hierarchy' obscures more than it reveals.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Compare to network-and-influence literature (Cross/Parker on organisational networks, Vedres/Stark on structural folds) where the alternative unit is the network of weak ties rather than the layered hierarchy. Also Heath/Sunstein on consensus-driven decision-making to test where 'hierarchy' really is the operating frame.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The claim that flat-org rhetoric is selvbedrag is supported by Eikeland's clinical observation and the innvandrer vignette but not by counter-evidence from organisations that have made the rhetoric operationally real (or that have failed in distinctively non-Eikeland ways). The conversation does not engage Holacracy, Buurtzorg, sociocratic governance, or the long literature of self-managing teams. The result is a strong descriptive claim resting on a single vantage point.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Read Laloux Reinventing Organizations, Buurtzorg case studies, Robertson's Holacracy critically — both for examples of self-organisation that genuinely changes which hierarchy operates and for documented failures that Eikeland's frame would explain well. Pair with Lee/Edmondson's typology of self-managing organisations.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The role-vs-person distinction is presented as universal but it conflicts with a strong current of contemporary leadership literature (authentic leadership, bring-your-whole-self-to-work, vulnerability-based trust à la Lencioni/Brown) that holds that role-thick depersonalised leadership is itself the failure mode in knowledge work. The conversation does not engage these counter-positions; it treats them only implicitly via the Trump example, which is a one-sided foil.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Read Brené Brown on Dare to Lead, Goffee/Jones 'Why Should Anyone Be Led by You?', Lencioni on vulnerability-based trust against Eikeland's role-thick line. The most interesting test case is: are there role-domains where role-thickness is right (dommer, kirurg, politi) and other domains where it is wrong (product-team leadership, R&D leadership)?
Trust
incomplete
Eikeland's developer-to-leader sorgprosess prescription is rich on the loss side but thin on the gain side. The transition presumably brings new sources of meaning (impact through others, system-level effect, mentoring, organisational design), but the conversation barely names them. The risk for the listener is to internalise the loss-frame and miss the path through.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Camille Fournier's Manager's Path; Will Larson's Staff Engineer/An Elegant Puzzle on the IC-vs-manager fork; Lara Hogan on growing engineering leaders. Pair with Olga Khazan or Lori Gottlieb on the psychology of mid-career identity transitions.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The illusion-of-explanatory-depth intervention (ask for mekanismer not meninger) is presented as reliably productive but Eikeland admits it provokes forbannelse and ejection from rooms. The conversation does not address how to make the intervention land without burning the relationship — i.e., the practice question of how to descend into mekanisme safely. This matters because the intervention has weight only if it can be sustained inside an ongoing leader relationship.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Edgar Schein on humble inquiry; Marshall Goldsmith on stakeholder-centred coaching; Patterson et al. Crucial Conversations. The Heis-internal pairing is with Brede Moe (ep 9) on psykologisk trygghet as the precondition for being usable as a diagnostic outsider.
Psychological Safety
uninformed
The argument that teknologene have gained makt at the expense of 'litt enklere selgere' is named as a flagged trade-off but not analysed. The conversation does not engage the broader political-economy story of which professions rise and fall with each kunnskapshierarki shift, or the consequences for democratic and labour-market dynamics when one type of fag becomes dominant. Eikeland's clinical vantage is not designed for this question; the gap is honest but real.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Read Daniel Markovits The Meritocracy Trap, Michael Sandel The Tyranny of Merit on the consequences of credentialist hierarchies. For the Norwegian case specifically, NAV's own reports on labour-market polarisation.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
Eikeland insists hierarchies are inevitable but does not develop how to design good hierarchies versus bad ones. Hierarchy as a design object — its arity (span of control), its time-horizons per level, its dual lines (formal/uformell), its rotation, its accountability — receives no operational attention. The descriptive claim therefore stops short of a constructive prescription beyond 'operate at the right abstraksjonslag and recognise rolle vs person.'
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Elliott Jaques on requisite organisation and time horizons per level; Stafford Beer's Viable System Model; James Mwangi on adaptive governance. Pair with Printzell Halvorsen (ep 8) on the Vend operating model as a worked example of an explicit, designed hierarchy that honours these structural pressures.
Hierarchy & Org ChartsInformal LeadershipOrchestration Tax / Human Review BottleneckOrganizational Abstraction Levels
incomplete
The claim that ledelse-as-coordination kicks in around eight people is asserted as 'avhenger litt av hvor mye interaksjon du trenger' but not defended against the broader social-science literature on group size, span of control, and self-organisation thresholds. The number is plausible but the conversation does not engage why eight rather than five or twelve, what happens in distributed/asynchronous groups, or how the threshold shifts with technology.
4) Morten Eikeland: Det finnes alltid et hierarki — read next: Robin Dunbar's hierarchies of group size (5, 15, 50, 150); Christopher Allen's optimal group sizes; Tuckman's small-group dynamics. For the distributed case, GitLab's all-remote handbook on team sizing and the post-COVID literature on async coordination.
Orchestration Tax / Human Review Bottleneck
incompleteclosed
The AI / agentic-coding chapter is conspicuously absent from this 2025-recorded episode despite being the explicit subject of Moe's near-contemporaneous LinkedIn essay 'KI, utvikling og følelser'. The Heis conversation is recorded in late 2025 (published 17 November 2025, the same week Moe's essay marks as the Opus 4.5 inflection point at SpareBank 1 Utvikling) yet the agent question is never raised — the closest touch is Moe's observation that change is 'ordentlig seig' even at the pair-programming level. This is a productive gap: the Heis episode is the unstated philosophical foundation that the essay's alienation diagnosis presupposes. If software development is structured learning and pair programming is the most effective form of knowledge sharing, then 'specifying systems' through an agent is alienating precisely because it displaces the learning act and breaks the pairing dyad.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Read the Heis episode and the 'KI, utvikling og følelser' essay together as one document. The essay's three mitigations (kode fundamentet selv; eksplisitt TDD med agenten; problemløsing fremfor programmering) are direct instances of 'gjøre det lett og rett' applied to the agent layer. Then read Beck's recent 'starving genies' / TCR work and Cutler's TBM 412 on institutionalised overload + AI as the anglophone analogues. Steve Yegge's vibe-coding manifesto is the live contrast position.
✓ closed by9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — Supplies the missing AI chapter for Moe's 'software development is structured learning' framework: AI threatens the learning act by displacing colleague interaction, and the mitigation is making AI use a collective, reflective team practice under tight supervision.
Emotional Impact of AI WorkThe Genie (Beck)Golden PathPair & Mob Programming
incomplete
The episode systematically tells the success-side of the story. Crisis → response → improvement is the recurring arc, but failed experiments, dead-end framework choices, or instances where 'gjøre det lett og rett' produced unintended organisational uniformity are absent. Moe's stated regret is only directional (could have done more, faster, in more places), not structural (e.g. 'this particular default we engineered was wrong'). The hosts twice surface the hierarchy-is-inevitable point but do not press on whether the framework-team's 'easy and right' becomes a hidden orthodoxy.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Pair with Allen Holub's #NoAccountability strand (already in the user's persona) and with anything on the costs of platform-engineering paved roads (e.g. Charity Majors / Honeycomb on the limits of golden paths). The user's existing extraction on Cutler's institutionalised overload is a strong complement here — Cutler's frame would ask what the second-order costs of the SpareBank 1 system look like.
Golden PathHierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
Moe's persistent 'vi' is itself a load-bearing rhetorical pattern that the hosts notice in closing but do not unpack. The episode is a portrait of a person who declines to be individuated — he says 'vi' even when narrating decisions whose intellectual origin clearly lies with him personally. This is consistent with the leadership-through-defaults thesis (the work is the structure, not the speaker) but it also obscures the part of the story that does depend on a specific senior practitioner persistently present in the system for 14 years. The question 'how transferrable is this without Moe (and Stian)?' is not engaged.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Read in tension with the previous Heis episode on hierarchy (Eikeland, ep 4, alluded to: 'forrige podcast-episoden snakket om... det er sjelden helt flat struktur') and with the leadership-presence strand in the user's library (Cutler on power and space, TBM 413). Beck's writing on senior individuals' role in evolutionary design is a direct anglophone parallel.
DORA & Engineering MetricsHierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The 'organisatorisk gjeld' coinage at the close is the most pregnant unfinished idea in the episode. Moe gestures at the next direction of 'lett og rett' — engineering golden paths for product development itself, with good practices baked into roadmap tooling — but does not work it out. This is exactly the territory where the AI question reconnects: an agentic product-development assistant that encoded the right defaults could be the next instance of 'lett og rett', and would be a Moe-shaped answer to the alienation worry from his essay.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Pair with the user's existing extractions on harness engineering for AI coding agents and on 'Agents Don't Know What Good Looks Like'. The Backstage / Internal Developer Platforms tradition (Spotify) is the obvious anglophone analogue. Team Topologies (Skelton & Pais) and DORA's recent platform engineering findings are the natural canon. Truls's closing observation that Toyota → DORA → Team Topologies is one lineage is exactly the bridge.
Agent HarnessCoding AgentsDORA & Engineering MetricsGolden Path
incomplete
Pair programming is named as the most effective form of knowledge sharing and as the substrate that the rest of the infrastructure exists to support, but the cost question (developer preference, time investment, neurodiversity, hybrid/remote context) is not engaged. Moe acknowledges that change in this direction is 'ordentlig seig' — really tough/slow — but does not ask why even at SpareBank 1, with all the structure in place, pair programming is not yet universal. The same gap exists in his essay's TDD-with-the-agent prescription.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Read Birgitta Böckeler's recent work on remote pair programming, and the user's already-extracted Beck material on TCR (which functions as a half-step toward pair-discipline). The 'AI Joy Gap Developers' extraction the user already has — which treats reconnection-of-developers as needing organisational structure — is a direct complement.
Developer Experience & FlowPair & Mob Programming
incomplete
The Norwegian / Lean-Norwegian context is implicit but not theorised. The åremål concept comes from Norwegian political and academic life and is being repurposed for engineering leadership. The fagtid concept has roots in Norwegian work-culture autonomy ideas. The DORA / Lean / Toyota convergence Truls names at the end is itself partially a Nordic management-culture convergence. There is a comparative essay latent here that the episode does not write.
5) Vidar Moe: Det eneste Vidar vil er å ha det lett — read next: Read in parallel with NAV's 'optimizing for fast flow' piece (already in the user's queue), with Norwegian Self-Determination Theory–inflected management writing (autonomi, mestring, mening), and with the wider tradition of Nordic engineering culture vs. anglophone agile-at-scale. The user's coding-agents corpus already includes Nils Brede Moe (no relation? — but a Nordic empirical-software-engineering voice in the same neighbourhood, including the upcoming Heis episode 9).
Craft CommunityDORA & Engineering MetricsLean & Toyota WayWriting as Thinking
incomplete
Haugseth's account is built on insourcing successes (Mattilsynet, NAV). There is no narrative of an insourcing that failed or backfired — no analysis of when insourcing produced worse outcomes than the status quo. The closest is the NAIS-reflection (where she would have stayed less ambitious about own platform), but that is presented as 'should have piggybacked,' not 'insourcing failed.' The asymmetry shapes what is visible: insourcing's hazards (talent risk, two-year ramp where the org loses access to specialists, capability concentration in a few key hires who then leave) are absent from the framing.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Susanne Justesen / John Cutler on the cost of capability concentration; the Norwegian NAV 'Optimizing for Fast Flow' article (already in the user's library) for a parallel account that emphasises the work-system rather than the ownership question; Capacity Planning literature on staffing as a long-cycle bet; failure-case writing on insourced platform teams that lost their flywheel (Stripe / Etsy public postmortems are useful counterpoints).
Insourcing & In-House CapabilityPlatform Engineering
incomplete
The business-case for insourcing is asserted but not specified. 'Either konsulentene get ny oppdrag andre steder, OR rådgiver-hverdagen improves enough to boost inntekt' is a frame, not an analysis. What kind of measurement? Over what horizon? Who validates the case 24 months in? The hard version of the case — that some insourcing pays for itself only in scenarios that don't materialise — is not engaged.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Roger Martin on the strategy-stack from where-to-play to how-to-win; cost-of-delay literature; Dan North on staffing-as-bet.
Insourcing & In-House Capability
incompleteclosed
Oppdragsbasert ledelse is presented as obvious, with the Nordic-vs-Soviet military analogy doing most of the rhetorical work. But the conditions under which mission command works (shared training base, mutual trust, common doctrine) are exactly the conditions large public-sector and bank organisations often lack. The transcript skips over how an organisation acquires those preconditions — i.e., the part that is hard.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Stephen Bungay 'The Art of Action' (the foundational text for Auftragstaktik in management); David Snowden on what context-shaping does that direct order cannot; Vidar Moe's account (Heis ep 5) for an adjacent leadership-style discussion.
✓ closed byPresentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code — Addresses how an organization acquires the preconditions mission command assumes: trust must be deliberately engineered via relationship-building before task work, repeated core messages, leader-modeled vulnerability, and rituals as persistent social infrastructure.
Team AutonomyTrust
incomplete
The ølbrikke method is presented as a virtuous compression artifact, but the failure mode — that radical compression elides important trade-offs and lets every reader project their preferred reading onto the three hovedområder — is not addressed. Olrud Moen (ep 15) discusses the strategy-as-PowerPoint failure mode explicitly; Haugseth's ølbrikke could fall into a sibling failure mode (the strategy-as-slogan trap).
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Olrud Moen Heis ep 15 (recently extracted) — direct counterpoint on strategy compression; John Cutler's TBM on 'making the trade-off visible'; Roger Martin on integrative thinking; Donald Sull on 'simple rules.'
incomplete
The sponsor-group mechanism is described in the abstract; what happens when the two sponsors disagree, or when a sponsor leaves konsernledelsen mid-oppdrag, or when sponsor priorities conflict with what the team's konsernledelse-wide one-pager actually says, is not analysed.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Marty Cagan / SVPG on product strategy governance; Cutler on accountability culture and DRIs (directly responsible individuals).
incomplete
Haugseth's epistemic update on skye is offered as an instance of changing her mind, but the underlying mental model that drove her earlier full-pro-cloud stance is not interrogated. What other current convictions might be in the same epistemic position now? The 'how does a leader earn forankring on the world' question (same as the Olrud Moen ep 15 gap) is unaddressed.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Annie Duke 'Thinking in Bets' (already in user's library) on calibration and the cost of being skråsikker about probabilistic claims; Philip Tetlock on superforecasting; Donella Meadows on systemic leverage points.
Decision-Making PracticesMental Models & UnderstandingStrategy & Direction-Setting
incomplete
Power and political economy are visible at the edges (the consultant houses' motstand to Mattilsynet cancellations; the 'kjipe' work of handling underperformance) but never named as power. The political cost paid by the consultant houses, by the developers whose subkulturer were dismantled, and by the avdelingsledere who had to take on accountability for cross-team samhandling is not analysed.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Cutler 'Power and Space' (TBM 413, already in user's library); Pfeffer 'Power' on organisational politics; Hamel/Birkinshaw on managerial innovation and its political cost.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The 'privilegium ikke identitet' frame is named but not connected back to the operating-model questions earlier in the conversation. A leader who treats the role as borrowed time presumably should design the operating model so it survives their departure — i.e., the ølbrikke and the sponsor-group should ideally outlast the leader. That implication is not drawn.
7) Stine Haugseth: Insourcing, retning og strategi på en ølbrikke — read next: Cutler on operating systems as artefacts that survive their author; Olrud Moen ep 15 on 'strategy as the few sharp trade-offs that nobody else has the authority to set' — and the corollary question of what the leader leaves behind.
incomplete
The case for 'lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet' rests heavily on the three favourable conditions (small cultural distance, similar maturity, no hub story) that Printzell Halvorsen names. The conversation does not seriously interrogate what happens in cases where one or more of those conditions does not hold — e.g. a Nordic group with one dominant and three start-up markets, or a public-sector federation where national differences (legal, fiscal) are large. The model may be narrower than its rhetorical appeal suggests.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Compare to Nordic banking (Nordea), Nordic telecom (Telia/Telenor), and Nordic public-sector federations to see where the conditions hold and where they don't. Read Geert Hofstede / Erin Meyer on the actual measured cultural distances within the Nordics.
incomplete
Privileged-Schibsted-vantage shapes what's visible vs. invisible. The lessons are drawn from a Schibsted-internal lineage of marketplaces with strong brands, a developer-heavy talent pool, and a long maturation as commercial businesses. The conversation is silent on: public-sector contexts, non-media startups, B2B enterprise software, government-mandated platform consolidations, and platforms where brand-in-consumers'-heads is not the load-bearing asset. The framework is presented as broadly applicable without owning these scope conditions.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read public-sector platform consolidation case studies (NAV, Skatteetaten, NHS Digital, Estonian e-government); read B2B platform consolidation studies (Salesforce-MuleSoft, Adobe-Figma). The presence/absence of the Vend pattern in those contexts would map the model's actual reach.
incomplete
The methodological claim that big-bang beats phased for integration projects with a known target state is grounded in three Vend cases and asserted as a general lesson. The conversation does not consider failure cases (industry has many) or the boundary conditions (rehearsal infrastructure, rollback capability, cohesion of the cut team). Listeners may over-apply.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read post-mortems on famous failed big-bang migrations (UK NHS NPfIT, TSB Bank 2018, Hertz/Accenture) to identify what made Vend's big-bangs work that those did not have.
incompleteclosed
The simple 'how confident are you?' status mechanism is presented as a powerful tool but with one premise — psychological safety — given a single sentence. Building that safety at the scale of 100 teams across four countries is non-trivial and the conversation does not explore how Vend actually did it (or whether it was already present from inheritance).
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Brede Moe ep 9 (already extracted) directly addresses the operational construction of psychological safety in teams; pairing his prescriptions with Printzell Halvorsen's claim that the self-reporting honesty held would test whether Vend's safety conditions are explicit or implicit. Also Edmondson on hybrid/distributed safety.
✓ closed by9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — Operationalizes the psychological-safety premise behind Vend's self-reported status mechanism: safety is collective, requires deliberate investment in shared practice, and is built through structured learning rituals and peer-agreed working agreements.
Psychological Safety
incomplete
Bob the Builder is presented as low-code AI tooling that produces useful prototypes and personal-productivity solutions but is firewalled from product production code. The conversation does not address how outputs ever cross that firewall — what happens when a marketing team's Bob the Builder solution becomes load-bearing and needs the engineering quality bar.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read low-code/citizen-developer governance literature (Gartner's 'fusion teams,' Microsoft Power Platform CoE patterns) for how organisations handle the citizen-to-production handoff.
Non-Engineers Building
incomplete
Printzell Halvorsen names the senior/junior asymmetry of technology fluency implicitly (his own developer past as anchor; CFO going through Bob the Builder to stay relevant) but never engages the question of how Vend develops the next generation of leaders without a developer past. The transferability of the abstraksjonslag instinct to non-technical leaders is left open.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read Beane on apprenticeship in mixed-expertise contexts; read deliberate-practice literature on developing abstraction skill without a code substrate.
Non-Engineers BuildingOrganizational Abstraction Levels
incomplete
The destination-vs.-chat scenario framing is treated as binary. The conversation does not engage the third possibility — that marketplaces become specialised APIs feeding many fronts (chat, AR shopping, voice assistants), with the consumer-facing brand decoupled from the consumer-facing surface. That would require a different bet on data infrastructure and brand strategy.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read literature on platform economics under generative AI distribution (Anu Hariharan, a16z's recent marketplaces writing); Matthew Prince's own writing at Cloudflare on the AI-content-economy collapse and 'pay-per-crawl' proposals (which Printzell Halvorsen alludes to when citing him).
uninformed
The conversation does not engage with the consumer-welfare or competition-policy implications of Nordic consolidation. Consolidating into Vend reduces marketplace competition within each vertical at a Nordic level. The hosts do not raise this; Printzell Halvorsen does not address it.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read EU competition decisions on Adevinta/eBay Classifieds, Schibsted's spin-off rationale, and Nordic competition authorities' marketplace cases for the regulatory frame the conversation omits.
incomplete
The autobiographical-prescriptive proposition ('do a good job where you are; opportunities follow; developer → product → business is the natural path') is presented without engaging the question of how much of Printzell Halvorsen's trajectory is reproducible vs. how much depends on McKinsey-as-accelerator, Norway's small leadership pool, and the specific historical moment of FINN. The advice may be more biographically contingent than it sounds.
8) Christian Printzell Halvorsen om å lede på riktig nivå: Lokalt forankret, nordisk samlet — read next: Read against the Cutler thread on what conditions enable senior individual-contributor-to-leader transitions inside specific organisations, and against literature on path-dependence in executive careers.
incomplete
The MIT '95% of AI projects show no return' figure is asserted as foundational but never sourced, methodologically interrogated, or compared to base rates for non-AI IT projects (where failure rates are also famously high). The framing relies heavily on this number.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Pursue the MIT study directly (likely the MIT NANDA / Sloan AI 'GenAI Divide' report from 2025) and compare against Standish CHAOS-style baselines for IT project failure to establish whether AI is failing more than other IT initiatives, or merely the same.
incomplete
The framing is almost entirely about knowledge-work software teams in professional services / large org settings. The argument is silent on: solo developers, small startups without team infrastructure, contracting/outsourcing relationships, non-developer roles where AI is also disrupting work, and contexts where 'team' is not the unit of production.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Read Tabarrok / Acemoglu on AI and labor markets in non-team-shaped work; and Beane's Skill Code on apprenticeship in mixed expert/AI environments outside software, to test how portable Brede Moe's prescriptions are.
incomplete
The senior/junior asymmetry is identified but the prescription for what to do about it stops at 'build safety so juniors can admit confusion.' There is no concrete model of how junior skill formation actually works in an AI-augmented team — what scaffolding, what kinds of exercises, what the new apprenticeship looks like.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Beane's Precision Proactivity (extracted this session) directly addresses this — proactive skill formation under AI assistance. Brede Moe's empirical team lens and Beane's apprenticeship lens together would be more complete than either alone.
Cognitive Load
incompleteclosed
Brede Moe's account of læringskultur emphasizes practices internal to a team (reflection, show-and-tell, Slack channels). It is silent on inter-team learning at scale — how lessons from one team's AI experimentation propagate across an organization of dozens or hundreds of teams. This is precisely where the MIT 95% gap might be most operationally relevant.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Cutler on institutional overload and operating systems; SINTEF's own work on large-scale agile (Brede Moe has co-authored on this); Lean enterprise / Forsgren-Humble-Kim on horizontal practice diffusion.
✓ closed by11) AI-spesial med Vilde Opsal og Anders Sveen — Describes a working organization-scale propagation pattern for AI learning across teams: surface an ordinary team that succeeded, run fast and safe Slack channels, workshops, internal blog summaries, and a no-opt-out learning culture.
Lean & Toyota WayLearning Culture & Slack
incomplete
The conversation treats AI tooling as relatively homogeneous — Copilot, ChatGPT, Cursor, Claude Code are mentioned as a cluster. But the team-level practices likely differ between chat-based assistants, autonomous agents, and in-IDE pair tools. Brede Moe gestures at this with 'augmented teams' but doesn't yet differentiate.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Empirical studies of differential effects by tool class — DORA's 2025 AI findings, Microsoft / GitHub research on Copilot effects, ongoing studies of agentic coding tools.

incomplete
The car-factory study (cited as 15-20 years old) is the central empirical anchor for the slow-down-to-speed-up claim, but it is cited without source — the listener cannot evaluate the comparison. Similarly the 9-of-10 figure.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Source the original study; even if accurate, ask whether software development resembles auto-manufacturing learning curves enough for the lesson to transfer.
incomplete
The argument assumes that team autonomy and team learning are the right units of analysis. It does not engage with alternative views — e.g. that AI might shift the unit back to the individual contractor or fragment work into smaller, more decomposable tasks, in which case the team-level prescription is the wrong response to the wrong question.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Read agentic-work literature (Eric Ries, Naval-style 'agency leverage' arguments, Yegge's vibe-coding manifesto) for the opposing view.

incompleteclosed
Psychological safety is treated as foundational and necessary, but the conversation does not address how to actually build it in distributed, hybrid, and AI-augmented configurations — only that it is harder there. This is the most-cited construct in the conversation and the least operationalized.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Edmondson's recent work on hybrid safety; Brede Moe's own SINTEF publications on distributed-team trust formation.
✓ closed byPresentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code — Supplies concrete mechanisms for building the safety Brede Moe treats as foundational but unoperationalized: relationship-first team formation, leaders modeling vulnerability, rituals that persist across changing team composition, and direct handling of fear.
Psychological SafetyTrust
uninformedclosed
The hosts and Brede Moe do not engage with the strong critique that AI agents are not yet reliable enough to be modeled as 'team members' in the augmented-team sense — they neither hold state, nor carry context across sessions in the way a colleague does, nor have stakes. The augmented-teams framing risks anthropomorphizing.
9) Nils Brede Moe om AI-paradokset, autonomi, læringskultur og fremtidens team — read next: Read critics like Gary Marcus, Emily Bender, and Murray Shanahan on the gap between LLM behavior and team-member competence.
✓ closed byResearch: Why You Shouldn't Treat AI Agents Like Employees — Engages the anthropomorphizing critique the podcast skipped, with experimental evidence: framing AI as a team member/employee measurably reduces human accountability and error-catching, supporting the case against modeling agents as colleagues (p1-p3, p7).
Agentic AI
incomplete
The conversation explicitly opens but does not close the team-augmentation question — what does multi-agent + multi-human team coordination look like in practice? Vilde admits FINN/Vend has not cracked it.

incompleteclosed
Evals come up as the durable skill, but the conversation never defines *what an eval actually looks like* for code-agent output (vs. business-agent product output). The asymmetry between 'evaluating an LLM in prod' and 'evaluating an LLM-generated diff' is not surfaced.
11) AI-spesial med Vilde Opsal og Anders Sveen — read next: Anthropic and Hamel Husain writing on LLM evals; Tobias Torrissen on kodekvalitet + AI (Heis ep 13).
✓ closed byDoorDash Used Copilot to Convert Its XCTest-Based iOS Test Suite to Swift Testing — Gives a concrete worked example of what an eval for code-agent output looks like: a deterministic ten-consecutive-pass reliability gate applied to each AI-migrated test before acceptance.
Evals
incompleteclosed
Both guests work with code agents that have full machine privileges but the autonomy-level framework (Droid) is mentioned only as a stopgap. There is no working theory of what the *next* sandbox abstraction should look like.
11) AI-spesial med Vilde Opsal og Anders Sveen — read next: Anthropic's harness/agent SDK posts; container-level sandboxing approaches; Cloudflare Mythos / OS-level isolation for agents.
✓ closed byRunning Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — Willison offers a concrete next sandbox abstraction beyond staged-autonomy stopgaps: a WASM/MicroPython execution boundary as the unit of safety for agent-generated code, with blast-radius containment rather than trust in model intentions.
Agentic AIMythosSandboxing
uninformed (in a time-stamped sense)
The episode is explicitly dated end of January 2026 and pre-DeepSick announcement; the participants flag this themselves.
11) AI-spesial med Vilde Opsal og Anders Sveen — read next: Re-listen after major model releases; compare against follow-up Heis special when produced.
incomplete
Cultural specificity is unexamined: Wedel never tests whether 'when the boss has the answer, the rest stay silent' depends on Norwegian (or Nordic, or hierarchical) defaults. In flatter or more confrontational cultures, the chilling effect may be weaker — or it may show up differently (silent disagreement vs. silent compliance).
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: Cutler TBM 423 on undiscussables (which treats power-asymmetry-creating-silence as general, not culturally specific) and Edmondson on psychological safety across cultures. Also Hofstede / Trompenaars on power distance dimensions in Nordic vs. other workplaces.
Chilling EffectsPsychological Safety
incomplete
Decision criteria for posture-switching are underspecified. Wedel says leaders should choose among coaching, deciding, hands-on, whiteboarding, director postures situationally — but he names only one explicit trigger (the over-coaching failure mode that calls for cutting through). When to take which posture is left as 'judgment'.
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: Kent Beck on tidy-first vs. mess-first decision rules; Heifetz on adaptive vs. technical work; Cutler's TBM 418 on campfires/trails/quests as posture-selection frame.
Multiplayer vs Single-Player AI
incomplete
The interaction between explicit mode-switching ('lederhatten av') and team-level psychological safety is asserted but not unpacked. Saying 'we're in whiteboarding mode now' presumably only works if the team trusts that the leader will actually honour the signal — which is itself a function of trust built elsewhere.
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: Edmondson on psychological safety as accumulated track-record; Schein on rituals and their preconditions; Pocock's 'alignment is not AFK' point about why alignment work must be live, not formal.
