incomplete
The junior-development problem is named but not theorised. Wedel says we don't know how to grow juniors when AI flattens the early production curve. He gestures at 'fundamentals matter more' but doesn't say how organisations should change apprenticeship.
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: Recent writing on AI and the apprenticeship pipeline (Pragmatic Engineer FDE piece, Addy Osmani on orchestration tax, Garg on reducing friction in AI development).

incomplete
AI-as-amplifier is asserted in both directions (good practice → better; bad practice → worse) but the magnitude of asymmetry is not addressed. If AI amplifies bad practice faster than good, the policy implication ('defer AI until guardrails exist') may be stronger than Wedel states.
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: DORA 2025 detailed findings; the Dave Farley YouTube series Wedel mentions; any empirical work on AI adoption velocity vs. defect rates.

incomplete
The six product-org first principles are alluded to but not enumerated in the conversation. Only the first (deep user/customer connection) is discussed.
12) Greger Teigre Wedel: Når sjefen har svaret, tier resten — read next: stuff.greger.io blog series on product organisation first principles.
incomplete
Torrissen's frame is largely Norwegian consultancy and public-sector. The 30-year-lifespan argument is anchored in NAV/Skatt/politi; he gestures at differential quality bars for low-stakes consumer apps but does not engage with product-company contexts (startup, scaleup, SaaS) where the relevant time horizon is 3-5 years and the relevant readers are 5-10 engineers, not 70 forvaltere.
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Read this against the Pocock workshop extraction (product-company AI workflow) and Vidar Moe (Heis), to see how product-company engineers calibrate quality differently.

Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Vlad Khononov's 'Balancing Coupling' (cited by Torrissen); Audun's reference to KOS as evidence; James Lewis / Sam Newman on microservices boundaries; possibly recent Fowler/Bockeler 'static sensors' work that already sits in the corpus.
incomplete
The 'engineering practices matter more under AI' claim rests on one cited Telenor talk. Strong intuition, weak evidence. A serious version needs DORA-style measurement of practice maturity × AI uplift.

incompleteclosed
Three-camp taxonomy of AI-coders is clean but binary on camp 3 (vibecoders) — conflates throwaway-app-spammers with large-scale parallel-agentic professional work. These have opposite stakes and opposite engineering risks.
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Gene Kim's vibecoding series; Steve Yegge's manifesto; Anthropic harness-design talk (already in extractions); 'No Vibes Allowed' counter-pieces.
✓ closed byBliki: Vibe Coding — Provides the distinction Torrissen's camp-3 taxonomy conflates: vibe coding (never looking at the code, appropriate only for disposable software) is categorically different from professional agentic programming (p1, p2, p5).
Agent HarnessVibe Coding
incomplete
The 'obvious over clever' principle is presented as inheritance from NAV's tekniske retning but the tradeoff against domain-modelling power, type-system expressiveness, and idiomatic language use is not explored. A 'maximally obvious' codebase in a functional language with strong types may look obscure to a Java-trained 70-forvalter pool.
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Greg Young / Vlad Khononov on domain modelling; Ousterhout's 'A Philosophy of Software Design'; Rich Hickey's 'Simple Made Easy' (vs. 'easy ≠ obvious').
incomplete
Torrissen acknowledges he's about to participate in a recursive case (reviewing code that was itself generated agentically) and only jokes. The methodology gap — how does code review change when the developer's intent is partially encoded in prompts, ADRs, and context files rather than commit history — is left for next time.
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Anthropic 'harness design' talk and the Pocock workshop already in this corpus.

incomplete
The advice to juniors assumes the fundamentals taught today are the right ones; doesn't engage with the possibility that fundamentals themselves need re-curation for a world where LLMs are the second author. What does TDD even mean when test generation is also automated?
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Kent Beck's recent 'Tidy First?' work on AI and design; 'starving genies' series; DORA on AI testing practices.

uninformed
Torrissen disclaims familiarity with the formal 'distribution law' he invokes (Parnas/Helland-style). He gets the spirit right but doesn't engage with the literature on when distribution is forced (latency, regulatory, fault isolation).
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Pat Helland's papers; Parnas on module boundaries; Vlad Khononov directly.
incomplete
Code quality discussion is silent on data and persistence design — arguably the longest-lived and hardest-to-change layer in 30-year systems. The whole conversation is about behavioural code.
13) Tobias K. Torrissen om kodekvalitet, konferanser og AI — read next: Stripe-style schema evolution practice; Martin Kleppmann's data engineering work; event-sourcing reflections from CQRS practitioners.
Code Quality
incomplete
Scale dependence of pendling. The whole career story plays out inside two large Norwegian financial-services organisations (Storebrand, Fremtind) that recognise technical depth as legitimate seniority. The conversation never tests whether pendling is feasible — or how it changes shape — in small startups (no role to come back to), pure consultancies (the IC vs. leader frame may not apply), public-sector hierarchies (rigid grade structures), or US-style stock-comp tracks where stepping back is also stepping out financially.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Pair with Charity Majors' 'Engineer-Manager Pendulum' original essay to see her conditions for it working; pair with Will Larson on staff-engineer archetypes for the IC-track question; pair with Norwegian-public-sector accounts (NAV optimising for fast flow) for the bureaucracy edge case.
Hands-On Leadership & the Pendulum
incomplete
The people who can't pendle. Ulseth's account is sympathetic to both directions but assumes the listener has the option of swinging both ways. It does not address those who are only-leaders (lost technical currency) or only-deliverers (no taste for organisational complexity) — and whether they should be coached, paired, or left in their lane. There is a real possibility that pendling is a personality privilege as much as an organisational one.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Camille Fournier on technical-leadership transitions; Cutler on the costs of forcing technical contributors into managerial roles; Allen Holub on accountability and the limits of role-fluidity.
Hands-On Leadership & the PendulumTaste & Judgment
incomplete
The architect's function is never defined. The talk asserts that architecture has not changed in its core function, only in practice — but it does not say what the function is. The closest thing to a definition is 'enabling speed and flow via loose coupling and high cohesion', which is more a goal than a function and which collapses architecture into platform engineering in some readings.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Vlad Khononov on coupling in distributed systems; Ruth Malan on architecture as 'the design decisions that are hardest to change'; Team Topologies for the sociotechnical framing Ulseth invokes.
Modularity & CouplingPlatform Engineering
incomplete
Bredde/dybde sounds clean but is operationally underspecified. How does a sjefsarkitekt choose which initiatives to take as 'mine'? Ulseth nods that they should be 'both important and interesting' and that the two usually overlap — but the harder cases (important-but-boring, interesting-but-marginal) are not discussed. This is the place where pendling-by-design either works or quietly collapses into one side.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Cutler on how leaders allocate attention; deep-dive into 'staff-plus' patterns where senior ICs balance technical and political work.
incomplete
The claim that AI raises the incentive to live good engineering culture is asserted but not mechanised. Continuous delivery has been well-described for 20 years and only patchily adopted. Why will AI succeed in changing this where the prior decade's tooling, DORA reports, and platform engineering did not? A causal story is missing.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: DORA 2025/2026 specifically on AI's interaction with delivery practices; Birgitta Böckeler and Martin Fowler on AI-coding feedback loops; Kent Beck's TCR work as the practice end of the same idea.
DORA & Engineering MetricsFeedback LoopsPlatform EngineeringTrunk-Based & Continuous Delivery
incomplete
No data on whether pendling actually produces better outcomes. The argument is largely 'I felt better and have heard I do better.' The episode lacks any reference to organisational research on whether engineer-manager pendulum careers correlate with better team or product outcomes — and whether the benefits go to the individual, the team, or the organisation.
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Pragmatic Engineer / Newcomer on staff-plus role outcomes; LeadDev surveys on engineer-manager transitions; academic work on bidirectional career mobility in knowledge work.
Hands-On Leadership & the Pendulum
uninformed
Ulseth notes he has not yet seen conversational architecture practiced and that sociotechnical architecture is a new acquaintance — yet these are the frameworks he is now bringing into Fremtind's IT strategy. The talk does not engage seriously with the published critiques of these models (e.g. that Team Topologies under-specifies platform-team funding, or that Harmel-Law's model assumes a maturity teams may not have).
14) Øystein Ulseth pendler mellom å lede og levere — read next: Andrew Harmel-Law's own book and the Architecture Decision Records literature; critical reviews of Team Topologies (Manuel Pais et al.).

incomplete
The criterion that distinguishes strategic deselection from defensive or pathological deselection is named (the climate example) but not developed. When the urgent legitimately overrides the important and when it doesn't is left unanalyzed.
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: John Cutler on institutional overload (TBM 412) and the cost of context collapse; Hamilton Helmer's 7 Powers for a more analytical theory of when narrowing is value-creating vs. value-destroying; Roger Martin's 'Playing to Win' for the 'winning aspiration → where to play → how to win' chain that forces the warrant question explicitly.
incomplete
The people on the wrong side of deselection ('det er noen folk bak') are acknowledged but their experience and what leaders owe them is not analyzed. The 'kjipe oppgavene' frame keeps it at the level of leader-discomfort rather than treating the human cost as its own analytical object.
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Annie Duke's 'Quit' for the ethics and cognition of stopping; Cutler on accountability culture vs. blame; literature on layoffs/restructuring as legitimacy work.
incomplete
The leader's epistemic warrant — what makes their read of 'how the world is moving' credible — is treated as given, not interrogated. The interview repeatedly invokes 'forankring' for the operation but does not ask: how does a leader earn (and update) forankring on the world?
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Annie Duke's 'Thinking in Bets' on calibration and the cost of being skråsikker about probabilistic claims; Philip Tetlock's 'Superforecasting' on the discipline of holding views with degrees of confidence; Donella Meadows' 'Thinking in Systems' on how leverage points are usually mis-identified.

incomplete
The HOW-sharpening proposal (be sharp on tempo vs. error-tolerance, not on implementation) is offered as a resolution but not tested against the failure modes of values-as-strategy (where 'speed over perfection' becomes a slogan everyone reinterprets locally).
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Cutler on operating systems and 'making the trade-off visible'; DORA/Accelerate on what concrete operating principles actually correlate with outcomes; Beck's Tidy First? framing for how small-scale design decisions encode strategic posture.

incomplete
The relationship between 'kompani-bets' and the legitimacy economy of an autonomous team-of-teams organization is sketched but not modeled. How many bets per year? Who funds them? How does the organization recover from a bet that fails publicly?
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Spotify's own writings on the post-bets era and what replaced it; Marty Cagan / SVPG on product strategy at scale; Cutler on directly-responsible-individuals and accountability when top-down moves intrude.
incomplete
Public-sector strategy is dismissed as 'mer taktikk' but the conversation does not return to it. Whether public-sector leaders can do something like deselection (and what its political constraints look like) is a productive missing thread for a podcast whose hosts work in NAV-adjacent contexts.
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Mark Moore on public value; Mariana Mazzucato on mission-led public strategy; specific Norwegian writing on NAV and Statens forvaltningsapparat strategy work.
incomplete
Strategy as power is gently visible (who gets the IT-køen priority that Vips got) but not analyzed as power. The political economy of must-win-battles inside a large organization — who loses access to resources, and through what mechanism — is left implicit.
15) Kari Olrud Moen om strategi, kunsten å velge bort og forskjellen på å være tydelig og skråsikker — read next: Cutler on power and space (TBM 413, recently in user's library); Pfeffer on power in organizations; Helmer/Porter for the strategic value of being explicit about who-loses-what.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The 'outsourcet problemløsning' framing is sharp but Strümke does not develop the policy mechanics: what counts as 'building it yourself' on a continuum? OSS-finetuned-from-an-open-base, Norway-hosted closed-weight, fully in-house from scratch — the framing presupposes a binary that procurement policy would have to operationalize.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Cuijpers / EU AI Act interpretation on capability sovereignty; Anu Bradford on Brussels effect; OpenEuroLLM / Silo AI literature on sovereign-capability tiers; recent NSM/Nkom positions on cloud sovereignty thresholds.
AI Regulation & Governance (External)Insourcing & In-House Capability
incomplete
The data-manifold frame is presented as Strümke's primary lens but she explicitly says 'we don't have access to either manifold' — so the entire empirical apparatus that would let us know how close approximation has gotten remains undeveloped in the conversation. The host doesn't push on what an operational proxy would even look like.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Yamins & DiCarlo on neural-net-vs-cortex representational geometry; Bengio on the manifold hypothesis as ML's organizing assumption; recent interpretability work (Anthropic's circuit-tracing, sparse autoencoders) as one empirical handle on the model side of the question.
incomplete
Strümke argues for neurosymbolic AI and points at Claude Code's regex as proof, but doesn't address the harder question: where does the symbolic boundary go? Claude Code's choice is for a single literal token; the production design pattern (when LLM, when regex, when AST, when proof) is unexplored.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Gary Marcus's neurosymbolic writings; DeepMind's AlphaProof/AlphaGeometry as advanced symbolic-neural hybrids; the harness-engineering literature already in the user's library (Anthropic, Boris Cherny) for production-design patterns.

incomplete
The Norway/Europe-vs-Big-Tech argument is geopolitically sharp but treats the US bloc as monolithic. The conversation does not engage with the question of whether dependency on US open-weight models (Llama, Mistral-Mistral-Mistral, etc.) is qualitatively different from dependency on closed-API products like Copilot — even though that distinction may be the most actionable lever Norway has.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Together AI / Mistral / EuroLLM positioning papers; Sam Altman's geopolitical AI writings as counter-foil; Helen Toner / RAND on AI compute geopolitics; recent open-weight-vs-closed-API analyses from CSET.

incomplete
The conversation reaches the 'cover your ass' diagnosis of Norwegian public-sector risk-aversion but does not investigate the structural causes — why has the last ~10 years been 'feigere'? Datatilsynet stance is named, the Nikolai Tangen episode is named, but the institutional feedback loop producing the pattern is not unpacked.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: John Cutler on institutional overload and accountability culture (already in the user's reading); recent NSM/Nkom assessments on resilience; Norwegian public-administration literature (NIBR / NIFU) on risk-distribution between politikere and direktorater; Annie Duke / Daniel Kahneman on outcome-bias driving risk-aversion.
Feedback Loops
incomplete
Strümke names two interesting categories — 'avklaringsspørsmål' (we have the methods, we just need agreement) and 'forskningsspørsmål' (genuinely open) — but doesn't extend the taxonomy. The third missing category is presumably 'capability-building-spørsmål' (we know what we need to build but lack the people / infra to build it) — which is arguably the dominant Norwegian bottleneck.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Donella Meadows on system leverage points (where in this taxonomy is the highest leverage); KI Norge mandate documents from Digdir; recent NTNU/SimulaMet hiring and capacity reports.
uninformed
Strümke is open about her own production-coding skepticism ('jeg merker når jeg sitter og koder med agentene, så er sånn tulleprosjektet'), and the hosts (Audun from NAV) push back gently with the more developed practitioner view that high-stakes work warrants Vim while front-end can be agent-led. Strümke's reading of agentic coding may underweight what's currently possible with proper harness, evals, and sandboxing — though she does flag the macOS sandbox feature.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: User's existing thread on harness engineering (Anthropic videos, Boris Cherny, Bockeler/Fowler static sensors); Beane on Precision Proactivity; the user's earlier Heis Anders Sveen / Vilde Opsal extractions; Greger Wedel; concrete production-engineering reports from Builder.io, Cursor, and Claude Code teams.

incomplete
The closing 'undetectable backdoors' research preview is fascinating but isolated from the rest of the conversation. If undetectable backdoors are plantable, the entire 'outsource problem-solving to Copilot' diagnosis acquires a much sharper national-security dimension that the conversation does not draw.
16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — read next: Goldwasser et al. on undetectable backdoors (the foundational paper); recent NeurIPS / ICML papers on neural-net backdoor detection; supply-chain security literature (SLSA, sigstore) extended to ML model artifacts.
incomplete
The flywheel/S-curve/liminal frame is used to describe both the NAV transformation (which succeeded) and the AI shift (in progress). The conversation never names a transformation that failed at the liminal phase and asks why — so the framework is at risk of being unfalsifiable on its own terms.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Read failed transformations through the same frame — e.g., government-IT projects that died in their liminal phase, large agile adoptions that reverted. Cutler's overload work and the 'people who tried Spotify model' literature would expose where the frame breaks. The Geoffrey Moore 'Crossing the Chasm' tradition has more developed accounts of curves that stall.
Technology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
incomplete
The 'learning vs production' rule is stated as a personal heuristic but not connected to any educational theory or empirical evidence. For a leader of consultants asked to scale this practice, there is no model for how the two modes should be partitioned, whether across days, projects, or moods.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Donald Schön's reflection-in-action; deliberate-practice literature (Ericsson); Beck's 'tidy first?' framing of preparatory vs delivery work — the tidy/messy split echoes the learning/production split structurally. Worth syntopical pass against Beck's recent 'Starving Genies' work on AI-assisted craft.
The Genie (Beck)
incomplete
The elevator metaphor is generalised from Jonas's own career and from the show's animating premise. The conversation does not seriously consider organisations where vertical traversal is structurally suppressed (military, regulated finance with information walls, classified environments). The implicit premise that elevators are always available may not hold.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Read about organisations with designed information barriers — security classifications, MAR-walled investment banking, healthcare compartmentalisation. James C Scott's 'Seeing Like a State' on legibility. Where the elevator is locked, what substitutes?
incomplete
The introvert-leader claim rests on one practitioner's experience and one rhetorical contrast (scale removes small-talk tax). Jonas notes that some colleagues found it hard to distinguish his opinions from his decisions — a cost that may be specifically introvert-shaped (lower social-cue density) but is not investigated.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Susan Cain on introvert leadership; the Cutler thread on 'signals that look like decisions' in leadership communication. The implicit problem of 'low-cue leadership at scale' is worth its own extraction.
incomplete
The tractor and car analogies argue for slow structural change, but neither is examined for disanalogies. AI compresses learning loops in a way physical machinery cannot. The argument relies on the assumption that adoption is gated by humans, not by capability — which is plausible but not defended.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: The general-purpose-technology economics literature (Bresnahan & Trajtenberg); David Autor on labour-market restructuring time. Counter-cases: dial-up to broadband transitions, mobile phones — both moved faster than tractors. What predicts speed?
Learning Culture & Slack
uninformed
The Gukesh anecdote is reported confidently ('the only one of the young players not allowed a computer before age 12') but is not actually verified in the conversation. Whether true or apocryphal, it is doing argumentative work it has not earned.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Quick fact-check; more importantly, treat the underlying claim about protected early craft as a hypothesis and look at the actual evidence on AI use in early-career learning — programming pedagogy research will move on this in 2026-2028.
incomplete
The claim that NAV's success was driven by capability investments is asserted from inside the case by the very people who made those investments. There is no counterfactual — no comparable agency that did not invest similarly and failed similarly. Some of the 'flywheel' may be storytelling.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: Compare cross-country COVID welfare-IT responses (Germany, Denmark, UK), and Norwegian agencies of similar size that did not transform similarly. The Skatteetaten and Helsedirektoratet experiences would be a natural comparison.
Technology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
illogical
Jonas argues both that we are early on the AI S-curve (so 10% gains are not the story) and that complexity will push back on productivity gains as they accumulate. These are compatible but in tension: if the curve climbs steeply because of intent-level abstraction, the complexity ceiling should appear later; if complexity bites soon, the curve is shallower than claimed. The argument is not adjudicated.
17) Jonas Slørdahl Skjærpe om svinghjuleffekter, liminalfaser og kunsten å ta heis — read next: The Brooks / Mythical Man Month tradition on accidental vs essential complexity; Ousterhout on system design; recent measurements on long-running agent harnesses (Anthropic harness design work) which directly test the complexity-ceiling claim.
Agent HarnessLong-Running & Managed AgentsSoftware Design FundamentalsTechnology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
incomplete
Vendor positioning is conspicuous: the story is told entirely from the survivors' chair. MobilePay engineers are quoted only as having insisted their tech was 'objectively better', MCash veterans get one similar line, and no failures are attributed to Vipps leadership specifically. The MCash 2017 carve-out is treated as smoothly inevitable; the operational chaos is named ('brannslukningsarbeid') but never traced to a decision.
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: MobilePay engineering blog posts from 2023–2024; the Danish fintech press's coverage of the merger; Kristian Pinsel Halvorsen interviews (his name is dropped as the host's 'old boss' from FINN, suggesting a counter-perspective on big-bang migrations in Schibsted/FINN territory)
incomplete
The role of DNB through the entire history is underexamined. Vipps was DNB's first public-cloud workload, DNB's CEO drove the founding, MCash brought the other Norwegian banks in, and yet the bank-shareholder governance — including how decisions like 'use Vipps's platform, not MobilePay's' actually get taken — is essentially a black box.
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: Norwegian financial press on Vipps AS ownership changes; Rune Bjerke's memoir/interviews; the prior Heis episode with Kari Olrud Moen (ep 15, 'strategi tydelig og skråsikker') — Moen is Vipps's CEO context
Strategy & Direction-SettingTechnology Leadership (Heis)
incomplete
Tap to Pay is celebrated as a success, but the speakers stop short of explaining what economic margin Vipps captures from international card schemes vs. domestic BankAxept on in-store transactions. Given that the entire European sovereignty argument hinges on dependency on American card schemes, the cost/risk picture matters strategically.
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: Norwegian card-scheme regulator (Finanstilsynet) cost analyses; EU Digital Markets Act / Payment Services Directive 3 documentation; ECB reports on European payments sovereignty
incomplete
The 'verb-status' achievement is mentioned but not problematised. Mobilepay is also a verb in Danish; both verbs are now inside one company. What happens to brand neutrality and to user expectations when a Norwegian verb is owned by a Nordic company that wants to be European? The speakers laugh it off ('det tror jeg ikke vi skal prøve å spørre' about Finnish usage).
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: Sociolinguistics of brand-verb status; comparable post-merger brand consolidations in payments (Klarna, Wise); MobilePay marketing material from the merger announcement
incomplete
AI in regulated production code is left as an open problem. The 'humanize ser på koden' (humans review code) regulatory expectation is named, but the speakers don't engage with how Norwegian Finanstilsynet, the EU AI Act, or DORA-style governance frameworks will actually treat AI-generated code in financial production.
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: Finanstilsynet guidance on AI in financial services; EU AI Act high-risk system requirements for payments; DORA 2025 findings on AI-assisted development in regulated industries
AI Regulation & Governance (External)DORA & Engineering MetricsPull Requests & Async Code Review
incomplete
Architecture is described as never planned ('aldri hatt et stort arkitekturprosjekt') but the result is a mature multi-cloud, multi-region, regulated platform with 35 product teams and 170+ engineers. The gap between 'we never planned it' and 'we ended up here' is unexamined — what is the actual feedback mechanism that lets a no-architecture-project culture not drift into mush?
