Technology Leadership (Heis)
incomplete
The speakers describe weekly releases and trunk-based-ish development for the app, but back-end deploy cadence and the cross-team coordination model when 35 product teams plus a payments backbone plus an AI platform team all ship at different speeds is not described in detail.
18) Vipps: Fra app til verb til nordisk infrastruktur — read next: DORA 2024–2025 reports on deploy frequency and change-failure rate in financial services; NAV's 'fast flow' writeup that Kjetil has already saved (per persona)
DORA & Engineering MetricsPlatform Engineering
incomplete
Survivorship bias in the case studies: Notion, Figma, Canva, and Stripe are all wildly successful companies. No failed community-led growth attempts are discussed. The conversation does not address companies that invested heavily in community and did not see returns, making it impossible to assess the base rate of success for this strategy.
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Seek case studies of failed community-led growth efforts to understand failure modes and necessary conditions beyond 'freemium + atomic unit of sharing'.
incomplete
The measurement problem is acknowledged but left unresolved. Ricketts advises against early measurement, and the primary metric offered (net new website visitors) has obvious attribution challenges. The advice amounts to 'trust that it's working based on qualitative signals,' which is reasonable for a $10B company with founder buy-in but unhelpful for resource-constrained startups.
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Look for frameworks that quantify community ROI without premature optimization — e.g., lagging indicators, cohort analysis of community-exposed vs unexposed users.
Managing Up & InfluenceTrust
uninformed
The cost side of community-led growth is never discussed. Three full-time community team members, subsidized events (30/month globally), influencer budgets — the total investment is never quantified. Without cost data, the implicit 'just invest in community' advice cannot be evaluated by companies with different resource levels.
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Seek data on the actual cost structure of running community programs at scale — headcount, event budgets, influencer spend, opportunity cost.
incomplete
The 2×2 matrix (PMF × enterprise/consumer) is teased but only two of four quadrants are discussed in any detail (post-PMF consumer = ambassadors/influencers; post-PMF enterprise = champions/consultants). Pre-PMF consumer and the full pre-PMF enterprise quadrant get only cursory treatment (customer advisory boards mentioned briefly).
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Ricketts mentions she will share the full matrix; seek that artifact or construct the remaining quadrants from other sources.
illogical
There is a tension between 'don't impose metrics early' and 'our KPI was net new website visitors.' If net new visitors was the team's responsibility metric, that IS an early metric. The advice to avoid measurement is somewhat contradicted by the actual practice described.
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Clarify the timeline: when exactly did Notion start measuring community impact formally, and what qualitative proxies did they use before that?
incomplete
The conversation treats community, content, and press as three organic growth levers but does not discuss how they interact or cannibalize each other. Are they additive? Multiplicative? Does over-investment in one reduce returns on others?
How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts — read next: Look for integrated frameworks that model the interaction between community, content, and earned media as a system rather than independent channels.
incomplete
Methodological (the study's own thinnest point): 'social listening' on public posts is a convenience sample of vocal, self-selecting users. There is no denominator, no correction for selection bias, and no way to distinguish frequency-of-posting from frequency-of-use — so a ranking of what people TALK ABOUT online is presented as a ranking of what people DO, and emotionally charged uses (therapy, grief, romance) that generate more posting are structurally over-weighted. The order-of-magnitude larger dataset improves precision but not representativeness.
How People Are Really Using AI in 2026 — read next: Read the author's own 'Making sense of research on how people use AI' (HBR, Nov 2025) alongside survey- or telemetry-based usage studies (e.g., first-party Anthropic/OpenAI usage reports) to triangulate the social-listening picture against a representative sample and quantify the selection bias.
incomplete
The 'marginal rather than game-changing, so far' verdict gives no mechanism, threshold, or leading indicator for what would convert marginal efficiencies into transformation — and this is now the third consecutive edition landing on a version of the same 'transformation still pending' verdict. The recurring lag is left unexamined: the piece never asks whether the bottleneck is temporal (models/adoption maturing) or structural (organizational absorption, legibility, shadow usage privatizing gains).
How People Are Really Using AI in 2026 — read next: Read enterprise-adoption analyses that locate the bottleneck — extract-the-organization-is-the-bottleneck (maturity as amplifier), extract-the-unsexy-truth-of-ai-adoption (the L1->L2 legibility wall), extract-enterprise-ai-leadership-problem (93/7 spend split), and the forward-deployed-engineer literature — to test whether 'so far' names a temporal or a structural gap.
Forward-Deployed Engineer
incomplete
Consumer/individual usage is used to reason about enterprise value: the dataset is 'mostly individuals... at home and at work,' yet the business transformation verdict is drawn from it. Individual bottom-up use and organizational value are different units and are conflated, which both flatters adoption breadth and muddies the value verdict.
How People Are Really Using AI in 2026 — read next: Read extract-who-cares-about-consumer-ai (the ~100x per-user token-economics split between consumer and enterprise) and enterprise-specific ROI studies to keep consumer-scale adoption separate from organization-level value realization.
uninformed
The thinkslop harm is asserted at population scale from vivid single-user anecdotes. The study measures the prevalence of use cases where thinking COULD be outsourced (a quarter of top uses) but not the cognitive damage actually incurred, and — crucially — it presents the sharpening 'sparring-partner' use from the same data without ever quantifying the crutch-vs-foil ratio, so the headline concern rests on an unmeasured mix.
How People Are Really Using AI in 2026 — read next: Read the cited 'cognitive debt' study (arXiv 2506.08872, 'Your Brain on ChatGPT') and extract-cognitive-surrender (offloading vs. surrender, posture-dependence, the 73% wrong-answer acceptance finding) for the mechanism and the controlled evidence the HBR piece substitutes anecdote for.
Cognitive Debt
incomplete
Provenance is asserted, not sourced, and the title oversells it. 'Straight from the team that built it' implies insider access, but the body reveals the actual source is Anthropic's PUBLIC official prompting guide, relayed by an anonymous X account with no link to the guide, no author attribution, and no way to see what was faithfully relayed versus added or distorted. The persuasive authority exceeds the sourcing.
How to Actually Prompt Fable 5, Straight From the Team That Built It — read next: Read the primary guide directly (extract-prompting-claude-fable-5) instead of this relay; treat 'straight from the team' as marketing, not provenance. Locate and confirm the thread-only specifics — the ultrathink/ultracode keywords, the thinking.display value semantics, the sub-5% refusal rate, prompt-cache-cost coverage, the 'audit beat softer phrasings' result, Mythos-drops-cyber — against Anthropic's actual published pages before relying on any of them.
MythosPrompting as Communication
incomplete
Double staleness / secondary-source drift. The thread compresses an UNDATED official guide (flagged in the sibling extraction as drifting silently as the model and platform update) and is itself a dated July-1-2026 tweet — a dated secondary source of an undated primary. Every version-specific claim (effort defaults, classifier domains, thinking.display values, xhigh-model list, sub-5% refusal rate) is twice-removed from the changelog.
How to Actually Prompt Fable 5, Straight From the Team That Built It — read next: Re-verify every version-specific claim against the platform changelog and the 'Introducing Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5' page at time of use; never migrate a production workflow on a tweet's restatement of an undated doc.
Mythos
incomplete
No evals or verification — the same bar as the docs, and no better. The thread inherits the official guide's central weakness (no reproducible benchmark, dataset, baseline, or numbers for any recommended prompt block) and layers on further unverified quantification ('audit-language produced more reliable self-checking than softer equivalents'; refusals affect 'under five percent of typical developer queries') with no method behind either. A thread adds zero measurement.
How to Actually Prompt Fable 5, Straight From the Team That Built It — read next: Do not treat the thread as evidence for any technique. Build an A/B eval harness (as the sibling gap prescribes) that measures each system-prompt block's marginal effect on your own tasks before adopting it; pair with an evals-disciplined source (extract-anthropic-harness-design-long-running-application-development).
Agent HarnessEvalsLong-Running & Managed AgentsPrompting as Communication
incomplete
Selection/hype bias of the thread format: it reproduces the guide's recommendations but trims its hedges. It presents effort as 'not a quality dial' while dropping the guide's intelligence axis and its warning that higher effort can over-deliberate; it never addresses interaction effects among the stacked system-prompt blocks (brevity, audit, proactivity, memory, final-response) that the reader is told to add together; and the engagement-optimized 'different category of tool' framing amplifies upside while muting trade-offs.
How to Actually Prompt Fable 5, Straight From the Team That Built It — read next: Read the primary guide for the caveats the thread trims; assemble and test the composed system prompt as a whole (the sibling's interaction-effects gap) rather than trusting the thread's flattened, upside-first presentation.
Agent Memory
incomplete
No outcome evidence or survivorship check. The essay is entirely prescriptive, illustrated with hypotheticals; there is no reported ask that succeeded or failed and no base rate, so we cannot tell whether these heuristics actually raise reply rates or merely describe a plausible-sounding etiquette derived from the author's own (presumably successful) experience.
How to ask for help from people who don't know you — read next: Read against empirical work on cold-outreach response — cold-email reply-rate studies, referral-effect data, and any A/B-tested outreach experiments — to convert intuition into base rates.
incomplete
The method presupposes the asker already has demonstrable proof of work and something to trade. It does not address the person at the very start with nothing to show, or asks made purely from need — arguably the case where help matters most and is hardest to earn.
How to ask for help from people who don't know you — read next: Read alongside literature on asking from low status / genuine need, and on how newcomers bootstrap a first credible artifact before they have any standing.
incomplete
Tension with the essay's own constructible-credibility thesis (p10). It dismisses institutional credibility as 'weak,' but inherited status, warm networks and pedigree heavily determine who even opens your message and grants the initial attention the whole method depends on; 'credibility is built, not assigned' understates how status-gated the starting line is.
How to ask for help from people who don't know you — read next: Read against work on networks, homophily and privilege in who gets replies — and against extract-heis-03-christin-gorman-leder-uten-tittel#g1 ('whose work is invisible without a platform').
Trust
incomplete
Silent on scale and on the responder's side of the ledger. There is no treatment of what happens when many askers apply the same tactics to one sought-after responder (specificity and low-friction blurbs become an arms race; inbox overload dominates), nor of when or why a responder should say yes.
How to ask for help from people who don't know you — read next: Read alongside attention-economy / inbox-overload treatments and issue-selling-at-scale critiques (e.g., the overload gap in extract-hbr-buy-in-for-big-idea).
Managing Up & Influence
incomplete
Culture-specificity is unexamined. The prescribed norms — directness, brevity, low-obligation asks, and especially 'make it easy to say no' — reflect a particular high-individualist, low-context professional culture; the essay presents them as universal, but reciprocity and obligation norms differ sharply across cultures.
How to ask for help from people who don't know you — read next: Read against cross-cultural communication research (high- vs low-context cultures; differing norms of reciprocity, face and obligation) to see where the heuristics invert.
paywall
Several content blocks could not be imported from the original HBR document due to paywall restrictions. These appear at the beginning, middle, and end of the article and likely contain supplementary content (author bio, related articles, or promotional material).
How to Convince Your Boss They Need a Coach — read next: Access the full article at hbr.org with a subscription to verify no substantive content was missed.
evidence
The article's recommendations rest almost entirely on the author's consulting experience and anecdotal client cases. No controlled studies, success/failure rates, or systematic evidence for the five-step framework are offered.
How to Convince Your Boss They Need a Coach — read next: Look for empirical research on upward influence tactics in organizational psychology (e.g., Yukl & Falbe on influence tactics) to assess whether the recommended strategies have empirical support.
scope
The article assumes a relatively healthy organizational context. It does not address what to do when the boss's blind spots constitute toxic or harmful leadership, where coaching alone may be insufficient.
How to Convince Your Boss They Need a Coach — read next: Consider literature on managing toxic leadership, whistleblowing, and organizational exit as complementary frames.
Decision-Making Practices
perspective
Written entirely from the subordinate's perspective. The executive's own experience of being 'managed into' coaching is not explored -- raising questions about whether the strategy, if detected, could feel manipulative.
How to Convince Your Boss They Need a Coach — read next: Contrast with literature on authentic leadership and ethical influence to assess the manipulation concern.
missing_counterargument
The article does not address the possibility that coaching may not be the right intervention -- for instance, when the problem is structural (bad incentives, poor organizational design) rather than behavioral.
How to Convince Your Boss They Need a Coach — read next: Consider systems-thinking perspectives on leadership problems, e.g., Senge's 'The Fifth Discipline.'
incomplete
The method assumes a single, fixed message that already exists, whole, before writing begins -- 'you know where you will stop before you start.' It has no place for exploratory or discovery writing, in which the point emerges only through the act of drafting. This is in tension with Thompson's own implicit 'clear writing = clear thinking': he treats thinking as prior to and separable from writing (think via the cards, then transcribe), whereas the writing-as-thinking tradition holds that writing IS the thinking, and that outlining-first can only help once the hard cognitive work is already done.
How to write clearly — read next: Read Peter Elbow 'Writing With Power' and Sonke Ahrens 'How to Take Smart Notes' (Zettelkasten) on writing as thinking-to-discover, and Franco 'What we lose when AI writes for us' on writing as constituting (not merely reporting) a view -- against Thompson's think-first-then-write separation.
Writing as Thinking
incomplete
'Clarity' is treated as always achievable by simplification, but some subjects are irreducibly complex, uncertain, or essentially ambiguous, and faithful writing must preserve that -- flattening them into one clean surface reading can falsify. Every Thompson exemplar (all the fish died; the cherry tree) is a subject where nothing complex is lost; none tests the claim against genuinely conceptual or contested material.
How to write clearly — read next: Read Ousterhout on essential vs. accidental complexity and deep-simple vs. simplistic, and technical-writing / explanation-theory work on rendering irreducible complexity without falsifying it -- to see where 'be clear' shades into 'oversimplify.'
Software Design FundamentalsWriting as Thinking
incomplete
The essay is pre-digital and pre-collaborative: it prescribes clarity as a solo writer's individual discipline, yet its own proof texts ('Notice how WE edited this', 'Condensing, as Reader's Digest does it') reveal that the exemplary clear prose is the product of a collective editorial apparatus. It says nothing about writing with editors, collaborators, revision tooling, or (now) AI drafting assistants -- the very machinery that actually produces the 'condensed' Digest style it holds up as the model.
How to write clearly — read next: Read on editorial process and collaborative revision (how Reader's Digest condensing actually worked as a pipeline), and current work on AI-assisted drafting where the outline-first and condense steps are negotiated with a machine rather than performed alone.
Writing as Thinking
incomplete
'Start where your readers are -- how much do they know?' assumes a single, knowable audience with an identifiable common level of knowledge (natural for a mass magazine with an imagined 'average reader'). It gives no guidance for heterogeneous or unknown audiences -- the default condition of digital publishing -- where readers span wildly different priors and there is no one level to start from.
How to write clearly — read next: Read on writing for mixed audiences and layered/progressive-disclosure documents (letting expert and novice readers each take the path suited to them) rather than a single pitched level.
Prompting as CommunicationWriting as Thinking
incomplete
The essay is entirely of the print-essay era: it says nothing about digital, collaborative, or AI-assisted writing. Its whole model — a single author revealing a single self to a reader — does not touch what 'sounding like yourself' or 'style as self-revelation' mean when text can be machine-generated, co-written, or stylistically homogenized at scale.
How to write with style — read next: Read against Franco 'What we lose when AI writes for us' (style/voice as the Self, and its convergence under AI), Acunzo 'The best response to AI slop' (distinctive firsthand voice as the non-commoditizable signal), and the taste article (correctness vs. quality when a machine produces the prose).
AI SlopTaste & JudgmentWriting as Thinking
incomplete
'Sound like yourself' is under-specified to the point of circularity for a novice: it presupposes an already-articulate, trustworthy voice worth sounding like, and locates that voice in one's origin and childhood speech rather than in any developable skill. It offers no method for a beginner whose natural voice is inarticulate or undeveloped to find or grow one.
How to write with style — read next: Read a pedagogy-of-voice source (Zinsser 'On Writing Well', Peter Elbow's 'Writing With Power') and the deliberate-practice literature on how voice is cultivated rather than inherited.
Writing as Thinking
incomplete
The simplicity argument generalizes from emotional profundity to all profundity. Every exemplar (Hamlet's soliloquy, Joyce's 'She was tired', Genesis) is emotional or narrative depth rendered plainly; none is a technically or conceptually irreducible subject. The claim that childlike simplicity fits 'the most profound subjects' may not survive contact with domains whose complexity is essential, not incidental.
How to write with style — read next: Read Ousterhout on essential vs. accidental complexity and the difference between deep-simple and simplistic; explanation theory / technical-writing craft on rendering irreducible complexity without falsifying it.
Software Design FundamentalsWriting as Thinking
incomplete
The claim that non-standard voice is 'delightful' assumes a reader who is charmed by it ('like a very pretty girl with one eye green and one blue'), with no account of the many contexts where non-standard or regional voice is penalized rather than trusted. 'Sound like yourself' is treated as costless, which may reflect the privilege of a canonical author rather than the situation of a writer whose authentic voice carries real reputational cost.
How to write with style — read next: Read sociolinguistics on prestige dialects and linguistic discrimination, and the literature on code-switching, to see where authentic voice is a liability rather than a delight.
incomplete
The lab-vs-enterprise conflation. The argument is strongest for frontier labs that BUILD token capital (models, evals, loops require irreverent frontier-movers) and weakest for the telco, bank or typical enterprise that BUYS and absorbs it. A firm that consumes AI rather than pushing the frontier may not need 'permission-averse, loudly-wrong' talent at all — for it the binding constraint is more likely absorption, integration and process legibility than porousness for mavericks. The essay treats the two as one problem and never tests whether its prescription even applies to the majority of the 54% of employment it invokes.
What is the harder human-capital problem beneath token capital? — read next: Read against the enterprise-AI-adoption literature that separates building vs. buying/absorbing AI capability (e.g. forward-deployed-engineer and readiness models): does a token-capital consumer need frontier-mover porousness, or is its real obstacle organizational absorption, and would importing 'uncorporate' talent even help?
EvalsForward-Deployed EngineerToken Capital
incomplete
'Porous structure' is named as the answer but never operationalized. The essay supplies no mechanism, org-design pattern, incentive structure, or worked example of what porousness looks like, nor how a firm hands 'corporate-scale compute + responsibility' to uncorporate people without recreating the committees and procurement logic that sand them down. The prescription is a direction, not a design.
What is the harder human-capital problem beneath token capital? — read next: Read toward concrete structural patterns that could fill the mechanism — small-teams/tiny-core bets, autonomous-team designs, skunkworks and lab-within-a-company structures — and test which actually preserve irreverence at corporate scale.
illogical
The essay's own evidence strains against its thesis. Demis Hassabis built Gemini INSIDE Google, a corporate giant — 'in spite of' it, yes, but still inside it and still winning — which shows rigid incumbents CAN produce frontier results, cutting against the flat claim that structure 'selects frontier talent out.' By naming only the winning obsessives (Hassabis, Dean, Liang Wenfeng) it also survivorship-selects, saying nothing about the uncorporate people who were in fact crushed. So 'structure selects them out' is asserted more strongly than the evidence, which really shows 'structure taxes them, but some still win.'
What is the harder human-capital problem beneath token capital? — read next: Read comparatively for cases where incumbents DID retain and productively hold frontier talent, to separate the strong claim ('structure filters them out') from the weaker, better-supported one ('structure imposes a heavy tax they sometimes overcome').
practical
No quantitative framework for when to invest in human scalability interventions. The talk says companies often come to Bravely at 100-150 people when employee surveys flag problems, but offers no guidance on investment timing, budget, or ROI metrics.
Presentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code
theoreticalclosed
The relationship between AI/automation and human scalability is acknowledged ('AI is doing a lot') but immediately dismissed ('this will still always be true'). No exploration of how AI tools might change the communication overload or shared context problems — or whether they introduce new trust challenges.
Presentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code
✓ closed byYour AI strategy has a trust problem, not a tooling problem — Explores what the talk dismissed: AI agents distribute context and shrink the middle-management translation problem, shifting the binding constraint of scaling from communication overload to trust and permission structures.
Trust
evidential
The talk relies heavily on the Spotify squad model and Google Project Aristotle as examples, but these are well-known cases that may not generalize. No counter-examples are discussed — organizations that scaled human cooperation successfully without these specific interventions.
Presentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code
theoretical
Dunbar's number is presented as a hard cognitive limit, but the speaker herself notes it was 'measured on neurotypical people' and is 'a guess.' The implications of neurodiversity for organizational scaling are raised but not explored.
Presentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code
practical
The interventions (communication architecture, trust engineering, distributed cohesion) are presented as a menu of techniques without a theory of sequencing or prioritization. Which matters most at 50 people vs. 200 vs. 1000? The talk doesn't say.
Presentation: The Human Scalability Problem: Why Your Teams Don't Scale Like Your Code
Coordination CostTrust
incomplete
Same gap as the Golden Bricks extract, in a different colour. The whole programme assumes a Team-Topologies / Spotify-style organisation already exists — stream-aligned squads inside tribes, a platform team adjacent to them, business decision-makers sitting at tribe level. The Federated SRE, production manager, and technical tribe lead are role inventions overlaid on this topology, not designed to produce it. The article describes role responsibilities mechanically but treats the underlying org redesign — the part that costs years and political capital in most enterprises — as already done. For a reader whose org is functionally shaped, this is the part that matters most, and it is invisible.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: Skelton & Pais, Team Topologies (esp. the 'enabling team' interaction mode, which is what Federated SREs functionally are). Cutler on operating-model design and accountability layering. Pais on cognitive-load assessment as a precondition for federation. Reinertsen on capacity allocation and queueing — the 20% tithe is a capacity-allocation decision that needs queue-theoretic defence.
Golden PathPlatform Engineering
incomplete
The 'democratisation' mechanism is never opened up. The article uses 'democratised SLOs/SLAs' as the load-bearing cultural claim but never explains how democratisation happens — who publishes the SLOs, in what tool, who is allowed to negotiate them, what stops squads from quietly setting easy SLOs to escape scrutiny, what the production manager does when a squad's SLO drifts. Without this, 'data-driven conversations' is a slogan with no enforcement surface. The mechanical specification matters: democratisation without governance is just SLO theatre.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: Google SRE Workbook on error budgets and SLO policy (the governance scaffolding democratisation usually rides on). Honeycomb / Charity Majors on observability ownership. Forsgren et al. on metrics that resist Goodhart pressure.
incomplete
The 20% mandatory squad investment is presented as a structural lever, but the article never says how it is funded. 20% of every squad is a large standing tax on feature delivery; in most orgs this is the single most contested resource decision a CTO can make. The article gives no defence of the figure (why 20% and not 10% or 30%), no description of how it shows up in planning (is it a fifth person, a fifth of everyone's week, a rotation?), no answer to what gets dropped when the rest of the squad's commitments don't shrink. This is the place where the prescription would either hold or shatter in practice.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: Reinertsen, The Principles of Product Development Flow, on capacity allocation under variability. Allen Holub on the politics of capacity-as-budget. Cutler on the operating-system level of organisational design.
incomplete
The four-rebuilds story is reported as triumph but never costed. Destroying and rebuilding the platform architecture four times has a cost — for the consuming squads who must re-learn it, for the regulators who must re-approve it, for the institutional memory that bleeds out at each rebuild. The article reports the platform team's experience ('it worked perfectly for us') without checking the consumer side. The same testimony from the squads might read very differently.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: Ford, Parsons & Kua on evolutionary architecture (the disciplined version of what Petean is describing). Hohpe on architecture decision records and the institutional cost of irreversibility. Naur on programming-as-theory-building — what gets destroyed in a rebuild that isn't in the code.
Architecture Decision RecordsEvolutionary Architecture & Fitness FunctionsPlatform Engineering
incomplete
Sovereignty is bolted on, not integrated. The piece spends most of its length on federation, roles, and metrics, then in the last beats pivots to digital sovereignty as a 'must-have' design constraint. There is no analysis of how the role/metric apparatus supports sovereignty — does the production manager track hyperscaler-exit readiness as an SLO? Do Federated SREs price each architecture decision against move-off cost? Without this connection the sovereignty section reads like a separate talk, and the unified 'culture of data-driven conversations' frame doesn't reach that far.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: EU NIS2 / DORA (financial regulation) on operational resilience requirements. Gregor Hohpe on multi-cloud strategy and exit costs. Honeycomb / CNCF material on platform portability.
Data SovereigntyDORA & Engineering MetricsPlatform Engineering
incomplete
Cost-per-change is offered as the financial counterpart to DORA but is not interrogated. Of the four reasons given for the decline (platform effect, expertise maturity, federation, massive business scaling), the fourth alone could explain the result via simple denominator inflation. Without weighting or counterfactual ('cost per change held flat through a comparable scaling period before federation'), the metric is decorative. This matters because cost-per-change is the kind of number that gets quoted as proof of platform ROI without the caveats travelling with it.
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: FinOps Foundation material on attribution and unit economics. Cutler on signal-vs-noise in metrics. The DORA / SPACE original papers on the limits of self-reported organisational improvement.
AI Unit EconomicsDORA & Engineering MetricsPlatform Engineering
uninformed
No engagement with the existing literature on embedded/federated SRE. Google's SRE Book has a chapter on the embedded-SRE model; the CNCF Platforms WG has written on platform-team interaction modes; Team Topologies' 'enabling team' is functionally close to the Federated SRE role described here. The article presents the three-role pattern as if it were freshly invented, which makes it harder to compare against the failure modes the prior literature documents (embedded SREs going native to squads, central guidance eroding, federation creating shadow operators).
How a Culture of Data-Driven Conversations Can Support Platform Engineering — read next: Beyer, Jones, Petoff, Murphy (eds.), Site Reliability Engineering, esp. the embedded-SRE chapter. CNCF Platforms WG whitepapers on team topologies for platforms. Skelton & Pais, Team Topologies, on the enabling-team interaction mode.
Platform EngineeringSRE & Incident Response
incomplete
No outcome evidence. The piece asserts the product approach is better but supplies zero before/after data — no reduced backlog, no adoption numbers, no user-satisfaction or lead-time improvement. It persuades by resonance with the reader's own pain, not by measured result.
Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products — read next: Mik Kersten, 'Project to Product' (value-stream flow metrics); DORA / SPACE for platform outcome measurement; Evan Bottcher, 'What I Talk About When I Talk About Platforms' (Thoughtworks) for how to evidence platform value.
DORA & Engineering MetricsPlatform Engineering
incomplete
The funding and governance shift is invisible. Moving from project budgets (predictable, milestone-scoped, one-time) to ongoing product funding — and granting the platform team authority to prioritize by aggregate value and to say no — is the real organizational cost of product thinking, and the article never names it.
Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products — read next: Mik Kersten, 'Project to Product' (funding argument); Sriram Narayan, 'Products Over Projects' (martinfowler.com); John Cutler on product operating models and how funding shapes behavior.
Platform Engineering
incomplete
The piece fuses the mindset shift with one specific cloud-provider architecture (self-service, CRDs, service/control/management planes). It leaves unresolved whether that architecture is a precondition of product thinking, a consequence of it, or merely this team's instance — a lighter platform could adopt product thinking without it.
Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products — read next: Skelton & Pais, 'Team Topologies' (platform-as-product and the 'thinnest viable platform'); John Cutler on separating operating model from tooling.
Platform Engineering
incomplete
The 'internal customer' analogy is imported without its main caveat: internal customers usually cannot exit or choose an alternative, so adoption can be produced by mandate rather than genuine demand — making adoption a gameable success metric. The captive-customer problem is never addressed.
Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products — read next: Marty Cagan, 'Empowered' / 'Inspired' (do product principles hold for captive internal users?); John Cutler on internal platforms and 'build vs. mandate' dynamics.
incomplete
No deprecation or sunsetting mechanism. The founding complaint is 'accumulated capabilities competing in priority', yet the product model as described adds new abstractions without any account of retiring unused ones — the accumulation problem is diagnosed but structurally left unsolved.
Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products — read next: Kent Beck on removing/simplifying (Tidy First? — subtraction as design work); lifecycle/deprecation practices in platform engineering (e.g., Team Topologies on platform evolution).
Platform Engineering
unsupported premise
The claim that ideal conditions 'do not exist and will never happen' is stated as absolute fact without argument. Some conditions genuinely do improve with waiting (e.g., financial readiness, skill acquisition).
Iron Pencil Statue — read next: Literature on satisficing vs. maximising; the 'good enough' principle in decision theory.
incomplete
The analogy between Bainbridge's control-room operators and software engineers is taken as given but never defended. Bainbridge studied operators of physical, bounded, regulated, slow-dynamics systems (nuclear plants, chemical processes, aviation cockpits) where (a) the human's role is fundamentally MONITORING-AND-OVERRIDE, (b) the system's correct behavior is specified ex ante, (c) regulators audit the human-machine boundary, and (d) the operator is licensed and trained on simulators. Software engineers are CONSTRUCTORS — they design the system itself, not just operate it; the system's correct behavior is partially undefined; no licensing or regulator polices the boundary; and the 'incident' role is only one slice of the work. Reed's mapping works best for the incident-response slice (which is what his job is) and is weakest for routine development. He never disambiguates these. The argument that 'AI ironies for engineers = Bainbridge ironies for operators' may overgeneralize from the incident-response edge case to the whole of engineering practice.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Read the original Bainbridge 1983 paper directly. Then Hollnagel & Woods on resilience engineering, and David Mindell's 'Our Robots, Ourselves' (history of human-machine collaboration). Pair with Naur's 'Programming as Theory Building' for the contrasting view that software engineering is theory construction, not operation.
incomplete
Reed's prescription assumes organizations will reinvest AI-driven efficiency gains in human thoroughness, but the same talk reports that the current pattern is the opposite: 'AI's main impact on my life is more incidents because we're generating more code.' The systems-thinking question of how to design feedback loops that prevent capture of efficiency-as-throughput is left untouched. Without that loop, the 'AI does efficiency, humans do more thoroughness' division is a moral exhortation, not a mechanism.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Donella Meadows on leverage points (especially feedback loops, rules of the system, and goals of the system). John Cutler on institutional overload and on how organizations metabolize efficiency gains. Possibly Christensen on capabilities-as-process — i.e., the org's existing process consumes new capability.
Feedback LoopsMeasuring AI Impact
incomplete
The empirical evidence is thin and uneven. The nurses study (Woods et al., July 2025) is presented as decisive but is a single study, in a different domain (clinical prediction), with a likely small N; the developer study (METR-style) is cited from memory. Reed does not engage the contrary evidence (e.g., positive AI productivity findings from DORA, GitHub Copilot studies) or distinguish task types where AI does and does not pay off. The asymmetric-risk frame is compelling and probably right but is underdetermined by the cited data.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: DORA 2024/2025 AI findings, the Princeton/MIT studies on developer AI productivity, METR's actual published methodology, Woods's published paper (rather than the slide). Also Cal Newport / Bainbridge-skeptics on whether the operator analogy fits knowledge work.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The animacy paradox is introduced as a phenomenological observation but is not connected to design recommendations. If interpretation flips with situational stakes, what design pattern reliably keeps the deterministic-machine interpretation alive in high-tempo contexts? Reed gestures at attribution and explainability but doesn't develop this.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Lucy Suchman 'Plans and Situated Actions'; Dave Woods's papers on 'envisioned worlds' and on coordination breakdowns; possibly the air-traffic-control HCI literature on 'mode confusion'.
Ironies of Automation
incomplete
Reed touches the systems-thinking question (juniors who never built the mental model, attrition of senior engineers carrying tacit knowledge) but doesn't push it to its leverage-point: the org's HIRING and TRAINING flows may be where the irony actually lives, not in the engineer's individual relationship to AI. The dynamic he describes is a stock-and-flow problem (the stock of tacit knowledge is depleting faster than it is being replenished) and warrants stock-and-flow analysis.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Meadows again on stocks-and-flows; the National Research Council's literature on knowledge attrition in nuclear and aerospace; and possibly the apprenticeship literature (Lave and Wenger, situated learning).
Mental Models & UnderstandingOrganizational Legibility
incomplete
The talk does not address AI in NON-incident contexts where its use might genuinely raise the floor — code review feedback, test generation, documentation drafting, refactoring suggestions. Reed says 'I am not an AI hater' but the talk has no positive case for AI use; the practical reader is left with 'avoid in incidents' but no guidance on the rest of the SDLC. The 'fitness for purpose' concept is introduced but only used to rule things out.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Addy Osmani's agent-patterns writing; Kent Beck on AI as a collaborator; the more practitioner-oriented Claude Code deep-dives. Look for places where the asymmetric-risk frame ALSO holds vs. where it doesn't.
Claude CodePull Requests & Async Code Review
illogical
Reed conflates 'AI is non-deterministic' with 'AI lacks interpredictability.' These are related but distinct. Humans are non-deterministic and have high interpredictability with their teammates. The reason AI lacks interpredictability is not non-determinism per se but lack of a SHARED MODEL of context, goals, and norms. This matters because it suggests the fix is not 'make AI more deterministic' but 'invest in shared context and norm-conformance' — a different research and product direction.
Presentation: The Ironies of A^2 I^2 (Ironies of Automation and Artificial Intelligence in Incidents) — read next: Herbert Clark on common ground in conversation; the multi-agent systems literature on shared mental models; recent work on AI alignment through context-engineering.
AI Alignment & SafetyIronies of AutomationLLM LimitationsMental Models & Understanding
omission
No engagement with the counter-argument that AI is categorically different from prior automation. Jevons' Paradox applied to computing, but AI automates cognition itself — the piece never addresses why the historical analogy should hold when the substrate being automated is fundamentally different.
Is AI Doom Going Out of Style?
Jevons Paradox
tension
Conflates elite commentary vibeshift with actual public narrative change. The piece acknowledges polls remain negative but does not reconcile this with its thesis that a meaningful shift is underway.
Is AI Doom Going Out of Style?
AI Labor Disruption
omissionclosed
Software engineering job data is presented without controlling for what kinds of roles are growing. If AI is creating demand for AI-adjacent roles while displacing traditional development, the headline number masks distributional harm.
Is AI Doom Going Out of Style?
✓ closed byAI Companies Are Hiring More — Delivers the distributional decomposition the gap asks for: AI-exposed sectors (tech, finance) losing ~28,000 jobs/month while the overall economy adds ~113,000, showing how headline optimism can mask sectoral displacement.
omission
The Anthropic revenue numbers ($9B to $44B ARR) are presented without questioning sustainability or margin structure beyond the current moment. Revenue growth rate =/= sustainable business.
Is AI Doom Going Out of Style?
underdeveloped
Klein's insight that 8 million displaced workers may be harder to help than 80 million is introduced but not developed. This is potentially the most important policy point in the piece and gets one sentence.
Is AI Doom Going Out of Style?
incomplete
The post never names which use cases the chatbot is serving. 'Hyper-flexible — business ideas to production in moments' is evocative but unactionable. Whether this stack is replacing Copilot Chat for general knowledge work, M365 Copilot for document grounding, or GitHub Copilot for coding makes a huge difference to whether the 'not a 1-1 replacement' concession is a small footnote or a load-bearing caveat.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Look for follow-up posts or case studies from Coop Bank on actual use cases. Compare to Norwegian-bank or DNB AI-adoption pieces. Anthropic's enterprise PDF in this corpus has a better breakdown of what enterprise users actually use AI for.
incomplete
TCO blind spot. The 10,000 DKK figure is Scaleway's invoice. It excludes the engineering team's time to build and maintain the custom UI, evaluate models, manage prompt regressions on model updates, handle incidents, and own the security review. Microsoft's 200,000 DKK at least bundles those responsibilities into a vendor SLA. The fair comparison would include loaded engineering cost — likely 1-3 FTE-equivalent depending on scope, which could be a multiple of the licence savings.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: DORA/state-of-AI reports on hidden costs of self-hosted AI; HashiCorp/Cloudflare PMOs on build-vs-buy economics; any post-mortem of a 'we built our own ChatGPT' enterprise project at 6/12/24 month checkpoints.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
Capability gap is conceded but not priced. Microsoft Copilot's value proposition is heavily about M365 grounding (your SharePoint, email, Teams content) and IDE integration, neither of which is replicable in one day of custom UI work on top of Scaleway. The cost-vs-cost comparison may be comparing two different products.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Compare to recent extractions: HBR managers-AI-productivity piece, Anthropic enterprise PDF on what users actually do with Copilot. Microsoft's own published case studies on Copilot ROI claims.
incomplete
Sovereignty definition is implicit. 'Rent europæisk cloud-miljø' could mean (a) EU data residency only, (b) EU-owned operating entity (Scaleway parent: Iliad SA, French), or (c) full immunity from US CLOUD Act/extraterritorial discovery. These are very different bars and matter differently to a Danish bank under DORA and Finanstilsynet supervision. Frederiksen doesn't disambiguate.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Schrems II commentary; EU Cloud Code of Conduct; Danish Finanstilsynet guidance on cloud sourcing for credit institutions; the EUCS sovereignty levels (High/Substantial) debate.
Data SovereigntyDORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
Competitive-lead claim doesn't connect. The post leaps from 'our IT stack is cheaper and more sovereign' to 'this widens our competitive lead'. Retail/cooperative banking competition is driven by deposit rates, distribution, brand trust, regulatory standing — not by internal AI chatbot choice. The closing boast is a rhetorical flourish, not an argued claim.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Look for case studies where a specific AI-IT decision moved a measurable bank KPI (NIM, cost-to-income ratio, customer acquisition cost). Beck/Shoup Jevons-paradox piece for why cheaper IT often gets eaten by induced demand rather than dropping to the bottom line.
Trust
uninformed
The post predates / sidesteps the harness-engineering thread in this corpus. Frederiksen is solving the procurement layer (which model, hosted where, billed how). Pocock / Anthropic / Cloudflare Mythos / smallcode are solving the orchestration layer above it (how to actually get reliable work out of any model, sovereign or not). The two layers are orthogonal — but a complete enterprise AI strategy needs both, and Frederiksen's post implies that getting procurement right is sufficient. It isn't.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Pocock workshop extraction; Anthropic harness paper; Bernhard post on value-stream targeting; Cloudflare Mythos for the orchestration economics.
Agent HarnessEnterprise AI Adoption & ReadinessMythos
uninformed
Doesn't engage with the Norwegian-public-sector / Nordic AI-sovereignty parallel discussion the user has been tracking (Entur, NAV, Heis ep 11 on AI in the public sector). The Danish-bank case is structurally similar to public-sector deployment decisions that this corpus already covers — same EU-egress constraint, same Microsoft-default temptation. The cross-Nordic comparison would strengthen the case.
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: Heis ep 11 AI-special; the Entur and NAV AI adoption pieces in the user's library; any Finanstilsynet/Datatilsynet (Norwegian) guidance on AI sourcing.
Enterprise AI Adoption & Readiness
incomplete
The 'under a day' UI build is the most contentious empirical claim in the post and the least examined. In most banks, the path from 'we built a chatbot UI' to 'production-grade chatbot serving internal users under bank IT governance' includes security review, authentication, audit logging, prompt-injection mitigations, content filtering, and operational monitoring — none of which fit in a day. Either the chatbot is internal-pilot scope and the production claim is loose, or substantial prior infrastructure made the one-day claim possible (auth, logging, deployment pipeline already in place).
95% cheaper and sovereign AI: Why we dropped Copilot at Coop Bank — LinkedIn post by Jannik William Frederiksen — read next: OWASP LLM Top 10; Anthropic's enterprise security posture documentation; case studies of bank chatbot post-mortems on production-grade rollouts.
evaluative
No empirical comparison to competing agent frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Semantic Kernel) on durability, latency, or cost.
JobRunr Introduces ClawRunr, an Open-Source Java AI Agent — read next: Hands-on benchmarks or independent reviews of Spring AI vs. LangGraph for durable agent workloads
security
Shell execution and broad file access are powerful tools, but sandboxing, permission scoping, and audit trails are not described.
JobRunr Introduces ClawRunr, an Open-Source Java AI Agent — read next: Security guidance for self-hosted agents with shell/tool access (Anthropic's Computer Use safety, OWASP LLM Top 10)

conceptualclosed

incompleteclosed
The 'skill issue' framing is never seriously challenged. The conversation does not explore cases where agent failures are genuinely caused by capability limitations rather than user error, creating an unfalsifiable narrative where every failure is attributed to the human.
Skill Issue: Andrej Karpathy on Code Agents, AutoResearch, and the Loopy Era of AI — read next: Research on systematic LLM failure modes that persist regardless of prompting quality (e.g., planning over long horizons, maintaining state across complex multi-step tasks).
✓ closed byLLMs Corrupt Your Documents When You Delegate — DELEGATE-52 documents systematic failures (25% corruption even in frontier models, unimproved by agentic tooling) that persist regardless of operator skill — the counter-evidence to the unfalsifiable skill-issue framing the gap asks for.
Prompting as Communication
incomplete
Auto research is demonstrated only on hyperparameter optimization — a narrow, well-defined search with clear metrics. No evidence or discussion of how it would work on research requiring genuine novelty, taste, or paradigm shifts.
Skill Issue: Andrej Karpathy on Code Agents, AutoResearch, and the Loopy Era of AI — read next: Literature on AI-driven scientific discovery beyond optimization: can agents formulate novel hypotheses, not just test existing ones? See work on AI scientists (e.g., Sakana AI's 'The AI Scientist').
Recursive Self-ImprovementTaste & Judgment
uninformed
The Jevons paradox argument for software engineering jobs is applied without discussing the significant disanalogies: ATMs automated a narrow task within banking, while AI agents potentially automate the entirety of software production. Demand elasticity analysis is hand-waved.
Skill Issue: Andrej Karpathy on Code Agents, AutoResearch, and the Loopy Era of AI — read next: Economic analysis of AI labor displacement that distinguishes between task-level automation (Jevons applies) and occupation-level automation (potentially different dynamics). See Acemoglu vs Brynjolfsson debate.

incomplete
Security and trust implications of the 'apps dissolve into APIs' vision are acknowledged but not seriously explored. Dobby discovered unprotected Sonos APIs — this is a security vulnerability presented as a feature.

incompleteclosed
The conversation between hosts and Karpathy is largely uncritical — hosts rarely push back on claims, and potential downsides of the depicted future (concentration of power, loss of human skill, surveillance via home agents) are not explored.

✓ closed by16) Inga Strümke om hvor AI er på vei, den outsourcede problemløsningen og datamanifolden — Provides the critical perspective on agent dependency the gap seeks: learning requires effortful practice, so agent delegation erodes junior developers' tacit knowledge and destroys the judgment needed to delegate well.
Agentic AI
illogical
Karpathy simultaneously claims (1) everything is a skill issue and current tools are sufficient, and (2) models are jagged, burst at the seams, and going too far ahead makes them net negative. These are in tension — if the tools truly suffice, jaggedness should be manageable.
Skill Issue: Andrej Karpathy on Code Agents, AutoResearch, and the Loopy Era of AI — read next: Framework for distinguishing 'skill issues' from genuine capability boundaries in AI agent systems.
Agentic AIAI BacklashLLM Limitations
unstated assumption
The argument that ideas replace code assumes LLM agents are reliable enough to produce correct, secure, and maintainable implementations from vague descriptions. No evidence or qualification is offered for this assumption.
Wow, this tweet went very viral! — read next: Literature on LLM code generation reliability and failure modes; studies on specification ambiguity.
missing evidence
The claim that RAG is unnecessary rests on a single data point (~100 articles, ~400K words). No discussion of what happens at 10x or 100x that scale, or with more heterogeneous source material.
Wow, this tweet went very viral! — read next: Benchmarks on LLM retrieval accuracy vs. dedicated RAG at varying corpus sizes.
unaddressed question
Quality and trustworthiness of LLM-generated wiki content is not discussed. How does the user verify that the LLM's summaries, categorisations, and imputed data are correct?
Wow, this tweet went very viral! — read next: Work on LLM hallucination detection, human-in-the-loop verification, and provenance tracking in knowledge bases.
Human-in-the-LoopKnowledge Base
tension
The post says 'you rarely ever write or edit the wiki manually' but also describes a deeply personal, expert-driven workflow. The tension between full LLM autonomy over the wiki and the need for expert curation is unresolved.
Wow, this tweet went very viral! — read next: Research on human-AI collaboration in knowledge curation; when does full delegation to AI degrade knowledge quality?
Knowledge Base
scope limitation
The 'idea file' concept is presented as a general replacement for code sharing, but the example is a single, relatively simple system. Whether idea files work for complex, multi-component systems is untested.
Wow, this tweet went very viral! — read next: Case studies of complex system specifications; comparison of idea-level vs. code-level sharing for different project complexities.

missing critical assessment

bias / framing

Knowledge Base
unstated assumptionclosed
The quality and accuracy of LLM-compiled wiki content is taken for granted. No discussion of hallucination risk, factual drift, or how errors propagate through an LLM-maintained knowledge base over time.
LLM Knowledge Bases — read next: Literature on LLM hallucination in knowledge-intensive tasks; studies on error accumulation in iterative LLM workflows.
✓ closed byYou can't unit test for taste — That doc takes the quality and accuracy of LLM-compiled wiki content for granted with 'no discussion of hallucination risk, factual drift, or how errors propagate.' Tryggvason supplies exactly that missing discussion empirically: LLM enrichment of wiki-style content hallucinated (Central Park/Decatur conflation, inflated populations and mountains), and the mitigation is to ground the model in metadata, keep deterministic attributable sources (Wikipedia, 'a more known failure model' with attribution) authoritative for facts, and restrict the LLM to subjective scoring.
Knowledge Base
missing evidence
No comparison with alternative approaches (manual Zettelkasten, vector-DB RAG, graph databases) to substantiate the claim that this workflow is superior.
LLM Knowledge Bases — read next: Comparative studies of knowledge management approaches; Zettelkasten methodology literature.
unaddressed question
At what scale does this approach break down? The '~small scale' qualifier is vague. No discussion of what happens at 1000+ articles or millions of words.
LLM Knowledge Bases — read next: Benchmarks on LLM context window utilization; scaling properties of index-based vs. vector-based retrieval.
unstated assumptionclosed
Markdown is assumed to be the right format for structured knowledge. No consideration of whether richer representations (knowledge graphs, ontologies, databases) would enable capabilities that flat markdown cannot.
LLM Knowledge Bases — read next: Knowledge representation literature; comparison of markdown wikis vs. knowledge graphs for LLM-assisted reasoning.
✓ closed byTony Seale's Post — That gap notes Karpathy assumes markdown is the right format and never considers whether richer representations (knowledge graphs, ontologies) would enable capabilities flat markdown cannot. Vault-LD is precisely that consideration and a proposed answer: keep the flat markdown but add optional YAML-LD frontmatter so the same files project into a formal knowledge graph/ontology, unlocking inference/validation/query without abandoning the prose.
missing perspectiveclosed
The workflow is described purely from the solo practitioner's perspective. No consideration of collaborative knowledge bases, version control of LLM-generated content, or multi-agent scenarios.
LLM Knowledge Bases — read next: Collaborative knowledge management; version control for AI-generated artifacts.
✓ closed byTony Seale's Post — Partially closes it. That gap flags that Karpathy's workflow is solo-only, with no collaborative or multi-agent perspective. Vault-LD supplies exactly that multi-scale story — team memory, enterprise integration and multi-agent exposure via DPROD data products up to an agentic web — though it still omits the version-control/provenance mechanics the gap also asks for.
Knowledge BaseMulti-Agent Orchestration
incomplete
Team-level and organizational dynamics are almost absent. The piece is written from the individual developer's interior, with the team as a setting but not an object of analysis. Yet many of the named harms (large PRs defeating review, knowledge transmission to new developers, burnout) are fundamentally social phenomena. What happens to pair-programming culture? To mentorship? To code-review-as-teaching? To the social production of shared mental models when an agent is the third participant? The essay names the symptoms but stays at the level of the individual practitioner.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Pair with John Cutler on team operating systems (the 'institutionalized overload' arc) and with Beck/Cutler on how design discussion functions socially. Also read Pamela Hieronymi or similar on what attention/understanding requires of a community, not just an individual. The user's existing extraction on 'AI Joy Gap Developers' — which treats the polarization as needing organizational structures (AI platform teams, cultural practices reconnecting developers) — is a direct complement to Moe and worth reading together.
Burnout & RecoveryDeveloper Experience & FlowMental Models & Understanding
incomplete
The transmission problem (how does the next generation acquire craft competence?) is named but not analyzed. Moe poses it as a question and entertains the possibility that values and principles might be what survive — but does not engage either the empirical case (how have older crafts handled the loss of hand-skills when tools mediated them? carpentry, surgery, accounting?) or the institutional case (does this require new apprenticeship structures, deliberately preserved code-without-agents zones, deliberate 'fundamentals' periods in a developer's career arc?). This is arguably the most consequential question in the essay and the most underdeveloped.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Richard Sennett, The Craftsman, on the loss and preservation of craft knowledge. Matthew Crawford, Shop Class as Soulcraft, on the cognitive consequences of manual mediation. Atul Gawande on surgical training. On the AI side: the user already has extractions on harness engineering and on 'Agents Don't Know What Good Looks Like' — those directly complement Moe's worry by giving operational shape to what 'knowing what good looks like' has to mean for the senior developer who is supposed to teach it.
Agent Harness
incomplete
What happens when the agent fails you, or when its behavior shifts under you (a model upgrade, a vendor policy change, an outage)? Moe writes from a moment of arrival ('Opus 4.5 is good enough'), but his entire psychological framework presupposes that the agent is a stable enough partner that you can build a working rhythm with it. The risk of dependency on a non-stationary, vendor-controlled colleague is not addressed.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Read alongside the user's existing extractions on 'AI Inference Unit Economics' and 'AI Companies Reckless Not Evil' — those address the supply side of the same dependency. Also worth pairing with anything on platform risk and lock-in in developer tooling.
AI Unit Economics
incomplete
The attention/dopamine critique in the closing section is the largest claim in the essay but the least supported. Moe asserts that twenty years of tech has 'pressed all the wrong buttons' and that generative AI risks being the next instance — but does not show the mechanism by which AI-coding tools specifically would exploit bias, validation-seeking, or dopamine. Agent coding may or may not have a Facebook-Instagram-style attention-economy shape; that requires argument, not assertion. The hidden persona layer — the user's preoccupation with attention and the fragility of ideas (Stolen Focus, Bailey) — is precisely the lens that could either rescue or contest this part of Moe's argument.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Connect to Chris Bailey's Hyperfocus / Stolen Focus tradition (already in the user's highlight-heavy hidden layer), and to specific work on agent-as-companion psychology — does the conversational frame around agents trigger validation-seeking? Is approving an agent's PR a microdopamine event? The user's extractions on 'AI Isn't a Tool Anymore It's a Collaborator' and on AI psychosis are relevant tributaries. This is the most productive open gap in Moe's argument.
Agentic AIAI Employee vs Tool FramingAttention as a Hijackable ResourceEmotional Impact of AI Work
incomplete
No engagement with the counter-thesis that agentic specification is itself a learnable new craft with its own first principles, rather than a hollow shell around an absent practice. Moe treats 'specifying' as essentially less than 'building' — the alienation premise depends on this. But it is at least defensible to argue that designing the specification surface, the test contract, the architectural guardrails, and the review protocol for an agent is a real engineering act that has its own depth. The essay would be stronger if it engaged this counter-position rather than implicitly dismissing it.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Steve Yegge's vibe coding manifesto (which the user is tracking) and Gene Kim's vibe coding series are explicit attempts to defend specification as craft. Read Moe in tension with them, not just alongside. Beck's evolutionary design tradition is also relevant: is the agent a new kind of pair-programmer that requires a new design discipline, or is it the thing that hollowed out the discipline?
Emotional Impact of AI WorkGuardrailsInsourcing & In-House CapabilityVibe Coding
incomplete
Generational and career-stage differences are absent. Moe writes as a senior developer at an established Norwegian banking organization. The piece's emotional grammar — feeling alienated, missing the satisfaction of having built something, worrying about transmission — is the emotional grammar of an experienced practitioner. For a developer who entered the profession in 2024, agent-coding is not a loss but a baseline; the very experience of 'fremmedgjøring' may not arise. Moe's essay is implicitly written from one career stage and would be more powerful if it acknowledged this.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Anything serious on generational cohorts in tech adoption. Look for empirical work on new-developer experience in AI-assisted environments — this is a real research gap right now. The user's interest in the 'Stolen Focus' tradition suggests this is also a place where the digital-native vs digital-immigrant frame might be productively applied to AI-native vs AI-immigrant developers.
AI-Native CompanyAttention as a Hijackable ResourceDeveloper Experience & FlowEmotional Impact of AI Work
incomplete
The Norwegian / Nordic context of the essay is implicit but not theorized. SpareBank 1 Utvikling, the T-bane, Norwegian engineering culture's relative emphasis on 'flyt', autonomi and mestring (drawing on Self-Determination Theory in a way that pervades Nordic management discourse) — all of this shapes Moe's framing. An anglophone reader might miss that 'venting på autocomplete' as a moral category is partly a Norwegian work-culture artifact. There is a comparative essay latent here that the piece does not write.
KI, utvikling og følelser — read next: Read in parallel with anglophone AI-coding voices (Beck's recent essays, Cutler's TBM 412 on institutionalized overload + AI) to see what gets emphasized differently. The NAV 'optimizing for fast flow' piece already in the user's queue is a useful Nordic-context anchor.
Developer Experience & Flow
unstated assumption
The argument assumes that coordination, alignment, and context-sharing across teams are either unnecessary or automatically handled in flat structures. Large-scale organizations face coordination problems that are not solely caused by hierarchy but by the inherent complexity of distributed work.
The founder of Postman says you have to kill your existing org chart — read next: Literature on coordination costs in flat organizations (e.g., Galbraith's information-processing theory of organization design); case studies of flat-org failures at scale.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
missing evidence
No evidence is provided that PMs building APIs or designers shipping PRs produces better outcomes than the prior division of labor. The implicit claim is that directness equals quality, but this conflates shipping speed with product quality.
The founder of Postman says you have to kill your existing org chart — read next: Research on specialization vs. generalization in knowledge work; outcomes data from companies that have adopted these structures.
ambiguity
'Management's job is to develop better judgment' is left undefined. Judgment about what? How is it developed? This is the most provocative redefinition in the thread but receives the least elaboration.
The founder of Postman says you have to kill your existing org chart — read next: Explore what 'judgment' means operationally — cf. Kahneman on decision quality, or strategic management literature on executive cognition.
Taste & Judgment
selection bias
The argument is attributed to the founder of Postman — a developer-tools company where engineering culture is dominant. The prescription may not transfer to organizations where the core work is not software (healthcare, logistics, manufacturing).
The founder of Postman says you have to kill your existing org chart — read next: Compare with AI-era org design thinking in non-tech industries.
unacknowledged tension
The binary 'either you're building, or you're selling' erases important organizational functions: strategy, research, compliance, people development, customer success. These are neither building nor selling but may be essential.
The founder of Postman says you have to kill your existing org chart — read next: Consider which organizational functions are genuinely automatable vs. which are being rhetorically dismissed.
incomplete
Adoption economics of decentralisation are left open. Kleppmann concedes that real decentralisation depends on funded alternative providers actually existing, but never examines why they would be funded or what sustains a commodity, easily-substitutable provider — the very portability that empowers users also destroys the provider's pricing power. Without a viable business model for the exit option, 'agency via guaranteed switching' risks staying hypothetical, as it largely did for email/XMPP federation.
Podcast: Increasing Users' Data Agency: From BlueSky's AT Protocol to the Local-First Software Movement — read next: Two-sided platform and commons-funding economics; the decline of federated protocols (XMPP, RSS) despite openness; Cory Doctorow on interoperability mandates as the missing forcing function.
incomplete
The incentive against open sync protocols is unaddressed. The argument assumes vendors will adopt open, portable sync (like Git's) even though proprietary lock-in is precisely what makes SaaS valuable. No mechanism — market pressure, regulation, a standards body — is offered that would make an incumbent like Google standardise away its own moat, and the Git example itself shows incumbents keep the non-portable parts (GitHub issues, wikis) closed.
Podcast: Increasing Users' Data Agency: From BlueSky's AT Protocol to the Local-First Software Movement — read next: EU Digital Markets Act interoperability provisions; the history of why Google Docs never opened its sync; the switching-cost/moat economics already in this corpus (extract-mellbye-wrapper-vs-infrastructure).
Moats & Defensibility
incomplete
Enterprise, regulatory, and security fit of local-first is barely touched. With the primary copy of data spread across many user devices, questions of enterprise governance, GDPR / data-residency, audit trails, device loss or theft, and access control at scale go largely unexamined — Kleppmann names end-to-end encryption and decentralised access control only as work still in progress. The hard organisational tradeoffs of device-held data are not weighed against the user-agency benefits.
Podcast: Increasing Users' Data Agency: From BlueSky's AT Protocol to the Local-First Software Movement — read next: Data-sovereignty sources in this corpus; EU AI Act / GDPR data-residency obligations; enterprise endpoint-security and MDM literature for the device-loss and access-control problems.
AI Regulation & Governance (External)Local-First Software
incomplete
The hard tradeoffs of CRDTs and local-first are presented as a near-free win. Automerge's costs — metadata/storage overhead, merge semantics that need not match user intent, the loss of a server as an authoritative point for validation and permissions — and Kleppmann's own admissions that peer-to-peer is 'really hard' and that encryption/access control are unfinished, are not balanced against the offline/speed/version-control benefits.
Podcast: Increasing Users' Data Agency: From BlueSky's AT Protocol to the Local-First Software Movement — read next: Ink & Switch's original local-first essay on the 'seven ideals' and their unmet challenges; CRDT literature on garbage collection, storage overhead, and interleaving anomalies.
Local-First Software
incomplete
Who actually benefits and who bears the cost is not asked. The framing centres user empowerment, but shifting storage, compute, conflict-resolution, and support burdens onto clients and app developers — while removing the server's authoritative control — may not translate into real agency for a median, non-technical user. 'Agency' that is only exercisable by switching providers or self-hosting may be agency mostly for the already-technical.
Podcast: Increasing Users' Data Agency: From BlueSky's AT Protocol to the Local-First Software Movement — read next: Hirschman's 'Exit, Voice, and Loyalty' on the limits of exit-as-empowerment; empirical uptake of GDPR Article 20 data-portability rights; digital-literacy research on who can actually exercise portability.
incompleteclosed
No discussion of failure modes, guardrails, or what goes wrong. The post presents a success narrative without acknowledging when agents produce bad code, misinterpret stakeholder requests, or make incorrect self-modifications. The 'staying in control of architecture and quality' caveat is asserted but not unpacked.
Henrik Kniberg's Post — read next: Look for post-mortems or critical accounts of multi-agent workflows in production. Also: research on AI agent reliability and oversight patterns.
✓ closed byThe Orchestration Tax is You — Supplies the failure modes and guardrails Kniberg's success narrative omits: shallow reviews, cognitive surrender, technical and cognitive debt, and sizing the agent fleet to human review capacity (p6-p9, p14).

The self-improvement claim (Grace editing the platform she runs on) is extraordinary but unsupported. What is the scope of changes? What approval process exists? Has it ever broken things? This is the most interesting claim in the post and the least examined.
Henrik Kniberg's Post — read next: Literature on recursive self-improvement in AI systems; also practical accounts of agents modifying their own codebases (e.g., Devin, SWE-bench results).

incomplete
Generalizability: Abundly is a small AI-native startup building an agent platform. Their incentives, team size, codebase complexity, and regulatory environment are unusual. No acknowledgment that this setup might not transfer to larger, more constrained organizations.
Henrik Kniberg's Post — read next: Case studies of multi-agent adoption in larger organizations; enterprise AI adoption literature.

uninformedclosed
No mention of the coordination costs and emergent failures that arise in multi-agent systems. The agents are presented as cleanly separated, but real-world multi-agent systems face issues with conflicting actions, race conditions, and cascading errors.
Henrik Kniberg's Post — read next: Multi-agent systems research; distributed systems failure literature applied to AI agent orchestration.
✓ closed byThe Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — Supplies the missing multi-agent failure-mode discussion: memory drift and cross-workflow data leakage, LLM-shortcut coordination, and cascade failures from specialist deployments, with mitigations (p9, p13, p14).
