unaddressed
The newsletter is a curated summary of a podcast interview, meaning the extraction is working from a secondary source. Nuance, caveats, and context from the original conversation may be lost in summarization.
This week on How I AI: The internal AI tool that's transforming how Stripe designs products (Protodash at Stripe)
incomplete
The article assumes AI is genuinely complementary to employee roles and that psychological debt is therefore a 'cost worth mitigating.' It does not engage the harder case: roles where AI is genuinely substitutive and where the psychological responses (autonomy loss, identity threat) are accurate perceptions of real displacement, not 'debt' to be paid down. The mitigation strategies — reframing, identity-affirming language — risk being euphemistic in those cases.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Acemoglu and Restrepo on task displacement; Daniel Susskind's 'A World Without Work'; the substitution-vs-complementation literature in labor economics
Professional IdentityPsychological Debt
incomplete
Causal direction is never established. Heavy AI users have lower psychological debt — but this could mean (a) using AI more reduces debt, (b) lower-debt employees use AI more, (c) both share an unobserved cause (e.g., role design, manager support). The training-program recommendation only makes sense under (a), which the data does not establish.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Longitudinal or quasi-experimental studies of AI adoption (e.g., the Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond customer-service study); methodology critiques of HR-survey-based causal claims
Enterprise AI Adoption & ReadinessPsychological Debt
incomplete
The exemplar companies are all success stories with no failure cases. Klarna in particular publicly reversed parts of its AI-first strategy in 2024-2025 after customer service quality concerns; the article treats the 90% adoption number uncritically as proof of working culture rather than as a snapshot that didn't hold. Selection bias on the prescription side is severe.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Follow-up coverage of Klarna's AI strategy reversal; broader research on the gap between announced and sustained AI adoption rates
Enterprise AI Adoption & Readiness
uninformed
The six debts are presented as independent dimensions but are conceptually entangled. Competency debt is partly a felt consequence of cognitive debt; identity debt overlaps with autonomy debt when the threatened identity is built on autonomous expert judgment. The taxonomy may be a useful management heuristic but is not analytically clean, and the survey's 0–100 conversion may double-count.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: The original Self-Determination Theory literature (Ryan & Deci) on how the basic needs interact rather than sum; latent-variable analyses of motivation constructs
Cognitive DebtPsychological Debt
uninformed
No engagement with the asymmetry the survey itself surfaces: people excuse their own AI use but judge others'. This is a textbook 'fundamental attribution error' / actor-observer asymmetry, and the credibility-debt prescription (make use visible) assumes a social-norms mechanism that the underlying psychology may resist. The author does not engage with whether visibility actually shifts the attribution or just relocates it.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Classic social psychology on actor-observer asymmetry (Jones & Nisbett); norm-shifting interventions in moral psychology
incomplete
Conflict of interest is disclosed but its implications are unexamined. The survey was commissioned for Meltwater Consulting (where the author is a director) and underwrites a consulting offer. The framing — that companies need expertly-designed adoption strategies — directly supports the consulting product. The article treats the data as objective evidence rather than as evidence shaped by a commercial frame.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Methodology of practitioner-commissioned research; meta-analyses comparing commissioned to independent organizational research
incomplete
The 'human infrastructure' closing claim is large but underspecified. Each prescription is a single-company anecdote; there is no theory of which mitigation pattern applies to which combination of debts, no sequencing guidance, and no analysis of how the patterns interact (e.g., does cognitive friction reduce or increase autonomy debt?). The article ends as a framework prospectus rather than as a developed framework.
The Psychological Costs of Adopting AI — read next: Work on socio-technical systems design (Trist, Pasmore); change-management research on layered intervention design
Cognitive SurrenderPsychological DebtSociotechnical Architecture
incomplete
The 'don't talk to customers' thesis ignores the distinction between different kinds of customer input: usability testing, behavioral data, Jobs-to-be-Done interviews, and direct feature requests are very different activities with different failure modes. Rabois appears to collapse them all into one category.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures) — read next: Teresa Torres's 'Continuous Discovery Habits' for a rigorous framework on which kinds of customer input are valuable and when; Clayton Christensen's Jobs-to-be-Done theory for a middle path between 'ignore customers' and 'build what they ask for.'
incomplete
The PM-is-dying thesis lacks a theory of what replaces PM functions beyond spec-writing: prioritization across competing initiatives, stakeholder alignment, go-to-market coordination, and cross-team dependency management. If AI handles translation, who handles these?
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures) — read next: Shreyas Doshi's writing on PM archetypes and which PM functions are strategic vs. tactical; Marty Cagan's 'Transformed' for a model of empowered product teams that might survive AI compression.
incomplete
The barrels framework assumes barrels are born, not made. There is no discussion of how to develop barrels, whether barrel-ness is domain-specific, or how the framework applies when AI itself can become a barrel (autonomous agents shipping end-to-end).
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures) — read next: Cedric Chin's writing on expertise development and deliberate practice for whether 'barrel' capability is developable; research on autonomous AI agents for whether AI will eventually replace barrels too.
Agentic AITalent Density
uninformed
The claim that CMOs will be the #1 token consumers does not engage with the possibility that AI agents (not humans) will be the primary consumers of tokens — where the distinction between 'marketing tokens' and 'engineering tokens' becomes meaningless because agents operate across functions.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures) — read next: Anthropic's and OpenAI's writing on agentic workflows; the emerging literature on AI-to-AI communication and multi-agent systems.
Agentic AIAgentic LoopMulti-Agent Orchestration
incomplete
The claim that psychological safety is negatively correlated with success is stated as a blanket assertion without evidence beyond a sports analogy (Jordan Rules). No engagement with the substantial research literature on psychological safety in knowledge work, nor acknowledgment that survivorship bias may be at play (we see the winning teams that lacked it, not the many that failed).
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
Psychological Safety
incomplete
The anti-customer-feedback position is argued entirely through consumer examples (Porsche, Airbnb, DoorDash) but then extended to SMB without distinguishing where on the consumer-to-enterprise spectrum the advice flips. The Porsche example proves that stated preferences diverge from revealed preferences, not that all consumer feedback is harmful.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
illogical
The argument that public criticism optimizes for the system while private criticism optimizes for the individual assumes a binary choice. It does not address whether the information benefits of public criticism could be achieved through other means (e.g., transparent issue tracking, team retrospectives) without the potential costs of public shaming.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
incomplete
The 'barrels and ammunition' framework treats barrel capability as largely innate and binary — you either are one or you aren't. The smoothie test story reinforces this (an intern on day two is a barrel). There is limited discussion of how barrels are developed, whether ammunition can become barrels, or what organizational conditions produce more barrels.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
Talent Density
uninformed
The claim that AI writing quality degrades with length due to 'token rationing' by LLMs optimizing economics is presented as a confident theory but reflects a misunderstanding of how inference works. Output quality degradation with length has more to do with attention mechanisms, context window limitations, and the difficulty of maintaining coherence over long sequences than with deliberate economic throttling.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
Usage Limits & Rationing
incomplete
The advice to skip senior hires and develop talent internally is supported by examples from companies that had extraordinary founding teams (Ramp, Trade Republic). The selection bias is unaddressed: these companies may have been able to develop internal talent precisely because their initial talent density was exceptional. The advice may not generalize to companies with weaker founding teams.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
Talent Density
incomplete
The existential question about whether foundation model labs will absorb all value from application-layer AI startups is raised but never addressed. Given that Rabois is actively investing in AI companies, this is a significant gap — he identifies the question but offers no framework for how startups should think about durability against improving foundation models.
Hard truths about building in the AI era | Keith Rabois (Khosla Ventures)
unspecified
The essay raises alignment and verification as 'very hard problems' but offers no concrete approaches, frameworks, or even directions for solving them — the safety discussion is entirely gestural
FOD#151: Recursive Self-Learning: Why It Matters Now
AI Alignment & Safety
unspecified
Clark's 60%+ probability claim for no-human-involved AI R&D by 2028 is presented without methodological backing — it is a forecast from authority, not an argued probability
FOD#151: Recursive Self-Learning: Why It Matters Now
Recursive Self-Improvement
unspecified
The essay does not distinguish between automating incremental improvements (hyperparameter tuning, kernel optimization) and automating genuinely novel research directions — these are qualitatively different challenges lumped together under 'RSL'
FOD#151: Recursive Self-Learning: Why It Matters Now
unspecified
No discussion of failure modes, diminishing returns, or reasons RSL might plateau — the narrative is monotonically progressive
FOD#151: Recursive Self-Learning: Why It Matters Now
unspecified
The competitive dynamics driving RSL (lab racing incentives, geopolitical pressure) are not examined, despite being arguably the primary force accelerating deployment ahead of safety work
FOD#151: Recursive Self-Learning: Why It Matters Now
incomplete
The article doesn't address how the agent decides whether it needs a research phase. The Liquid case didn't need one; the llama.cpp case did. But the agent couldn't have known this in advance. A decision heuristic ('when should I read before coding?') is the missing meta-skill that would make this approach self-directing rather than human-configured.
Research-Driven Agents: What Happens When Your Agent Reads Before It Codes — read next: Metacognitive agent architectures; 'knowing what you don't know' in AI systems; confidence calibration research
Meta-Skills & Contextual Judgment
incomplete
The claim that well-optimized codebases (Redis, PostgreSQL, SQLite) are harder targets is mentioned in one sentence but not explored. This is actually the most interesting boundary: at what point has a codebase been optimized enough that agent-driven improvement becomes uneconomical? This would define the useful scope of the entire approach.
Research-Driven Agents: What Happens When Your Agent Reads Before It Codes — read next: Performance engineering literature on diminishing returns; the 'last mile' problem in optimization
incomplete
The research phase is described as reading papers and studying forks, but the article doesn't discuss how the agent evaluates which papers and forks are relevant. With thousands of arxiv papers and potentially dozens of forks, the agent needs a research strategy — and that strategy itself requires domain knowledge.
Research-Driven Agents: What Happens When Your Agent Reads Before It Codes — read next: Information retrieval for technical literature; RAG architectures for agent research; the 'research taste' problem
Taste & Judgment
incomplete
The article is honest about agent overconfidence (the benchmark bug) but doesn't propose a solution. How should agents sanity-check their own instrumentation? This is a general agent reliability problem that extends far beyond optimization benchmarks.
Research-Driven Agents: What Happens When Your Agent Reads Before It Codes — read next: Agent self-verification; Anthropic's work on AI oversight; the 'quis custodiet' problem in autonomous systems
Agentic AIEvals
missing evidence
No discussion of whether NVIDIA has issued or plans mitigations (firmware, driver, or hardware). The post reports the vulnerability but not the vendor response.
Rowhammer Attack Against NVIDIA Chips — read next: NVIDIA security bulletins and the researchers' responsible disclosure timelines.
AI-Driven Vulnerability Discovery
unasked question
No analysis of which real-world threat scenarios make this exploitable in practice. Cloud multi-tenancy (shared GPUs), browser WebGPU, and ML inference services all have different exposure profiles, but none are discussed.
Rowhammer Attack Against NVIDIA Chips — read next: Research on GPU sharing in cloud environments (MIG, vGPU) and whether they partition DRAM sufficiently to prevent cross-tenant rowhammer.
scope limitation
No mention of whether non-NVIDIA GPUs (AMD, Intel Arc) share the vulnerability. GDDR6 is used across vendors, so the rowhammer physics may generalize.
Rowhammer Attack Against NVIDIA Chips — read next: The GDDRHammer and GeForge papers' discussion sections on vendor generalizability.
unasked question
The post does not address whether newer NVIDIA architectures (Hopper, Blackwell) using HBM instead of GDDR6 are also affected, or whether HBM's different physical structure provides natural mitigation.
Rowhammer Attack Against NVIDIA Chips — read next: HBM rowhammer research and architecture comparisons between GDDR6 and HBM3.
missing evidence
No discussion of detection: can a defender observe GPU rowhammer attempts in progress? CPU rowhammer has performance-counter-based detection methods; it is unknown whether analogous GPU telemetry exists.
Rowhammer Attack Against NVIDIA Chips — read next: GPU performance monitoring literature and existing rowhammer detection mechanisms on CPUs.
incomplete
No treatment of model risk management for the predictive model itself. If EPSS or a local knowing machine becomes the action-trigger, it inherits MRM scrutiny (drift, bias, calibration, explainability). The author critiques CVSS compliance inertia but does not engage the parallel inertia that 'models making security decisions' will trigger inside the same MRM/audit teams he's asking CISOs to renegotiate with.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: FIRST EPSS calibration studies; SR 11-7 / OCC model-risk frameworks applied to security tooling; recent work on operationalizing AI governance for cyber decisions (NIST AI RMF crosswalks with vulnerability management).
AI Regulation & Governance (External)AI-Driven Vulnerability Discovery
incomplete
Asymmetric-advantage claim under-defended. Local context is asserted as defenders' 'perhaps the only' asymmetric advantage, but attackers routinely acquire defender-side context via reconnaissance, insiders, prior compromises, and now AI-assisted OSINT. The argument needs a falsifiability condition: under what compromise of defender context does the predictive advantage collapse?
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: Threat-intel literature on adversary recon; red-team write-ups on environment learning; emerging work on AI-augmented OSINT against enterprise estates.
incomplete
No engagement with the political economy of the scanner/RBVM vendor base. 120+ vendors embed EPSS but largely sell on finding-volume. The piece prescribes a shift that undercuts their current product KPIs without naming that incentive conflict or how a CISO survives a renewal negotiation under the new metric.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: RBVM vendor positioning analyses; Gartner/IDC RBVM market notes 2024-2026; CISO procurement post-mortems.
incomplete
Mythos and the '10x findings in 24 months' projection are presented as forcing function but uncited; treated as known. For a piece whose authority move is 'a frontier lab said X', the empirical claim about future discovery rates deserves either Anthropic's source or independent grounding.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: The cited Anthropic April 2026 security-ops guide in full; recent AI-discovered vulnerability disclosure datasets; CISA KEV growth-rate analyses.
Mythos
incomplete
Local knowing machine implementation is described at the inputs layer (CMDB, telemetry, controls) but not at the modeling layer. What kind of model? Trained how, evaluated how, retrained on what cadence? The piece tells CISOs to build something it does not specify enough to procure or staff against, beyond hiring a 'security-data engineer'.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: Academic literature on enterprise-local exploit prediction; case studies from organizations (Kenna/Cisco, ZeroFox, etc.) that have shipped contextualized scoring; MLOps patterns for security telemetry.
AI-Driven Vulnerability Discovery
illogical
Mild tension: the piece both leans on Anthropic's authority to dislodge CVSS and warns that LLM detection without local prioritization is net-negative — yet does not address that Anthropic is simultaneously the producer of the LLM bug-finder (Mythos) it is warning about. The recommendation is treated as disinterested expertise when it could equally be read as supplier-side risk mitigation.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: Conflict-of-interest analyses of frontier-lab safety/security guidance; comparison with cloud-provider 'shared responsibility' framings that similarly externalize risk to customers.
AI-Driven Vulnerability DiscoveryMythos
incomplete
Public-sector and small-org perspective absent. The prescriptions assume a CISO with CMDBs, MRM teams, board reporting cadences, and audit relationships. The 80% of organizations without those structures — and which still face Mythos-class discovery volume via shared CVEs — get no treatment.
Predict, Don't Enumerate — read next: CISA small-business cybersecurity guidance; municipal/SLED security maturity studies; managed-defense economics for under-resourced orgs.
Mythos
unstated_assumption
The interview assumes that Uber's survival-mode engineering culture was net positive, but does not examine the human cost (burnout, 'people went to the beach and stared at water for a month' after China launch). The sustainability of this approach is not questioned.
Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) — read next: Examine research on sustainable pace in engineering organizations; compare with less intense scaling stories (e.g., Stripe, Shopify) that grew large without the same crisis-driven culture.
Burnout & Recovery
missing_perspective
The narrative is told entirely from the CTO's vantage point. The experience of mid-level and junior engineers living through constant rewrites, impossible deadlines, and organizational churn is not represented except as positive growth.
Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) — read next: Seek accounts from IC engineers who worked at Uber during 2013-2017; Gergely Orosz's own newsletter has some of these but they are not surfaced here.
unexamined_tension
Thuan advocates for both 'redlining yourself' (pushing past limits) and humility/perspective-of-death (don't take yourself too seriously). How these two values interact — especially when the 'redlining' culture contributes to the very organizational crises (2017 turbulence) he later had to stabilize — is not explored.
Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) — read next: Literature on organizational culture tensions: Edgar Schein's work on espoused values vs. enacted values; research on high-performance cultures that avoid toxicity.
survivorship_bias
The framing 'we did the impossible and it worked' does not account for the many startups that adopted similar crisis-driven engineering and failed. Uber's extreme approach worked partly because Uber succeeded as a business — the engineering culture was necessary but not sufficient.
Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) — read next: Case studies of companies with similar engineering intensity that did not succeed (e.g., WeWork's engineering org, Theranos's move-fast culture).
underdeveloped_topic
The 2017 turbulence (Travis's departure, leadership crisis) is mentioned but deliberately not explored. This is arguably the most consequential organizational event in Uber's history and Thuan was at the center of it.
Scaling Uber with Thuan Pham (Uber's first CTO) — read next: Mike Isaac's 'Super Pumped'; public reporting on Uber's 2017 leadership crisis.
incomplete
Schneier locates the failure in the LLM ('not trustworthy enough') without separating three plausible loci: (a) the LLM's susceptibility to natural-language manipulation, (b) the workflow design that gives the bot tool-access to mutate the email on file and trigger a password reset, and (c) the underlying identity system that treats 'received a code at the email I just added' as proof of ownership. The same incident is consistent with any of the three being primary — and the right remediation differs sharply: refusal-train the model vs. revoke the bot's tool scope vs. require an independent factor anchored to the pre-existing identity (old email, registered device, hardware key).
Hacking Meta's AI Chatbot — read next: Read alongside agent-governance work on tool-access scoping (least-privilege for LLM tool calls), and on identity-proofing standards (NIST 800-63 IAL/AAL) to separate 'the bot got fooled' from 'the workflow accepted a code-to-attacker-controlled-channel as proof of ownership.' For Norwegian-context relevance: NSM/Nasjonal sikkerhetsmyndighet guidance on account-recovery and the trend toward eID-anchored re-authentication offer a contrasting model where no support channel — human or AI — can mutate identity-bearing attributes without an independent strong-auth event.
incomplete
No comparison to the human-agent baseline. Human support agents have been social-engineered into the same outcome for two decades (SIM swaps, telecom account takeovers, Twitter 2020). The interesting question is whether LLMs are worse on the margin, equally bad, or simply scale the same failure to higher throughput at lower cost — which changes the threat model (volume, automation, no fatigue) without changing the qualitative vulnerability.
Hacking Meta's AI Chatbot — read next: Compare with telecom SIM-swap case studies and prior Twitter/Coinbase social-engineering postmortems to assess whether 'LLM chatbots are not trustworthy enough' should generalize to 'human-mediated account recovery is not trustworthy enough.'
Agentic AI
incompleteclosed
'Cannot be blocked as a class' is asserted, not argued. The post does not engage with structured-output / tool-mediation designs where the LLM proposes actions but a deterministic policy engine enforces invariants (e.g., 'no email-on-file mutation without prior strong-auth') — which would be a class-level defense regardless of what the LLM is convinced to say.
Hacking Meta's AI Chatbot — read next: Read agent-governance literature on policy-engine-wrapped LLM tool calls and on capability-scoping (e.g., Anthropic / OpenAI tool-use safety patterns) to test whether class-level defenses are genuinely unavailable or merely absent from Meta's deployment.
✓ closed byRunning Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — Tests the 'no class-level defense' assertion: cutting the exfiltration leg and enforcing safety at the execution boundary are class-level architectural defenses that work regardless of what the LLM is convinced to say.
Policy-as-Code
incomplete
The essay prescribes 'put the old discipline back' but does not address why those disciplines eroded in the first place or what specific mechanisms (governance structures, tooling, ceremonies) make them stick in an AI-accelerated context. If the old discipline was sufficient, it would not have been so easily displaced.
Meet the Scope Creep Kraken
uninformed
No distinction is drawn between scope creep in exploratory/prototyping phases (where rapid expansion may be desirable) vs. committed delivery phases (where it is destructive). The Kraken metaphor treats all unplanned expansion as harmful.
Meet the Scope Creep Kraken
Scope Discipline
incomplete
The essay does not address the cost of the friction it mourns. The old staffing-constraint model also prevented valuable features from being built. The analysis is one-sided: it treats lost friction purely as lost governance, not as lost waste.
Meet the Scope Creep Kraken
illogical
The essay implicitly assumes that the integration cost of AI-generated features is comparable to that of human-generated features. If AI can also assist with integration, testing, and documentation, the cost calculus may differ from what the argument assumes.
Meet the Scope Creep Kraken
Technical Debt
incomplete
Mallaby's open-source prohibition argument has a critical counterfactual gap: the Mexico case demonstrates that closed-API models can be shut down, but the attacker could have used an open-weight model instead. The argument assumes that closed APIs are the only available alternative, which may not hold as open-weight models proliferate. Mallaby acknowledges that powerful open-weight models 'circulate already' but doesn't resolve this.
Sebastian Mallaby on AI Safety and the Race for Superintelligence — read next: Research on whether capability differences between frontier closed and open-weight models are large enough to matter for attack scenarios; Stanford HAI's AI Index on capability gaps
Open-Weight Models
incomplete
Mallaby endorses CERN-model governance and notes that US cooperation requires a new administration, but provides no mechanism for bridging the gap. The proposal assumes governance is intellectually solved and politically delayed — but the enforcement mechanism (how do you ensure Chinese labs comply?) is not examined.
Sebastian Mallaby on AI Safety and the Race for Superintelligence — read next: Arms control literature on verification mechanisms; IAEA safeguards as model; Dafoe's 'AI Governance: A Research Agenda'
AI Regulation & Governance (External)
uninformed
Mallaby doesn't engage with the critique that 'AI safety' discourse itself may serve corporate interests — as a way to delay open regulation (safety research as a substitute for binding rules), generate credibility for incumbent labs, and frame existential risk in ways that crowd out more immediate harms. This critique is prominent in AI ethics literature and among researchers like Timnit Gebru.
Sebastian Mallaby on AI Safety and the Race for Superintelligence — read next: Gebru et al.'s 'Stochastic Parrots'; Bender's work on framing; Whittaker's 'The Steep Cost of Capture'
incomplete
The interview is a podcast transcript — the dialogue form allows Mounk to push back but not to synthesize. Several threads are opened and not closed: the precise mechanism of the alignment tax; whether Chinese political safety and alignment safety are actually converging or just using similar words; what 'frontier model' means precisely for open-source prohibition purposes.
Sebastian Mallaby on AI Safety and the Race for Superintelligence — read next: Mallaby's book 'The Infinity Machine' on DeepMind presumably develops these arguments more carefully
AI Alignment & SafetyRiding Model Improvements
illogical
The 'Three Mile Island' analogy implies that a catastrophic AI incident will produce useful regulation. But Three Mile Island produced nuclear regulation in part because the harm was localized and attributable. A rolling infrastructure attack by an open-weight model — Mallaby's nightmare scenario — might not be attributable in the way necessary to drive political response. The analogy may not transfer.
Sebastian Mallaby on AI Safety and the Race for Superintelligence — read next: Perrow's 'Normal Accidents' on how catastrophes produce (and fail to produce) regulatory change; March, Sproull & Tamuz on learning from near-failures
Open-Weight Models
incomplete
No quantitative evidence for 'extreme ROI' — no metrics, time savings, error rates, or before/after comparisons are provided for any of the four use cases.
Simon Sebergsen's Post
incomplete
No discussion of risks, failure modes, or limitations — hallucination risk in financial analysis, regulatory compliance concerns, or quality control of AI-generated IC memos and investor communications.
Simon Sebergsen's Post
incomplete
The Bloomberg-to-Claude-Code analogy is asserted but not developed. Bloomberg is a data terminal; Claude Code is a generative AI agent. The shared property ('terminal interface') is superficial.
Simon Sebergsen's Post
Agentic AIClaude Code
potential_bias
The author sells Claude Code bootcamps and consulting. Every claim in the post also functions as marketing for his services. The growing-demand signal (bootcamp sign-ups) is self-referential.

Claude Code
incomplete
The 'financial-services-plugins' package from Anthropic is mentioned as a tip but not explained — what it contains, how it relates to the use cases described, or whether it was used in the client work.
Simon Sebergsen's Post
evidential
The article does not describe what the specific disputed point in IUT actually is, even at a high level. The reader cannot assess the nature of the obstacle.
The secret project to settle controversial maths proof with a computer — read next: Read Scholze and Stix's 2018 report 'Why abc is still a conjecture' for details on the contested Corollary 3.12.
perspectival
No voice from a mathematician who believes formalisation will fail or is the wrong approach. The article presents only optimistic assessments.
The secret project to settle controversial maths proof with a computer — read next: Seek critical perspectives on whether IUT's novel category-theoretic constructions can even be expressed in Lean's type theory.
Lean & Toyota Way
implicit_premise
The article assumes that a machine-verified proof would end the social controversy, but it is possible that disagreement could shift to whether the formalisation faithfully captures IUT's intended meaning.
The secret project to settle controversial maths proof with a computer — read next: Consider philosophical literature on the relationship between formal and informal proof (e.g., Avigad, Azzouni on formalisation and mathematical practice).
temporal
No timeline or estimate is given for how long full formalisation might take. Major formalisation projects (e.g., Liquid Tensor Experiment) have taken years for far shorter proofs.
The secret project to settle controversial maths proof with a computer — read next: Compare with timelines of completed Lean formalisation projects (Liquid Tensor Experiment, Sphere Eversion, FLT-regular).
Lean & Toyota Way
incompleteclosed
No mechanism is offered for the redistribution problem Wardley calls 'the main problem in this space'. The post diagnoses inequality precisely and then walks away from the prescription.
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read alongside political-economy work on technology and redistribution — Mariana Mazzucato on entrepreneurial state, Daron Acemoglu's recent work on AI and labour share, Carlota Perez on technological surges and golden-age policy regimes. Also Frederiksen's Coop Bank piece for the sovereignty/redistribution angle at the firm and national level.
✓ closed byThe Tidy House — Supplies the missing prescription: concrete value-distributing mechanisms (subsidized tokens for community work, ContentID-style revenue splits) that labs could build for the redistribution problem Wardley diagnoses but leaves without a mechanism.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The 'chain of understanding' is named as the new bottleneck but not operationalised. What tools, practices, or organisational shapes produce or sustain comprehension at billion-LoC scale?
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read against the harness-engineering corpus you have this session (Anthropic harness paper, Cloudflare Mythos, Pocock, smallcode). Bernhard's value-stream framing is a candidate operationalisation: comprehension as a stream-level property, not an individual one. Also Donella Meadows on bounded rationality and information delays — the chain of understanding is a feedback-loop integrity problem.
Cognitive DebtMythos
illogical
The China-dominance argument compresses 'supply chain understanding' into a near-sufficient condition for long-run dominance. Other variables (capital allocation efficiency, demographics, talent migration, capability of domestic models, export-control regimes, energy supply, civil-military fusion costs) are not weighed.
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read against Dan Wang on Chinese tech policy, CSIS/RAND supply-chain analyses, and any sceptical takes on PRC industrial policy execution (e.g. semiconductor self-sufficiency progress vs targets).
incomplete
The Jevons-paradox reframing (adjacent possibles becoming real) is asserted but the conditions under which adjacent possibles actually become real industries — vs stalling — are not specified. Sometimes the adjacent possible is real and stays small.
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read against the slower-than-expected industrialisation histories — early electricity (the 40-year productivity paradox), enterprise software in the 1990s. Beck and Shoup on Jevons-in-coding is a sharper instantiation of this same claim worth juxtaposing.
uninformed (mild)
The post under-weights regulatory ratchets — particularly EU AI Act, sector-specific liability regimes, GDPR-style data sovereignty rules — as climatic variables that shape what 'industrialised ML' actually looks like in different jurisdictions. Wardley's mapping treats regulation as doctrine; this post treats it as background.
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read alongside EU AI Act implementing acts, NSM/Datatilsynet guidance for the Norwegian context, and Frederiksen on Coop Bank sovereignty for how an enterprise actually reasons about regulatory ratchets as architectural constraint.
AI Regulation & Governance (External)Data Sovereignty
incomplete
The post is silent on the cultural and organisational variation in how 'practice converges' over the 5-8 year window. Nordic public-sector institutions, Japanese manufacturers, and Silicon Valley startups will not converge to the same practice — but the post talks as if there is one convergence trajectory.
Simon Wardley's Post — Industrialisation of ML: nine notes on what to expect — read next: Read against Cutler on organisational operating systems and the Heis stable's Norwegian-leader interviews — concrete evidence that practice-convergence is mediated by national culture and institutional shape, not just by elapsed years.
missing evidence
The exact prioritization formula is never disclosed. We learn the inputs (seven impact factors, urgency, confidence, effort) and that weights are tunable, but the mathematical relationship between these variables is not shared.
Assumptions as code: SiriusXM's approach to platform prioritization — read next: Look for SiriusXM conference talks or blog posts that may publish the formula; also consider whether the formula matters less than the conversation process it enables.
unexamined assumption
The framework assumes that leadership can meaningfully assign numeric weights to impact factors. Whether leaders actually have the information and cognitive capacity to set accurate weights -- or whether the weights merely formalize existing biases -- is not examined.
Assumptions as code: SiriusXM's approach to platform prioritization — read next: Research on calibration in expert judgment (e.g., Tetlock's superforecasting work) and whether structured elicitation improves or merely formalizes biases.
Decision-Making Practices
missing evidence
The assumptions-as-code system is described conceptually and architecturally (Git backend, AI surfacing, three modes) but no concrete examples of stored assumptions, their schema, or real validation outcomes are shown.
Assumptions as code: SiriusXM's approach to platform prioritization — read next: Look for open-source implementations or detailed case studies of the assumptions repository; consider building a prototype to test the concept.
scope limitation
The discussion focuses on prioritization within platform engineering but does not address how this framework interacts with prioritization in the product engineering teams that platform engineering serves. Cross-org priority conflicts are mentioned but not deeply explored.
Assumptions as code: SiriusXM's approach to platform prioritization — read next: Explore how platform engineering priorities can be formally linked to product engineering priorities, possibly through shared impact factors or dependency mapping.
Platform Engineering
unexamined assumption
The 'builder persona' argument relies heavily on GitHub's 25% YoY user growth as evidence of non-developer adoption. This statistic could have many explanations (more professional developers, more student accounts, more bot accounts) and is not validated.
Assumptions as code: SiriusXM's approach to platform prioritization — read next: Examine GitHub's actual user demographics data; look for stronger evidence of non-developer builder growth in enterprise settings.
Non-Engineers Building
incomplete
The central benefit — reduced brittle-test maintenance and improved resilience — is asserted with zero quantification. There is no flake-rate before/after, no measure of how often the agent successfully adapts versus how often it produces a wrong or non-reproducible result, and no comparison of maintenance effort against scripted tests.
Slack Introduces Agent Driven End-to-End Testing to Improve Resilience in UI Test Automation — read next: Read the linked Slack engineering post directly for any concrete metrics; compare with evaluation work that measures agentic vs scripted E2E flake and maintenance rates. Absent numbers, treat the resilience claim as a hypothesis.
EvalsMeasuring AI Impact
incomplete
The load-bearing risk is never acknowledged: a self-healing agent that 'attempts alternate paths' around a change could mask a genuine functional regression (a false negative), turning a should-fail test into a pass. The approach presumes the agent can distinguish a cosmetic UI change from a real defect, but neither the mechanism nor any bound on what the agent may adapt around is given.
Slack Introduces Agent Driven End-to-End Testing to Improve Resilience in UI Test Automation — read next: Read against extract-what-survived-contact#p5 (self-healing has moved only halfway because unsupervised remediation hands the machine the blast radius) and llm-limitations (non-determinism, silent corruption). Look for how the exploration/stop constraints and human assertions are designed to catch, not hide, real regressions.
GuardrailsLLM Limitations
incomplete
The cost claim that gates CI use is stated without any figures — no cost-per-run, no comparison to the cost of a deterministic test run, and no threshold at which the economics would flip and make CI execution viable.
Slack Introduces Agent Driven End-to-End Testing to Improve Resilience in UI Test Automation — read next: Read alongside ai-unit-economics and token-cost analyses; check the Slack post for actual per-run costs or budgets. Consider model-routing (cheaper models for exploration) as a possible lever the piece omits.
AI Unit EconomicsModel Routing
incomplete
The headline '87% benchmark with 4B-active model' is the entire credibility hook and is completely unspecified in the body. No benchmark name (SWE-Bench? Aider polyglot? HumanEval? a custom suite?), no subset, no scoring methodology, no comparison baseline (what does a frontier model score on this same eval? what does the same 4B model score without SmallCode?), no link to results. For a rigorous adopter this is the single most disqualifying gap.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Check the GitHub repo itself for an evals/ or results/ directory; check the project's `/eval` command output; check whether any third party has reproduced. Compare against published SWE-Bench / Aider / HumanEval+ leaderboards for similar-sized models. Look for the actual model named in the example (gemma-4-e4b) and find its baseline scores.
EvalsRiding Model Improvements
incomplete
No comparison to other small-model-aware agentic coding tools — Aider (with local models), Cline + Ollama, Continue, OpenHands with local backends, Roo. The only named comparator is OpenCode, which is set up as a strawman ('assumes frontier models').
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Read Aider's local-model documentation and benchmark notes (Paul Gauthier publishes Aider polyglot scores for many models). Compare with Cline's local-model guidance and OpenHands' SWE-Bench results with open models.
Agent Tool ManagementLocal AIOpen-Weight ModelsRiding Model Improvements
incompleteclosed
No failure-mode analysis: what *can't* this do? Where does the harness break? At what task complexity does the small model fall off? The README enumerates failure modes the harness handles, but not failure modes it doesn't.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Look for issue tracker discussions, particularly around long-horizon tasks, large refactors, and tasks requiring deep type-system reasoning. Run the agent on a known-hard task (e.g. a non-trivial SWE-Bench instance) and observe.
✓ closed byExperiences with local models for coding — Partially. SmallCode's g3 asks the general question of at what task complexity a small model falls off and where the harness breaks. Böckeler supplies concrete, hands-on failure boundaries — cumulative/derived computations, continued-conversation runtime crashes, the 'text wall of doom', and complex game logic falling apart — from OpenCode/Pi/LM Studio setups (not SmallCode's own harness), so it answers the general question, not SmallCode's specifics.
Agent HarnessLong-Running & Managed Agents
incompleteclosed
No discussion of total cost of ownership: hardware to run a 7B-20B model at usable latency (GPU memory, electricity), engineer time to maintain a local model server, escalation cost when small model fails. The privacy/local row in the comparison table treats locality as pure upside.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Map total cost (hardware amortization + electricity + maintenance + escalation API spend) against frontier-API consumption rates for a comparable workload to compute break-even.
✓ closed byExperiences with local models for coding — Partially. SmallCode's g4 flags missing total-cost-of-ownership discussion (hardware, latency, RAM). Böckeler reports concrete hardware (M3 Max 48GB, M5 Pro 64GB), RAM pressure (48GB 'stretches' the 35B model, must close other apps), and latency anecdotes (2.5 min success, ~6 min, 'gave up after 11 minutes'). Still qualitative and specific to her setups rather than SmallCode's, so it narrows but does not close the accounting.
Model Routing
incomplete
One-author ecosystem risk: SmallCode depends on BoneScript, MarrowScript, and budget-aware-mcp — all by the same GitHub user. If the author stops maintaining, four projects rot together. This single-maintainer concentration is not flagged anywhere.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Check commit history and contributor counts on all four repos; check npm download stats for smallcode and budget-aware-mcp; check whether MarrowScript / BoneScript have any external adopters.
misinformed
The escalation target list cites 'Claude Sonnet 4.5 / 4.6, Haiku 4.5' and 'GPT-5.4 Mini / Nano' and 'DeepSeek V4 / V4 Pro / V4 Flash'. As of the document's apparent date, these specific named version numbers may be aspirational, outdated, or fabricated. The presence of confidently-named future/non-existent model versions weakens credibility on other un-checkable claims.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Cross-check the named model versions against vendor product pages; if they are not real, treat the rest of the README's confident numbers (87%, '8-15 → 1-2 tool calls', '1400+ lines from 50 lines') with proportional skepticism.
Model Routing
illogical
The marketing claim ('87% with 4B-active model') and the body text ('7B-20B local models') are inconsistent. The title advertises a 4B-active model; the body describes the project as targeting 7B-20B. If 4B-active means MoE active params with a larger total, both can be true — but the README never reconciles them, leaving the reader to guess.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Identify the actual model used to produce the headline number; if it is MoE (e.g. gemma-4-e4b with ~4B active), check whether 7B-20B framing in the body is meant as total-parameter range, active-parameter range, or both — the project's positioning depends on which.
Local AI
incomplete
No discussion of how the harness itself is engineered, tested, or evaluated. The Observability section mentions traces and `/eval` but provides no example evaluation results, no harness-coverage metrics, no failure-mode regression tests beyond 'trace-to-test'. The harness is presented as fait accompli, not as something the reader could audit.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Connect to Birgitta Böckeler's harness-engineering article (already in the user's library): her steering-loop concept asks how a team iterates the harness based on observed failures. Apply that lens here — what is SmallCode's own steering loop?

incomplete
The cybernetic / control-theory framing latent in the design (variety reduction via BoneScript topologies, feedback via early-stop detection, model profiles as adaptive control) is never made explicit. Connecting this project to the broader harness-engineering conversation (Anthropic's effective harness paper, Böckeler's framework) would let it argue from theory rather than just engineering instinct.
GitHub - Doorman11991/smallcode: AI coding agent optimized for small LLMs. 87% benchmark with 4B-active model. — read next: Read SmallCode against Böckeler's 'Harness engineering for coding agent users' (in this batch) — map SmallCode's components to her feedforward/feedback and computational/inferential axes. Read against the Anthropic harness-design materials the user is also tracking.
Agent HarnessCoding Agents
incomplete
The post identifies the junior training crisis but offers no solution. If simple tasks are the on-ramp and they're disappearing, what replaces them? AI-assisted pair programming? Simulation environments? Apprenticeship models? The post stops at the problem statement.
SMOC.AI's Post — 5 Minutter: AI og utviklere — read next: Addy Osmani on AI-assisted learning; Kent Beck on coaching with AI; literature on medical residency models (which face similar 'hours reduction' training challenges)
Pair & Mob Programming
illogical
The 'AI as junior developer' metaphor contains a self-defeating dynamic the author doesn't notice: if AI eliminates junior training, and seniors are needed to guide AI, then the pipeline that produces seniors is broken. The metaphor predicts its own obsolescence — eventually there are no seniors left to guide the AI-junior. This is the most consequential implication of the post and it goes entirely unaddressed.
SMOC.AI's Post — 5 Minutter: AI og utviklere — read next: Systems thinking (Meadows) on stocks and flows — the senior developer pool is a stock that requires inflow from the junior pipeline; cutting the inflow depletes the stock over time
Mental Models & Understanding
incomplete
The claim that AI code quality is 'often low' is stated but not analyzed. Low in what dimension? Maintainability? Correctness? Performance? Architecture? Different quality dimensions have different implications for when and how AI should be supervised.
SMOC.AI's Post — 5 Minutter: AI og utviklere — read next: DORA 2025 findings on AI and code quality; empirical studies on AI-generated code defect rates
Code QualityDORA & Engineering Metrics
misinformed
The post assumes juniors can only learn by doing simple tasks themselves. But research on learning suggests that code review, debugging, and explaining code are equally powerful learning mechanisms — and AI makes all of these more accessible, not less. A junior reviewing AI output may learn faster than a junior writing CRUD endpoints from scratch.
SMOC.AI's Post — 5 Minutter: AI og utviklere — read next: Felienne Hermans on programming education; cognitive apprenticeship literature; research on learning through code review
Pull Requests & Async Code Review
illogical
The asymmetric-payoff argument for betting on quality assumes that quality-sceptics can 'easily remove their quality infrastructure at low cost' if wrong, while sceptics who guessed wrong must 'spend a lot to repair' their code. This treats the cost of removing quality as small and the cost of retrofitting it as large without examining the symmetry; the whole recommendation rests on that unexamined asymmetry, which the author only partly acknowledges when admitting he is unsure low-level quality matters apart from token cost.
Software Design in the Agentic Age: Place Your Bets — read next: Read on the economics of refactoring and of retrofitting quality, tests, and modelling into large agent-generated codebases, to test whether the cost asymmetry actually holds.
Code QualityTechnical Debt
incomplete
The essay surveys the debate over disposable, regenerable code but leaves it unresolved by admission: it does not settle when the token and verification cost of regenerating code from specifications beats patching, nor whether frequent regeneration makes the system less deterministic.
Software Design in the Agentic Age: Place Your Bets — read next: Seek empirical data or benchmarks comparing regenerate-from-spec versus patch-in-place on cost, determinism, and verification of functional and non-functional requirements.
Malleable SoftwareSpec-Driven Development
uninformed
The central bet on design quality depends on long-term maintainability and evolvability, yet the author concedes there 'isn't much data on long term evolvability with agentic development' because the space is young. The most load-bearing timescale of the argument is therefore supported largely by analogy to human software engineering rather than by evidence from agentic development itself.
Software Design in the Agentic Age: Place Your Bets — read next: Watch for longitudinal studies of agent-maintained codebases as they mature, and for evidence on whether agentic assumptions about cost-of-change transfer from the human case.
Agentic EngineeringChangeability & Optionality
incomplete
The closing externalities argument (energy, water, data-centre impacts, cognitive decline, funding burn, bubble risk) is presented as an unquantified list, and the jump from 'externalities are large' to 'build revertible systems' does not examine whether reversion to human engineering is actually achievable once specifications, agents, and toolchains are deeply embedded and human familiarity with the code has eroded.
Software Design in the Agentic Age: Place Your Bets — read next: Read AI energy and water lifecycle analyses and work on lock-in and exit costs of agentic toolchains, plus Bainbridge-style analysis of eroded human competence after automation.
