incomplete
Verification is elevated to the central frontier and the new quality bar ('is it verifiable enough'), yet no concrete method is given for verifying non-deterministic agent output; 'testing, but bigger than testing' names the gap without specifying how to close it.
We've Stopped Arguing About Whether AI Changes Software — read next: Read on evals, property-based and specification-based testing, and formal/executable specifications for agentic output.
EvalsSpec-Driven Development
illogical
The author leans 'spec-as-center' while conceding the 'code is the better spec, because deterministic' argument is hard to wave off, but never reconciles how a fuzzy prose spec that an agent re-interprets each run can be the durable source of truth when verifiability is the stated quality bar. The essay's own thesis is in tension with the counter-argument it admits.
We've Stopped Arguing About Whether AI Changes Software — read next: Read on executable/formal specifications, spec-to-code determinism, and whether specs can be made unambiguous enough to be the source of truth.
Spec-Driven Development
incomplete
The junior-pipeline collapse is diagnosed sharply but left without a solution; apprentice, pairing, and rotation models are 'floated' and explicitly declared to have no clean answer, so the mechanism for growing judgment/taste/troubleshooting in an agent-first world is unresolved.
We've Stopped Arguing About Whether AI Changes Software — read next: Read on deliberate practice, apprenticeship models, and skill acquisition in AI-augmented teams.
AI Labor DisruptionLearning Culture & SlackTaste & Judgment
uninformed
Security is flagged as terrifying but the author explicitly relays no specifics (he missed the session); the threat models and defenses are reasoned from the attack-surface list and the reaction of senior attendees rather than from firsthand content, leaving the actual mechanisms and mitigations thin.
We've Stopped Arguing About Whether AI Changes Software — read next: Read on prompt injection, the lethal trifecta, memory poisoning, and agent/supply-chain security architectures.
Agent SecurityLethal TrifectaSoftware Supply-Chain Security
incomplete
The 'compounding gap becomes a permanent structural moat' claim rests on flywheel logic plus a single secondhand anecdote (an org of thousands); it does not examine catch-up dynamics, whether late adopters can buy tools/talent, or evidence that early-mover AI advantages actually persist rather than erode.
We've Stopped Arguing About Whether AI Changes Software — read next: Read on technology-diffusion S-curves, durability of first-mover advantage, and enterprise AI adoption catch-up patterns.
Enterprise AI Adoption & ReadinessMoats & DefensibilityTechnology Diffusion & Transformation Curves
evidentiary
All supporting evidence comes from retail, service, hospitality, and franchise contexts; the author acknowledges seeing 'similar principles' in other sectors but provides no cases outside these domains, leaving the framework's generalizability to knowledge-work, tech, or manufacturing undemonstrated.
How Fast-Growing Companies Can Make Better Decisions
methodological
The case studies are observational and retrospective; no controlled comparison or counterfactual analysis is presented. OXXO's tripling of stores and doubling of profitability is attributed to structured empowerment, but confounding factors (market growth, competitor dynamics, macroeconomic conditions) are not addressed.
How Fast-Growing Companies Can Make Better Decisions
Team Autonomy
conceptual
The boundary between 'structured empowerment' and well-designed delegation with guardrails is under-theorized. Many mature organizations already provide decision playbooks and measure outcomes; the article does not clearly distinguish where existing management practice ends and structured empowerment begins.
How Fast-Growing Companies Can Make Better Decisions
GuardrailsTeam Autonomy
scopeclosed
The framework assumes decisions can be modularized into a small set of curated options. It does not address domains where the decision space is genuinely novel, ambiguous, or unbounded — situations where no pre-curated menu could capture the relevant options.
How Fast-Growing Companies Can Make Better Decisions
✓ closed byTBM 421: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 3) — Provides strategies for decision spaces that resist curated menus: flexible consistency (shared purpose/protocol, not shared form) and legible variety (making structurally different local work legible) cover novel or unbounded domains where no pre-curated option set fits.
Decision-Making PracticesDORA & Engineering Metrics
implicit_premise
The article assumes that organizational knowledge about 'what works' can be reliably captured, codified, and distributed as curated options. This presupposes that tacit knowledge can be made explicit and that best practices are transferable across contexts — an assumption that the knowledge management literature has long debated.
How Fast-Growing Companies Can Make Better Decisions
Decision-Making PracticesOrganizational Legibility
incomplete
No method for developing the one skill it names as decisive. The essay says attention is the key, says it can't be taught, and then offers only the paid 'Inner Circle' as a path — leaving the practical reader without an actionable next step, which is ironic for a self-styled 80/20 practical newsletter.
The real skill behind good AI prompts is older than computers — read next: Cal Newport 'Deep Work'; Nir Eyal 'Indistractable'; William James on attention; mindfulness / meta-attention research — the how-to layer the essay withholds
Attention as a Hijackable Resource
illogical
The thesis is close to unfalsifiable: any AI success is credited to attention, any failure blamed on inattention. It also equivocates between 'attention' as (a) present-moment noticing and (b) accumulated domain expertise ('knowing what matters about the task') — much of what he calls noticing is actually expertise/tacit knowledge, a different faculty.
The real skill behind good AI prompts is older than computers — read next: Michael Polanyi 'The Tacit Dimension'; Gary Klein 'Sources of Power' (expert intuition as pattern recognition); Ericsson on expertise — to separate 'attention' from 'expertise'
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
Title/body mismatch. The headline promises a skill 'older than computers,' but the body only asserts the pre-digital, timeless pedigree of attention — it never substantiates the historical claim it uses as a hook.
The real skill behind good AI prompts is older than computers — read next: Matthew Crawford 'The World Beyond Your Head' (attention as a cultural/historical achievement); Simone Weil on attention
uninformedclosed
Treats the model as a neutral mirror — 'what the AI just gave you' — assuming the signal to notice is fully in the human's court, and underweights that model capability, tool scaffolding, and prompt structure genuinely shift outcomes independent of user attention.
The real skill behind good AI prompts is older than computers — read next: Anthropic/OpenAI prompt- and context-engineering guides; evals showing model-choice and scaffolding effects — evidence that technique and model are not negligible
✓ closed byPrompting Claude Fable 5 — Svenson treats the model as a neutral mirror and underweights that model capability, tool scaffolding, and prompt structure shift outcomes independent of user attention. This official Anthropic guide is primary-source evidence for precisely that: model-specific structure (effort settings, brief-vs-enumerated instructions, subagent and memory scaffolding, elicitation language) measurably changes behavior — matching the gap's own reading_direction, which asked for exactly these Anthropic prompt-/context-engineering guides.
EvalsPrompting as Communication
incomplete
The newsletter curates and frames linked articles but does not itself develop full arguments. The reader must follow the links to evaluate the actual claims. The curator's commentary is endorsement, not analysis.
SWLW #698: From World Model to Domain Intelligence, How to build your Second Brain, and more. — read next: Read the linked articles, especially Boaz Katz on Domain Intelligence and Lalit Maganti on AI-assisted building, to evaluate whether the curator's framing is warranted.
World Model (Organizational)
uninformed
No discussion of risks or failure modes of the 'Domain Intelligence' framing. What happens when AI domain models encode biases? What if the 'mirror of strategic thinking' reflects flawed assumptions that AI makes harder to detect, not easier?
SWLW #698: From World Model to Domain Intelligence, How to build your Second Brain, and more. — read next: Look for critiques of AI-mediated organizational models, especially work on algorithmic bias in decision support systems and the risk of false clarity.
World Model (Organizational)
incomplete
The 'AI-native engineer' discussion acknowledges the framework will change but does not address what stable, durable qualities might persist across tool generations. Is there a timeless core to AI-native engineering, or is it entirely contingent?
SWLW #698: From World Model to Domain Intelligence, How to build your Second Brain, and more. — read next: Read the Augment Code article in full; compare with historical analyses of how 'internet-native' or 'cloud-native' engineer definitions stabilized over time.
AI-Native CompanyTalent Density
misinformed
The customer service argument assumes customers universally want proactive, personalized AI concierge service. Research on the 'uncanny valley' of AI personalization and consumer privacy preferences suggests this may not be universally welcomed.
SWLW #698: From World Model to Domain Intelligence, How to build your Second Brain, and more. — read next: Consult research on consumer attitudes toward AI personalization, e.g., Accenture and Pew studies on the personalization-privacy tradeoff.
illogical
The rodriscoll tweet claims that if everyone uses AI at the same level, no one has an advantage 'by definition' -- but this proves too much. The same argument applies to any technology (databases, cloud, etc.), yet operational excellence in commodity technologies has historically been a real differentiator.
SWLW #698: From World Model to Domain Intelligence, How to build your Second Brain, and more. — read next: Consider Porter's operational effectiveness vs. strategic positioning distinction; examine whether AI is more like electricity (everyone gets it) or more like software (execution quality varies enormously).
incomplete
The 'raise the floor but not the ceiling' claim is repeated approvingly but never operationalized. How does a leader, in practice, recognize ceiling-extending original work as opposed to confidently-presented model-mediated work? Without a heuristic, the framing becomes a flattering self-description rather than a usable filter.
SWLW #701: Gazing at the misty AI Frontier, From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering, and more. — read next: Read Cedric Chin on tacit knowledge or Tyler Cowen on 'what counts as taste in an AI era' for concrete diagnostics; also Karpathy's full talk for his hiring criteria as a partial answer
Organizational LegibilityTaste & Judgment
incomplete
Two of the curator's headline framings — 'AI raises the floor' and 'AI eats closed loops first' — are pulled toward each other but not reconciled. If AI raises the floor on closed-loop work, the implication is that closed-loop expertise becomes commodity labor while open-loop / relational / ethical work becomes the new premium. The newsletter implies this but does not state it, leaving leaders without an explicit instruction.
SWLW #701: Gazing at the misty AI Frontier, From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering, and more. — read next: Elad Gil's full 'Misty AI Frontier' piece + a careful read of Yue Zhao's leadership argument against Christensen's disruption framing — the synthesis is more useful than either alone
incomplete
The newsletter implicitly equates 'agentic engineering' (a craft skill) with 'agentic leadership' (a hiring/strategy task) but never separates them. Whether the same person should both code with agents and lead teams that code with agents — and whether the screening criteria are the same — is left ambiguous.
SWLW #701: Gazing at the misty AI Frontier, From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering, and more. — read next: Bruno Fernandez-Ruiz's 'Engineering in the Compiler Age' slides (referenced here, worth a focused extraction) and Karpathy's actual hiring section to see how he separates IC craft from leadership selection
Agentic Engineering
incomplete
The 'Anthropic AI-resistant evaluation' story is treated as a positive exemplar, but the newsletter does not engage with the obvious tension: a long-horizon take-home where AI is permitted requires significant unpaid candidate time, which is a known equity problem. The curator endorses the design pattern without acknowledging its cost.
SWLW #701: Gazing at the misty AI Frontier, From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering, and more. — read next: The original Anthropic write-up, plus any of the recent compensated-interview / pair-programming-screen literature, for a more honest cost-benefit picture
illogical
The endorsement of 'gut over head' (SahilBloom tweet) sits uneasily next to the endorsement of 'shared design concept before you code' (Pocock). The first valorizes unjustified judgment; the second valorizes explicit-and-shared rationalization before action. The newsletter treats both as obviously correct without noting that they pull in opposite directions for a leader deciding which to lean on in a given situation.
SWLW #701: Gazing at the misty AI Frontier, From Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering, and more. — read next: Daniel Kahneman on when intuition is trustworthy (closed environments with rapid feedback — i.e., precisely the closed loops AI is eating); Gary Klein's recognition-primed decision research for the conditions under which gut beats analysis
Lean & Toyota WaySoftware Design FundamentalsTaste & Judgment
incomplete
The newsletter juxtaposes the OpenClaw story (passionate builder using AI agents productively) with the AI Psychosis critique (executives using AI agents unproductively) but never articulates what distinguishes productive from unproductive AI agent use. The implicit answer seems to be 'building vs. managing' but this is never made explicit or tested.
SWLW #702: How I created OpenClaw, Your CEO is suffering from AI Psychosis, and more. — read next: Read the actual OpenClaw video/article and the AI Psychosis article side by side to extract the distinguishing criteria
Agentic AIEmotional Impact of AI WorkOpenClaw
incomplete
The compounding argument (10% effort → 2x output) is stated as fact but has no empirical backing cited. The Hamming lecture is anecdotal authority, not evidence. The actual compounding dynamics of knowledge work are far more complex than financial interest.
SWLW #702: How I created OpenClaw, Your CEO is suffering from AI Psychosis, and more. — read next: Cal Newport's 'Deep Work' or Ericsson's deliberate practice research for more rigorous treatments of effort-to-outcome dynamics
Attention as a Hijackable Resource
incomplete
The curator's editorial voice synthesizes across articles but never interrogates the tension between 'seek measurable value' (AI Psychosis commentary) and 'shape what's possible rather than optimize what's in front of you' (strategic thinking commentary). These are potentially contradictory: strategic thinking often requires investing in things that are not yet measurable.
SWLW #702: How I created OpenClaw, Your CEO is suffering from AI Psychosis, and more. — read next: Roger Martin's work on strategy as an integrative activity, or Clayton Christensen on the innovator's dilemma of measurability bias
Emotional Impact of AI Work
incomplete
The issue diagnoses AI-era pressures (salary-scale economics, direction-less amplification, agent reliability, term-inflation) and poses them as open questions to the reader ('where does your company sit?', 'what would you do differently?') but never synthesizes them into an operating model — it stops short of what a leader should actually do.
SWLW #710: Revised rules of Eng Leadership, When AI costs more than the engineer, and more. — read next: Will Larson's 'Revised Rules of Engineering Leadership' itself (the linked favorite), and John Cutler / engineering-strategy sources that convert principles into concrete decision rules.
Measuring AI Impact
incomplete
Ellenbogen asserts AI investments must deliver 'clear, sustainable value' but offers no method for attributing revenue or user growth to AI spend; the measurement problem (how to prove AI ROI) is treated as solved.
SWLW #710: Revised rules of Eng Leadership, When AI costs more than the engineer, and more. — read next: DX / measuring-AI-impact work on oppositional, outcome-anchored measurement; the amplification-vs-augmentation ROI split.
Measuring AI Impact
incomplete
The continuity framing (fundamentals and judgment are what matter, AI only raises their stakes) under-weights the possibility that some fundamentals themselves must change under AI — it treats 'fundamentals' as fixed rather than a live question.
SWLW #710: Revised rules of Eng Leadership, When AI costs more than the engineer, and more. — read next: Sources debating whether the fundamentals taught today are still the right ones (e.g. Tobias Torrissen on juniors and fundamentals; 'The Future of Software Engineering' on mid-level retraining).
Taste & Judgment
scope limitation
The article never addresses contexts where T*D may not apply or may need significant adaptation — solo developers, open-source projects with asynchronous contributors, research/exploratory coding, or very early-stage prototyping.
T*D — read next: Investigate when trunk-based development or pairing breaks down (e.g., distributed open-source, 1-person teams, spike work).
Trunk-Based & Continuous Delivery
missing causal mechanism
The three practices are presented as a unified triad (T*D) but the article never explains why these three specifically, or how they causally reinforce each other. The mutual reinforcement is implied but not argued.
T*D — read next: Look for literature on the interaction effects between TDD and CI/CD, and between pairing and TDD adoption success.
survivorship bias risk
The 'natural emergence' argument draws on teams that succeeded. Teams that tried these practices and failed, or high-performing teams that use different approaches, are not discussed.
T*D — read next: Examine DORA data more carefully for whether TBD is necessary or merely correlated; look at high-performing teams using different workflows (e.g., Linux kernel's patch-based model).
DORA & Engineering Metrics
evidence selectivity
The pairing research cited (Williams & Kessler 2000) is over 25 years old. More recent and nuanced studies on pair programming effectiveness are not referenced.
T*D — read next: Review recent meta-analyses on pair programming effectiveness, particularly in remote/distributed settings.
Pair & Mob Programming
is-ought gap
The article moves from 'these practices emerge naturally' (descriptive) to 'these are the only sensible way to work' (prescriptive) without bridging the logical gap. Natural emergence does not entail optimality.
T*D — read next: Consider evolutionary arguments: not all convergent outcomes are optimal; they may reflect local optima or shared biases in the practitioner community.
incomplete
Cutler identifies the problem (legitimacy is skipped) and gives the Haier counter-example, but doesn't describe what legitimacy-grounded AI organizational design would actually entail. The essay points at the question but doesn't begin to answer it.
TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy — read next: James C. Scott's 'Seeing Like a State' (the source of the legibility/power argument); democratic workplace theory; platform cooperativism literature
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The critique targets Dorsey specifically but the pattern Cutler describes (efficiency metrics as value substitutes, decentralization rhetoric concealing control) applies to most AI organizational proposals. The essay doesn't synthesize the critique into generalizable criteria.
TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy — read next: Literature on algorithmic management: 'The Platform Society' (van Dijck et al.); Nick Srnicek's 'Platform Capitalism'; Cutler's own TBM work on operating systems and accountability
illogical
The coda ('AI is a catalyst for whatever you already believe') slightly undermines the specificity of the critique. If AI just amplifies existing values, then the problem is Dorsey's pre-existing values — not a rhetorical trick specific to AI. This is a different and stronger argument than Cutler develops.
TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy — read next: Winner's 'Do Artifacts Have Politics?' — the more rigorous version of this claim
uninformed
Cutler doesn't engage with the existing literature on algorithmic management (gig worker surveillance, scoring systems in platform companies like Uber/Amazon warehouses). These are the live cases where the legibility/legitimacy conflict has already played out, with empirical data.
TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy — read next: Alexandrea Ravenelle's 'Hustle and Gig'; Alex Rosenblat's 'Uberland'; Shoshana Zuboff's 'The Age of Surveillance Capitalism'
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The post diagnoses the problem brilliantly but doesn't offer a transition path. How does an organization that recognizes it's running the engineering-as-bottleneck model actually shift to the learning model? What are the first moves? Who has to change their behavior first? The 8 questions at the end are diagnostic, not prescriptive.
TBM 415: Demand Mix, Discovery, and AI as a (Dys)function Multiplier — read next: Cutler's earlier work on mandate levels and enabling constraints; Team Topologies (Skelton & Pais) on organizational design; Marty Cagan's 'Transformed' on product operating model transitions
Code Was Never the Bottleneck
incomplete
The post treats AI as a uniform amplifier, but AI tools are heterogeneous. Code generation, customer research synthesis, experiment automation, and demand analysis are different AI applications that amplify different parts of the funnel. A more specific analysis of which AI applications amplify dysfunction vs. which could help shift the model would be more actionable.
TBM 415: Demand Mix, Discovery, and AI as a (Dys)function Multiplier — read next: The Research-Driven Agents piece (research phase as demand shaping for agents); specific AI tooling literature on product discovery vs. delivery
Product Discovery & Experimentation
incomplete
The feature factory PM says 'I wish it was different.' There's an unexamined question: can a team that has achieved functional feature factory operation use that credibility and trust as a foundation to gradually expand its mandate toward the learning model? Or does the success of the feature factory create path dependency that makes the transition harder?
TBM 415: Demand Mix, Discovery, and AI as a (Dys)function Multiplier — read next: Innovation literature on the 'competency trap'; Christensen's innovator's dilemma applied to internal teams
Trust
incomplete
The article doesn't address the organizational power dynamics that make stewardship habits hard to sustain. Who has the authority to insist on these practices? What happens when a new VP arrives and disrupts the habit? The argument assumes a continuity of leadership and culture that many organizations lack.
TBM 416: Investment Stewardship (As Habit) — read next: Literature on organizational routines and institutional memory (e.g., Nelson & Winter's evolutionary theory of the firm) would strengthen this argument.
incomplete
The Bayesian updating framework is invoked but never operationalized. What are the priors? What counts as evidence? How do you avoid anchoring bias in your priors? The metaphor does real work in the argument but remains at the level of analogy.
TBM 416: Investment Stewardship (As Habit) — read next: Douglas Hubbard's 'How to Measure Anything' provides practical methods for Bayesian estimation in business contexts that could concretize this.
Decision-Making Practices
incomplete
The article conflates two distinct audiences: engineering leaders who need to practice stewardship, and executives/finance who need to ask better questions. The five action steps mix both perspectives without differentiating who owns each behavior.
TBM 416: Investment Stewardship (As Habit) — read next: Exploring the principal-agent problem between engineering and finance would sharpen the argument about whose habit this needs to be.
incomplete
The PE aside is presented as a concession but actually undermines part of the thesis. If PE firms can arrive and quickly create clarity through explicit models, that suggests some form of cold-start IS possible -- just a reductive one. The author waves this away without fully engaging the tension.
TBM 416: Investment Stewardship (As Habit) — read next: A more careful treatment would distinguish between types of clarity: PE provides cost-side clarity but not value-side clarity, which is the harder problem the article is actually about.
implicit_premise
Cutler's argument depends on the implicit premise that multi-perspective input produces better understanding of complex product territory than single-perspective input amplified, regardless of how powerful the amplification. This premise is doing enormous work — it is the reason a 'cartographer for places you haven't visited' is a bad metaphor even if the cartographer is brilliant. The premise is plausible but not defended, and it is exactly the premise that AI optimists would contest: 'what if the AI cartographer can simulate enough perspectives well enough that the collision-of-stakes advantage shrinks toward zero?' Cutler dismisses this as 'directing a puppet show' but does not say why simulated diversity must always be epistemically inferior to embodied diversity.
TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests — read next: Cass Sunstein and Reid Hastie 'Wiser' on group decision-making and the conditions under which diverse groups outperform individuals; Helene Landemore 'Democratic Reason' on epistemic diversity; Andy Clark on extended/predictive cognition and where the boundary between simulated and real perspectives lies
incompleteclosed
The taxonomy of trails/quests/campfires is asserted as the carving of collaborative work but is not tested against alternatives. Where does individual deep work without AI fit? Where do decision points fit — are they a fourth mode, or part of campfires? Where does external customer contact fit — is talking to a customer a quest, or something outside the model? The neatness of the three-way split is rhetorically powerful but the boundaries are not interrogated.
TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests — read next: Etienne Wenger on communities of practice and the modes of engagement/imagination/alignment; Diana Larsen and Esther Derby's retrospectives literature on cadence design; Allen Holub or Jessitron on team patterns
✓ closed byThe Office Amenity That Actually Improves Teamwork — The article does not use the campfires/trails/quests vocabulary, but it directly answers that gap's open question, 'where does individual deep work fit?': deep work is not a fourth collaborative mode but the precondition the collaborative modes depend on, since effective teamwork requires protected solitude for independent thinking before and between collective work (p3, p7, p8).
Attention as a Hijackable ResourceCraft CommunityMultiplayer vs Single-Player AI
implicit_premise
The piece assumes that the friction of collaboration is itself epistemically valuable — that 'wait, go back' moments and the trading of the steering wheel produce understanding that solo work cannot. The opposite hypothesis is plausible: collaboration friction is a cost teams pay because they have no better way to integrate perspectives, and AI that genuinely integrates multiple perspectives could deliver the integration without the friction. Cutler's strongest moment ('the back-and-forth... was the outcome') is also his most assertion-heavy.
TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests — read next: Karl Weick on sensemaking and the role of equivocality; the literature on collective intelligence (Anita Williams Woolley); critiques of meeting-heavy cultures (Cal Newport, 'A World Without Email')
unacknowledged_question
Cutler's prescription assumes psychological and organizational conditions for genuine multiplayer collaboration — trust, time, willingness to be redirected mid-stream, role flexibility. He notes these implicitly (his diagram is for 'a team with real trust') but does not engage with what teams should do when those conditions are absent. The advice 'make AI a shared surface' presupposes the willingness to share. In a low-trust team, single-player AI may be a rational defensive posture, and 'multiplayer or bust' could be advice that only works for teams that already don't need it most.
TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests — read next: Amy Edmondson on psychological safety as a precondition for collaborative learning; Patrick Lencioni's trust pyramid; research on remote/distributed team trust formation
Multiplayer vs Single-Player AIPsychological SafetyTrust
incomplete
The trails/stigmergy framing inherits a curation problem the analogy obscures. Pheromone trails decay biologically; Wikipedia has revert mechanisms and stewards. Context pointers in a codebase do not decay — they accumulate, can become stale, can contradict each other, and can mislead future readers and AI agents. The piece names trails as an asset without acknowledging the maintenance liability or the political question of who has authority to prune.
TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests — read next: Brian Foote and Joseph Yoder 'Big Ball of Mud' on accumulated context as decay; Ward Cunningham on technical debt; the documentation-rot literature (Daniele Procida's 'Diataxis')
Agentic AIContext EngineeringMultiplayer vs Single-Player AITechnical Debt
incomplete
The base-camp/climbing-mode prescription is offered as a heuristic but not operationalized. How does a team decide when to switch modes? Who calls the switch? What happens when the climb reveals the plan was wrong — can you go back to base camp mid-climb?
TBM 419: Stop Being So Negative! Stop Being So Naive! — read next: Amy Edmondson's work on teaming and psychological safety; decision-making frameworks like RAPID or DACI for explicit mode-switching
Psychological Safety
incomplete
The gender and power dimension is raised but not developed. The piece acknowledges that the same words land differently depending on who says them, but does not explore how the base-camp/climbing framework interacts with power asymmetries.
TBM 419: Stop Being So Negative! Stop Being So Naive! — read next: Literature on voice and silence in organizations (Morrison, Milliken); intersectionality in organizational behavior
incomplete
The piece focuses on the interpersonal and team level. It does not address how organizational incentive structures (promotion criteria, performance reviews, leadership selection) systematically filter for outcome optimism and against capability optimism.
TBM 419: Stop Being So Negative! Stop Being So Naive! — read next: Research on organizational culture and selection effects; Jerry Harvey's 'The Abilene Paradox'; Chris Argyris on defensive routines
incomplete
Constructive controversy research is cited but the conditions for cooperative framing are not specified. What makes a team able to disagree cooperatively rather than destructively?
TBM 419: Stop Being So Negative! Stop Being So Naive! — read next: David W. Johnson and Roger T. Johnson's constructive controversy research; Patrick Lencioni's 'The Five Dysfunctions of a Team'; Linda Hill's 'Collective Genius'
incomplete
Bucket 3 (genuinely new practices only possible with AI) is the most interesting category but receives the least development. The author acknowledges these exist but provides zero concrete examples of what they might look like.
TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle — read next: Look for writing on emergent AI-native workflows: Maggie Appleton on AI interaction patterns, or Linus Lee on AI-augmented thinking tools.
AI-Native Company
incomplete
The COM-B analysis is applied only to individuals. The organizational and structural factors that create and reinforce the three traps (incentive structures, promotion criteria, budget cycles) are not analyzed.
TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle — read next: Westrum's organizational typology applied to AI adoption; Amy Edmondson on psychological safety as a precondition for the identity work Cutler describes.
Enterprise AI Adoption & ReadinessPsychological Safety
incomplete
The argument assumes a product/tech organizational context. How the four-bucket framework applies to non-tech domains (healthcare, education, government) is unaddressed.
TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle — read next: Sector-specific AI adoption research; Ethan Mollick's work on AI across professions.
Enterprise AI Adoption & Readiness
incomplete
The eight-step journey is presented as linear and individual. No treatment of how teams or organizations move through this collectively, or what happens when team members are at different stages.
TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle — read next: Bridges' Transition Model for organizational change; Kegan and Lahey on immunity to change.
illogical
The claim that 'the more certain someone sounds, the more likely they're relying on outdated models' risks being unfalsifiable. It could also apply to Cutler's own confident claims about identity threat being the biggest obstacle.
TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle — read next: Philip Tetlock on superforecasting and calibrated confidence — certainty is not inherently a signal of poor reasoning.
Professional Identity
incomplete
The essay provides no method for diagnosing which strategy to apply where — the questions at the end are reflective prompts, not a decision framework. How do you determine whether a given consistency point should be sharp, flexible, or legible variety?
TBM 421: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 3)
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
Job 3 is missing from the AI section (jumps from Job 2 to Job 4) — either an editing artifact or a deliberate omission, but it is unexplained.
TBM 421: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 3)
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The taxonomy is presented as exhaustive (three strategies) but no argument is given for why these three are sufficient. Could there be a fourth strategy (e.g., emergent consistency that arises from shared tooling without explicit design)?
TBM 421: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 3)
incomplete
The AI risk analysis identifies that friction can be a learning mechanism but offers no heuristic for distinguishing productive friction from pure overhead — the central practical question for anyone applying the framework to AI adoption.
TBM 421: Minimally Viable Consistency (Part 3)
Enterprise AI Adoption & Readiness
incomplete
The essay names courage/trust as the binding constraint but does not theorize how it is produced. What organizational conditions, leadership behaviors, or institutional commitments actually lower the political cost of honest description? Cutler mentions 'psychological safety in workshops' in passing but doesn't extend it to the org level.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Amy Edmondson on psychological safety at the team and org level; Patrick Lencioni on vulnerability; potentially Kim Scott on radical candor — but also more structural treatments like Argyris/Schön on double-loop learning that connect the act of 'naming undiscussables' to organizational learning capacity.
Hierarchy & Org ChartsLearning Culture & SlackPsychological SafetyTrust
incomplete
Cutler's prescription is essentially individual ('surf the tension', 'find pockets'). He gestures at — but does not develop — what institutional design would look like for an organization that natively holds two views. The flattening tendency of org systems is named but not redesigned.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Stafford Beer's Viable System Model and Espejo on organizational cybernetics (which explicitly model multiple recursive views of the same system); Wardley mapping for multi-frame strategy; Niels Pflaeging's beta-codex / 'org physics' (cells vs. functions). Also Cutler's own earlier writing on 'operating systems' for product orgs.
Hierarchy & Org ChartsOrganizational Legibility
incomplete
Power and political economy are named but not modeled. Who actually has standing to call an undiscussable? When does the act of naming protect the speaker (e.g., a senior leader with capital) and when does it ruin them (the director who 'knows how things go')? The essay treats these as moods rather than as predictable functions of organizational position.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Jeffrey Pfeffer on power in organizations; Mary Parker Follett on integrative conflict; Argyris on defensive routines (Cutler cites Argyris on undiscussables but doesn't engage the full theory). Also Henry Mintzberg's typology of power configurations.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
Design is positioned as the 'truth-telling' frame because designers see the seams, but design's own blind spots and the cost of giving design more structural power are not examined. The essay grants design moral authority without scrutinizing it.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Jared Spool and Don Norman critiques of design's overreach; service design literature on the limits of journey-mapping; Tony Ulwick / jobs-to-be-done as a competitor frame that disputes design's own narrative.
Decision-Making PracticesHierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The essay focuses on the product/design/tech axis inside a single org. It does not address how this dynamic changes in regulated, public-sector, or critical-infrastructure orgs where 'teams' are also legal/audit/governance entities — a context particularly relevant to Norwegian public-sector tech (NAV, Entur, public services).
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Pia Andrews / GovTech writing on public-sector team design; Mark Schwartz ('A Seat at the Table', 'War and Peace and IT') on bureaucracy and IT leadership; Mariana Mazzucato on mission-led public organizations. For the Nordic context: Skatteetaten/NAV/Entur case studies on team topologies in regulated environments.
incomplete
The 'pendulum' framing is descriptively useful but normatively underspecified. Cutler observes the swing between 'everything is a product' and 'three products only' but doesn't propose what the stable middle looks like — what's the unit of organization that resists both inflation and deflation of the product label?
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Team Topologies (Skelton & Pais) explicitly types teams (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem) as a possible answer; Sriram Narayan on agile organization design; Marty Cagan's 'empowered product teams' framing as a competitor.
incomplete
The 'organized hypocrisy' frame is borrowed but not pressure-tested. When is a publicly stated aspiration actually load-bearing (it constrains future decisions; it can be used as leverage) and when is it inert? Cutler's 'is leadership treating the gap as something to close or as a convenient fiction' is a binary test where reality is likely a spectrum with measurable indicators.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Brunsson's original 'Organization of Hypocrisy' and 'Mechanisms of Hope'; March & Olsen on logics of appropriateness; Pfeffer & Sutton 'The Knowing-Doing Gap'.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
AI/agent-assisted development is conspicuously absent from an essay published in 2025/2026 about how teams are defined. If a meaningful share of engineering work is mediated by agents, the definition of 'team', headcount-as-cost, and the engineering manager's role all shift. Cutler doesn't touch this, though it is plausibly the next pendulum swing.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: DORA AI capabilities report; Kent Beck on AI-assisted design ('Tidy First?' series); Addy Osmani / Steve Yegge on agentic engineering; Cutler's own TBM pieces on institutional overload and AI (TBM 412).
Agentic AIAgentic EngineeringDORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
Cutler's prescription ('surf the tension', 'find pockets') leaves the change agent without a diagnostic for distinguishing a real pocket of opportunity from a temporary opening that will close, or from a trap that consumes effort without producing change. The post advises a posture but not a method.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Donella Meadows' 'Places to Intervene in a System' (leverage points hierarchy) supplies the missing taxonomy of where intervention has high leverage. Fearless Change (Manns & Rising) — which Kjetil's persona shows he highlights heavily — supplies pattern-level guidance for what to do once a pocket is identified.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The post diagnoses the asymmetric cost geometry but never asks whether the asymmetry could itself be redesigned. Could product be held accountable for operational consequences? Could design have budget authority? Cutler treats the geometry as a given and prescribes only how to live within it.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Allen Holub's #NoAccountability work and the broader DRI (Directly Responsible Individual) literature — which Kjetil already engages with — would supply the missing accountability-redesign frame. Henrik Kniberg's writing on Spotify-era squad accountability is another natural extension.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The post is silent on AI tooling, despite Cutler's recent series on AI and organizational design. This is a striking absence — if AI is changing how work is coordinated (skills, agents, asynchronous artifacts), does it also change the cost asymmetry that this post identifies as the root cause?
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Re-read TBM 412 (institutionalized overload + AI) and TBM 418 (campfires, trails, quests) against this post. Test whether AI-mediated artifacts could shift the cost asymmetry — does cheap AI-generated documentation make tech's 'I have to live with it' position cheaper or more expensive?
Multiplayer vs Single-Player AI
uninformed
Cutler invokes Brunsson's 'organized hypocrisy' as a positive frame but does not engage with Brunsson's own caveats about when the gap becomes self-reinforcing (when the aspirational model is used to deflect accountability for the real model). The post borrows the lens but not the warning.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Brunsson, 'The Organization of Hypocrisy' (1989/2002) — the original argument is more pessimistic than Cutler's borrowing suggests. Worth reading directly.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
incomplete
The post barely engages with what 'aspirational' actually looks like — there is no example of a North-Star map that is being incrementally closed versus one that is permanent theater. Without exemplars, the distinction is rhetorical rather than operational.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Team Topologies (Skelton & Pais) — their stream-aligned/platform/enabling/complicated-subsystem taxonomy is the closest thing to an operational aspirational map; useful to read it through Cutler's lens to see whether it survives the 'permanent theater' test.
illogical
Cutler argues both (a) describing reality is cognitively easy and (b) organizational history is load-bearing and rarely documented. If the historical context is missing, then describing reality is not actually easy — it is partial in ways the workshop participants cannot fully see. The post does not reconcile these two claims.
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Engage with Edgar Schein on organizational culture as historically embedded — the surface-level description that workshops surface may be necessary but not sufficient, and the post understates how much remains invisible even after the political cost has been paid.
incomplete
The C-suite is named (via Dame's quote — 'cross-functional with complete oversight') but never analyzed as itself a structural location with its own incentive geometry. If the fractal breaks at middle management, what is the corresponding pathology at the top?
TBM 423: Why Defining Teams Is So Hard — read next: Henry Mintzberg on structure in fives; Russ Ackoff on idealized design — both supply tools for analyzing the C-suite as an organizational location rather than a moral category. Camille Fournier on engineering leadership ladders is closer to home.
incomplete
Cutler shows that mirrors alone don't produce change and pivots to 'mirroring', but never shows how mirroring actually surfaces and resolves the hard, uncomfortable realities (the coin-flip odds, the org-chart vestiges) that mirrors were meant to confront. Without that, 'helping people feel seen' risks becoming a sophisticated way to avoid the elephants — comfort substituting for change, and the resolution quietly undermining the very problem it set out to solve.
TBM 426: The Trouble With Mirrors — read next: Read Amy Edmondson on psychological safety as enabling candor (not merely comfort); Kegan & Lahey, Immunity to Change, on how meaning-making translates into action; and Peter Block's Community in full, alongside Cutler's own operating-systems / legibility work (TBM 414, the visibility guide) for the mechanism from being-seen to actually confronting the hard things.
Organizational LegibilityPsychological Safety
incomplete
The relationship between mirroring and accountability is left unresolved. Cutler insists surfaced truth must be met with 'stewardship' and asks 'who is accountable for what happens next', yet the mirroring resolution emphasizes feeling seen and NOT being 'held against' people — potentially in tension with the accountability that acting on uncomfortable truths requires.
TBM 426: The Trouble With Mirrors — read next: Read on accountability cultures (Allen Holub) and the distinction between blame and accountability; Cutler's own writing on directly-responsible individuals; and restorative-vs-punitive framings of organizational feedback.
incomplete
The cynical, power-analytic read of transparency advocates ('actually about power'; candor is cheap for the already-won) is asserted without evidence and, as Cutler half-concedes ('I realize all of this sounds negative'), risks proving too much: by the same logic his own mirroring prescription is equally positional and equally a bid to define what good relating looks like.
TBM 426: The Trouble With Mirrors — read next: Read Langdon Winner, 'Do Artifacts Have Politics?', and critical-management studies for the rigorous version of the power-in-ideals claim, and test whether the argument self-undermines when turned on the author's own prescription.
incomplete
No account of mechanism or scale. Cutler's clearest example of mirroring is a two-party coaching dyad (you, the coach, the scale), but his own solo-vs-collective distinction makes the collective the hard case. How a leader practically moves a whole team or organization from mirrors to mirroring — how many people 'draw the map side by side' at once — is not addressed.
TBM 426: The Trouble With Mirrors — read next: Read facilitation and large-group-intervention literature (Open Space, liberating structures) and Peter Block's Community for community-scale practices that operationalize joint meaning-making beyond the dyad.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The essay is almost entirely diagnostic on the systemic register: it insists AI cannot fix incentives, legitimacy or voice safety and that these must be addressed by humans/systems, but offers no method for doing so. The 'individual + systemic' synthesis names both levels without specifying how to act on the systemic one.
TBM 428: Yes, Robot. Yes, Boss? — read next: Cutler's own 'AI Won't Save Your Kingdom' and legibility/legitimacy pieces; systems-change literature on incentive redesign; Amy Edmondson on building psychological safety as a deliberate practice.
Organizational LegibilityPsychological Safety
incomplete
The capability/friction vs. motivational/political blocker taxonomy is presented as a clean binary, but many real blockers are mixed (e.g., 'missing scaffolding' can itself be a symptom of low legitimacy or fear). The essay doesn't address how to tell which kind of blocker you actually face — precisely the diagnosis on which its advice depends.
TBM 428: Yes, Robot. Yes, Boss? — read next: Diagnostic frameworks that separate technical from adaptive problems (Heifetz, adaptive leadership); root-cause methods that trace surface friction to underlying incentives.
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The accountability question is raised and dropped: if AI makes bad-faith performance management cleaner and more defensible, the essay's own 'would they be successful?' implies existing legal/organizational recourse is inadequate, but it neither examines that recourse nor proposes countermeasures (audit trails, prompt provenance, HR governance).
TBM 428: Yes, Robot. Yes, Boss? — read next: Agent-governance and decision-provenance literature; employment-law treatments of pretextual performance management; work on organizational undiscussables.
incomplete
The asymmetry claim — that AI amplifies dysfunction more readily than honesty — is argued qualitatively (bad-faith use is the low-effort default) but never sized. Whether the reflective-use gap is closable by design (defaults, prompts, scaffolds that nudge toward self-interrogation) is left unexplored.
TBM 428: Yes, Robot. Yes, Boss? — read next: Choice-architecture / nudge work applied to AI defaults; 'scaffolded cognitive friction' as an antidote (cognitive-surrender literature).
Cognitive Surrender
evidential
Only one bug class demonstrated — the TOCTOU race condition. No evidence the technique works for other common flakiness causes: environment-dependent tests, time-sensitive tests, test-order dependencies, resource leaks, or non-deterministic external services.
Teaching an AI Agent to Debug Flaky Tests
conceptual
The claim of 'deterministic' debugging conflates procedural repeatability with outcome reliability. The agent's interpretation of the coverage diff is still probabilistic, and the article does not measure how often the agent correctly identifies root causes across varied bugs.
Teaching an AI Agent to Debug Flaky Tests
practical
The technique requires the flaky test to actually fail during the collection runs. For tests that fail once in thousands of runs, the script's loop-until-both-outcomes approach may be impractical without guidance on how many runs are needed.
Teaching an AI Agent to Debug Flaky Tests
Prompting as Communication
conceptual
Assumes line-level coverage granularity is sufficient. Some flakiness may involve identical line-level execution with different data values, timing, or ordering that coverage cannot capture.
Teaching an AI Agent to Debug Flaky Tests
incomplete
The document identifies the productivity/experience paradox but offers no framework for resolving it beyond the 'agent experience' reframe. How should organizations actually measure and invest in developer satisfaction when it is decoupled from output?

uninformed
The document does not address the economic incentive structures that drive organizations toward the regressions it warns about (e.g., short-term productivity metrics rewarding large batch AI output). Without addressing incentives, the warnings may go unheeded.

evidentiary
No quantitative evidence is offered for any of the enumerated harms. The 'net negative' claim rests entirely on an unweighted list of negatives versus two positives.
The growing AI backlash
AI Backlash
analytical
The exemption of coding (and brainstorming) from the 'net negative' verdict is asserted without criteria for what makes a domain qualify as net-positive. No framework for how to weigh domain-specific benefits against cross-cutting harms.
The growing AI backlash
AI Backlash
dialectical
No engagement with counterarguments: productivity gains in knowledge work, medical applications, accessibility tools, scientific research acceleration. The piece treats these as non-existent rather than as insufficient to offset harms.
The growing AI backlash
predictive
The political prediction (2028 elections) lacks any mechanism story — how does diffuse frustration convert into voting behavior or policy change?
The growing AI backlash
constructive
The closing hope that 'some future form of AI might be great' is unconnected to any analysis of what would make future AI different from current GenAI.
The growing AI backlash
incomplete
The four-level taxonomy is presented as self-evident without empirical grounding, prior art references (beyond Engelbart), or criteria for distinguishing the levels in practice. It is unclear whether the levels are discrete, overlapping, or a continuum, and what determines when a system transitions from one level to another.
The keyboard was never the bottleneck — read next: Compare with established taxonomies: Lehman's laws of software evolution, the Cynefin framework, or Conway's law literature for more rigorous level-distinction criteria.
Sociotechnical Architecture
incomplete
The claim that AI-generated code characteristically fails at higher levels is asserted as an observed pattern but supported only by a brief characterization ('works in isolation, fails when inputs vary'). No specific examples, studies, or failure cases are cited.
The keyboard was never the bottleneck — read next: Look for empirical studies on AI-generated code in production systems, e.g., GitHub Copilot studies, or case analyses of AI-generated code failures in integration contexts.
incomplete
The article does not address the possibility that AI capabilities at higher levels could improve significantly. The boundary between levels 2 and 3 is treated as if it were a stable, inherent limitation rather than a current snapshot of AI capability.
The keyboard was never the bottleneck — read next: Follow research on AI agents for multi-component system design, autonomous software engineering agents, and LLM-based architecture reasoning.

incomplete
Amundsen argues the opportunity to 'get better at getting better' exists but immediately hedges that it may not be realized. No mechanisms, incentives, or conditions for realizing it are discussed.
The keyboard was never the bottleneck — read next: Engelbart's original 'Augmenting Human Intellect' framework, and organizational learning literature (Argyris, Senge) for conditions under which meta-improvement actually occurs.
evidentiary
No evidence beyond one DORA citation and personal anecdote — no data on how many organizations are actually struggling vs. succeeding with AI tools.
The Organization Is the Bottleneck — read next: The 2025 DORA State of AI-Assisted Software Development report for the underlying data.
DORA & Engineering Metrics
unasked question
The essay treats 'organizational maturity' as a single dimension but never asks whether an organization might be mature in CI/CD but immature in documentation or ownership — and which specific dimensions matter most for AI agents.

unasked questionclosed
No discussion of how AI agents might actually change the required organizational structures. The assumption is that the same structures apply, but AI might demand new ones (e.g., agent-specific observability, prompt governance, output review workflows).

implicit premise
The book promotion is structural to the argument — the entire piece is framed to show the book is newly relevant, which may bias the selection and framing of parallels toward ones that support the book's thesis.

unasked questionclosed
No engagement with the possibility that AI tools could help organizations build the missing foundations themselves (e.g., AI generating tests, documentation, or CI configs for organizations that lack them) — which would undermine the 'prerequisites first' framing.

✓ closed byDoorDash Used Copilot to Convert Its XCTest-Based iOS Test Suite to Swift Testing — Partially addresses whether AI can build the missing foundations itself: DoorDash used coding agents to modernize its own test-suite safety net (tooling-first, team-owned, gated migration) rather than requiring the foundation to pre-exist.
unsupported claim
The 3x shipping speed claim is stated without citation or qualification.
The Organization Is the Bottleneck
incomplete
The entire article is paywalled. All propositions beyond the teaser's two sentences are inferred from the title and HBR's typical article structure. This extraction is a structured placeholder — not an analytical reading of the actual text.
The Pitfalls of an Interim CEO — read next: Access the full HBR article; search for the underlying research (likely event studies on interim CEO announcements; Quigley & Hambrick 2012 on interim leadership; Ballinger & Marcel 2010 on CEO succession and interim appointments)
incomplete
The available teaser doesn't distinguish between types of interim appointments: external interim (a professional turnaround/transition specialist), internal interim (an existing executive temporarily elevated), or board member as interim. Each likely has different risk profiles. The 'pitfalls' may not apply uniformly.
The Pitfalls of an Interim CEO — read next: Succession research distinguishing interim types; Spencer Stuart data on interim CEO outcomes by appointment type
incomplete
The 'long-term value loss' claim requires a counterfactual: compared to what? A rushed permanent appointment? A failed external search? The article presumably addresses this, but without the text, the comparison baseline is unknown.
The Pitfalls of an Interim CEO — read next: The academic literature on CEO succession planning quality as a predictor of transition success
incomplete
L2-L5 is paywalled. The most consequential transitions — L3 (trusting your data), L4 (acting on signals), L5 (self-improvement) — are named but not analyzed. The article promises the full framework but delivers only half of it to free readers.
#2: The Unsexy Truth of AI Adoption — read next: The premium version of this article; or: Gartner AI maturity frameworks; Jeanne Ross's 'Designed for Digital' on enterprise data capability
incomplete
The article frames knowledge opacity as a problem to solve, not as rational behavior driven by real incentive problems. The construction firm example shows that project managers had strong reasons to maintain opacity (managing client expectations, personal protection). But the article doesn't ask: what incentive structures need to change to make L2 sustainable, not just temporarily forced?
#2: The Unsexy Truth of AI Adoption — read next: Cutler's TBM 414 (legitimacy question); organizational economics literature on information asymmetry; Zuboff on surveillance capitalism
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The article mentions 'AI compresses distance between enterprises and small teams' as a key topic but this section is paywalled. This is potentially the most actionable insight for small organizations.
#2: The Unsexy Truth of AI Adoption — read next: Paul Graham's essays on startups vs. enterprises; Ben Thompson on the 'Aggregation Theory' and how AI changes the size/capability frontier
Small Teams & Compression
uninformedclosed
The framework doesn't address the labor implications of making organizational knowledge explicit. The bookkeeping and construction examples show that legibility removes cover for informal power — but whose interests does that serve? When tacit knowledge is codified, what happens to the people whose value was in holding it?
#2: The Unsexy Truth of AI Adoption — read next: David Graeber's 'Bullshit Jobs' (on the social value of informational gatekeeping); Zuboff's 'The Age of Surveillance Capitalism'
✓ closed byThe Agentic P&L: Beyond the Empire of Headcount — Answers what happens to the people whose value was holding tacit knowledge once it is codified: knowledge is synthesized into agent-readable enclaves, labor contracts sharply (400 to 80-100), and span-of-control empires are dismantled (p7, p10, p13).
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The piece argues the subsidy era is ending but never addresses what happens to the vast user base that was acquired through subsidized pricing. Will usage-based billing cause churn, consolidation around cheaper models, or a bifurcation between power users and casual users? The demand-is-infinite claim and the end-of-subsidies claim are in tension — if prices rise substantially, demand may not be as infinite as claimed.
The Week AI Grew Up — read next: Benedict Evans or Ben Thompson on AI pricing elasticity; economic analysis of SaaS-to-usage transitions in cloud computing history
AI Unit Economics
incomplete
The claim that knowledge workers will choose to become more technical (favoring Codex over Cowork) is asserted without evidence. Historical precedent suggests the opposite — most technology adoption succeeds by lowering the technical bar, not raising it.
The Week AI Grew Up — read next: Clayton Christensen on 'good enough' technology; history of no-code/low-code adoption
uninformed
The piece treats the AI industry's 'growing up' entirely from a US-centric perspective. No mention of Chinese AI labs, EU regulation, or non-Western demand patterns, despite framing AI as 'critical global economic infrastructure.'
The Week AI Grew Up — read next: Matt Sheehan on China's AI ecosystem; EU AI Act implementation updates

evidential
No evidence or examples are provided for the effectiveness of Duke's decision-making methods or the course format. The email relies entirely on the reader's prior familiarity with Duke's work.
Ask Me Anything! — read next: Read 'Thinking in Bets' (book) or Duke's other Substack posts for the underlying framework.
Decision-Making Practices
specificity
The email offers no specifics about course content, methodology, curriculum, or learning outcomes. 'Practical ways' and 'better calibrated' are the only descriptors.
Ask Me Anything! — read next: The Maven course page (linked) likely has a detailed syllabus.
audience
No indication of who this course is for (experience level, profession, prior knowledge). The implicit assumption is that the Substack audience self-selects.
Ask Me Anything! — read next: Check Maven course listing for prerequisites or target audience.
implicit premise
The framework assumes a sufficiently large and complex system to justify the investment. The 'when this is overkill' section is brief (one paragraph) and provides no quantitative thresholds for when the investment begins to pay off.
Three Pillars of Platform Engineering: A Virtuous Cycle
missing evidence
The entire argument is grounded in author experience ('patterns I've seen work repeatedly') with no cited case studies, metrics, or controlled comparisons. The only external reference is to Martin Kleppmann on distributed locking, which is tangential.
Three Pillars of Platform Engineering: A Virtuous Cycle
unexamined tension
Opinionated SDKs are presented as unambiguously positive, but the trade-off between opinionation and flexibility is not explored. Teams with genuinely novel requirements may find absorbed patterns constraining rather than enabling.
Three Pillars of Platform Engineering: A Virtuous Cycle
missing perspective
The economic dimension is absent: there is no discussion of the cost of building and maintaining a control plane, opinionated SDKs, and layered observability — or how to prioritize investment when resources are constrained.
Three Pillars of Platform Engineering: A Virtuous Cycle
Platform Engineering
implicit premise
The virtuous-cycle argument assumes that freed-up operator bandwidth will actually be invested back into platform improvement. In practice, organizations may redirect that bandwidth to other priorities, breaking the cycle.
Three Pillars of Platform Engineering: A Virtuous Cycle
Platform Engineering
incomplete
The '3-4x faster' adoption claim is stated without supporting data, methodology, or definition of what metric is being measured. What specific growth metrics are being compared?
Podcast: Tiger Teams, Evals and Agents: The New AI Engineering Playbook — read next: Look for empirical studies comparing technology adoption curves (e.g., Thoughtworks Technology Radar data, Stack Overflow surveys, or job posting analysis).
incomplete
No discussion of how to determine appropriate accuracy thresholds for different risk levels. The framework jumps from 'build evals' to 'hit your threshold' without addressing how to set the threshold.

uninformedclosed
No mention of adversarial testing, security evals, or prompt injection risks for customer-facing agentic applications. The HR SaaS example involves sensitive data but security is only mentioned as a motivation (CSV-to-ChatGPT privacy concern), not as an eval category.

incomplete
The Tiger Team concept is described but there is no discussion of how Tiger Teams dissolve back into the organization, how knowledge transfers, or how the org evolves its permanent structure based on what Tiger Teams learn.

misinformed
The distinction between vibe coding and building agentic applications is drawn too sharply. In practice, development workflow agents (the Discord-to-GitHub-issue agent described) are themselves agentic applications that need evals, blurring the boundary Bhagwat draws.
Podcast: Tiger Teams, Evals and Agents: The New AI Engineering Playbook — read next: Inner loop / outer loop development frameworks; the concept of 'dogfooding' applied to AI agent development.
Agentic AIAgentic LoopEvalsVibe Coding
incomplete
The piece asserts 'decline' in the title but argues only for a personal travel choice. The civilizational thesis — that America is in decline — is gestured at, not defended. A reader sympathetic to the personal decision still gets no argument that this episode is symptomatic of a larger fall rather than a single-administration anomaly.
Declining America — read next: Pair with longer-form diagnoses of US decline (e.g., institutional-decay writers, comparative democratic-backsliding literature) to see whether Bray's tacit framework holds up.
incomplete
The implicit premise 'attendance confers legitimacy' is never defended. There is a serious counter-tradition — that engagement with flawed states is precisely how skeptical voices do their work — which Bray does not address.
Declining America — read next: Read against the 'engagement vs. boycott' debate in academic, scientific, and dissident traditions; consider how AI skeptics in particular weigh presence vs. absence.
incomplete
No discussion of remote participation. The 21st-century alternative — joining the unconference by video, contributing in writing, sending a paper — is conspicuously absent, even though it would let Bray's 'unbeliever contribution' reach the room without the border risk.
Declining America — read next: Look at how distributed/hybrid professional gatherings handle politically constrained participants; this is a Cutler-adjacent organizational design question.
incomplete
The border-risk argument is asserted as 'significant risk of an extremely negative outcome' without evidence or calibration. For a writer this careful, the absence of any base rate or case is notable; the reader is asked to take the probability on trust.
Declining America — read next: Reporting on US border social-media screening practices (EFF, Knight Institute, recent journalism on detentions of writers/academics) for actual frequencies and patterns.
Trust
incomplete
The piece is silent on the asymmetry it creates: the people most likely to self-exclude are precisely those whose skeptical AI contributions are most needed in US-hosted rooms. Bray notes the gathering wanted 'unbeliever contributions' but does not reckon with what his absence — multiplied — does to the discourse he cares about.
Declining America — read next: Read alongside discussions of self-censorship effects and how skeptic exit reshapes communities (Hirschman's Exit, Voice, and Loyalty is the classic frame).
AI BacklashChilling Effects
incomplete
The load-bearing implicit premise — that voluntary action by incumbents, labs, and self-starting individuals can deliver the mechanism design and tidying required — goes undefended. Government, regulators, and organized labor appear only as obstacles (DOGE as wrecking ball, Kaiser nurses as bargaining problem), never as design partners. This narrows the solution space to a Silicon Valley-friendly subset.
The Tidy House — read next: Mariana Mazzucato on the entrepreneurial/mission-driven state; Daron Acemoglu & Simon Johnson 'Power and Progress' on who captures the surplus from general-purpose technologies; recent labor-side accounts of AI rollout (Wendy Liu, Brian Merchant).
DORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
Devoted is used as the load-bearing exemplar without examining selection effects: it was founded by a chief-data-scientist-grade leader with capital, on a fresh codebase, in a payer model with strong economic incentives for clean data. The piece does not address how a 40-year-old hospital with EHR debt and 30% volume drops gets to the tidy house from here.
The Tidy House — read next: Healthcare IT case studies on EHR modernization (e.g., Epic-on-FHIR transitions); writings on technical-debt-laden AI adoption (Will Larson, Charity Majors on infra readiness); McKinsey/Bain healthcare AI deployment reports for contrast.
Enterprise AI Adoption & Readiness
incomplete
The 'layers above the model' thesis is asserted as a strategic forecast but not differentiated from prior hype cycles (each platform era has claimed value would accrue above it). The conditions under which model-layer commoditization actually occurs in 2026 are not examined — particularly given vertical integration trends and shrinking margins above commodity inference.
The Tidy House — read next: Recent writing on harness/orchestration economics (Simon Willison, Will Larson on platform engineering); Ben Thompson on aggregation vs. integration in AI; the Anthropic / OpenAI app-layer move (e.g., Skills, agents).
Platform Engineering
incomplete
'Mechanism design' is used loosely — slipping between an economist's formal sense (incentive-compatible institutions) and a technologist's informal sense ('design good defaults'). The article doesn't distinguish which kinds of failures voluntary mechanism design can solve and which require collective enforcement.
The Tidy House — read next: Hurwicz/Myerson on mechanism design; Elinor Ostrom on commons governance; Lessig 'Code is Law' for the techno-determinist counterweight.
incomplete
Labor's role is undertheorized. Kaiser nurses appear as an example of resistance triggered by bad lab narrative — but unions could equally be co-designers of the 'use AI to remove BS, not workers' bargain the piece advocates. The asymmetry — labs need persuading via mechanism design, labor needs persuading via better narrative — is unexamined.
The Tidy House — read next: Writers Guild AI agreement post-mortems; Daron Acemoglu interviews on shared prosperity; Aaron Benanav on automation discourse vs. labor reality.
uninformedclosed
The piece doesn't engage with the 2025-2026 wave of empirical evidence on AI displacement at the entry-level junior tier (e.g., entry-level coding role contraction data), beyond the anecdote about internship callback rates. The strength of the 'broken promise' diagnosis would benefit from numbers, not just stories.
The Tidy House — read next: Recent BLS / Brookings / NBER papers on AI labor-market effects; Anthropic's economic-impact research; HBR coverage of AI-and-juniors.

AI Labor Disruption
unstated assumption
No discussion of the performance and cost implications of per-request token issuance at scale.
Why Token Handling Must Evolve for AI Agents — read next: Research on token-service throughput benchmarks and latency budgets for per-call issuance
open problem
Agent identity is treated as a given rather than an open problem. Reliable agent identity is a prerequisite for context-aware authorization, yet current standards (including MCP) do not fully specify how to authenticate and distinguish agents.
Why Token Handling Must Evolve for AI Agents
Agentic AI
missing evidenceclosed
No agent-specific breach data is presented; all cited breaches involve human credential misuse. The extrapolation from human-credential breaches to agent-credential risk is plausible but unsubstantiated.
Why Token Handling Must Evolve for AI Agents
✓ closed byBreaking: Autonomous Agents are a Shitshow — Supplies the missing agent-specific breach evidence: 91% tool-chaining vulnerability, 94% memory-poisoning vulnerability, and the OpenClaw/Moltbook incident compromising 770,000 agents.
Agentic AI
bias
The article is partly a promotional vehicle for Curity's Access Intelligence product but does not disclose the commercial relationship or compare alternative solutions.
Why Token Handling Must Evolve for AI Agents
incomplete
No general evidence for 'compounding correctness.' The single data point (AISI/Mythos: no diminishing returns across tested budgets) is narrow security exploit-discovery, where raw compute plausibly substitutes for correctness in a way it may not for open-ended coding loops. The regime-shift claim ('spending more tokens generally results in better results') is generalized from that one benchmark and from vibes.
Agentics / Tech Things: Tokenmaxxing is dead, long live tokenmaxxing — read next: Look for benchmark data on whether iterative agent loops monotonically improve on general coding/knowledge tasks versus plateau or degrade; read the test-time / inference-time compute scaling literature (o-series scaling curves) and any controlled loop-vs-single-shot comparisons.
AI Regulation & Governance (External)Mythos
incomplete
Cost is quantified, value is asserted. Every number in the piece is a cost (token prices, $12.5k/attempt, $600/month, $1000/day); the productivity or quality VALUE that would justify maximal spend is never measured, only hedged ('ostensibly'). The essay therefore cannot tell marginal from transformative benefit.
Agentics / Tech Things: Tokenmaxxing is dead, long live tokenmaxxing — read next: Read HBR's 'How People Are Really Using AI in 2026' / the 'workslop'/'thinkslop' findings (marginal-not-game-changing benefits) and controlled productivity studies; ask whether the dev-loop value curve is transformative or merely marginal against unsubsidized cost.
incomplete
No mechanism for the regime shift. The author asserts 'That's no longer true. We've entered a different regime' but never explains what technically flipped compounding error into compounding correctness — better base models? harness-level self-verification? evals in the loop? RL-from-verifiable-rewards? The rebirth's causal engine is a black box.
Agentics / Tech Things: Tokenmaxxing is dead, long live tokenmaxxing — read next: Read on agent self-verification and eval-in-the-loop harness design, RLVR / verifier-based training, and what actually reduces error accumulation in long runs; distinguish model-capability gains from harness/scaffolding gains.
Agent HarnessEvals
incomplete
Vendor-vs-user value and the author's own incentive alignment are unexamined. 'More tokens = better' is exactly the narrative that benefits whoever sells tokens or AI-adoption services — and the author runs such a service, soliciting business throughout ('reach out, we can help!'). Who bears the cost (users, post-subsidy) versus who captures the value (vendors, consultants) is never separated, and the conflict is undisclosed.
Agentics / Tech Things: Tokenmaxxing is dead, long live tokenmaxxing — read next: Read critically for supply-push vs demand-pull: who profits from maximal token consumption; compare against sources arguing 'most effective users are not the heaviest users'; watch for the token-seller framing in any pro-spend argument.
incomplete
The two-kinds distinction is not integrated with the rebirth. The author says compounding correctness rescues developer loops but that pipeline agents are waste; he never reconciles whether compounding correctness would also rescue pipelines, or whether the generalist-platform shift moots custom pipelines regardless. The two 'this too shall pass' stories run in parallel without a unifying account.
Agentics / Tech Things: Tokenmaxxing is dead, long live tokenmaxxing — read next: Trace whether reliability gains (compounding correctness) and consolidation gains (generalist platforms replacing bespoke agents) are the same phenomenon or two; read on why deterministic pipelines still beat agents for fixed high-accuracy tasks.
missing evidenceclosed
The article does not detail what specific cybersecurity exploits Mythos demonstrated, making it hard to assess the proportionality of the government's reaction.
The Trump administration's AI doomer moment — read next: National security reporting on AI-enabled cyber capabilities; Anthropic's own responsible scaling disclosures.
✓ closed byNHS England rushes to hide software over AI hacking fears — Reports the UK AI Security Institute's evaluation of Mythos — capable only of attacking small, weakly defended, vulnerable enterprise systems — supplying the capability detail needed to assess the proportionality of the government's reaction.
Mythos
unasked question
No discussion of whether other frontier models (from Google, OpenAI, xAI) have similar capabilities — the narrative is anchored entirely on Anthropic/Mythos, suggesting the policy shift may be broader than one model.
The Trump administration's AI doomer moment — read next: Comparative assessments of frontier model capabilities across labs; government red-team evaluations.

unasked question
The piece does not explore whether the 'supply chain risk' designation against Anthropic was legally sound or politically motivated beyond noting the Pentagon contract dispute.

scope limitation
