AI Regulation & Governance (External)
implicit premise
The article assumes the pre-release review mechanism will function effectively but does not examine the Commerce Department's actual capacity to evaluate frontier models — a critical institutional capacity question.
The Trump administration's AI doomer moment — read next: NIST/Commerce Department AI evaluation capabilities; UK AISI's experience with model evaluation.
AI Regulation & Governance (External)Riding Model Improvements
tension
Casey Newton discloses that his fiancee works at Anthropic but does not explore how this shapes the article's Anthropic-centric framing — the piece treats Anthropic as the protagonist of the policy reversal.
The Trump administration's AI doomer moment — read next: Coverage of the same events from non-Anthropic-adjacent journalists for comparison.
incomplete
Trust is treated as one undifferentiated resource. Trust between paired programmers, trust from the rest of the team, trust from the customer, trust in the system (observability) — Beck collapses these. But they have different production functions, different decay rates, and may not be substitutable. A skeptic might point out that customer trust is far more about delivered value than pairing rituals, while pair-trust is largely independent of customer outcomes.
Trust Factory — read next: Janson + Wester on pair-trust (Heis ep 6 — 'if something feels off, say it once'); Printzell Halvorsen on Nordic societal trust enabling distributed teams (Heis ep 8); Onora O'Neill on the trust-trustworthiness distinction and on different kinds of trust requiring different verification structures.
Trust
incompleteclosed
No treatment of misplaced trust, trust-in-bad-actors, or trust-as-attack-surface. The essay frames trust as monotonically good. But trust without verification is the prompt-injection vulnerability, the rogue-employee vector, the 'too-much-trust' problem. Anders Sveen's argument (Heis ep 11) that LLM tool-use authorization can't rely on trust because the agent can be prompt-injected applies here.
Trust Factory — read next: Sveen on AI authorization (Heis ep 11); zero-trust architecture literature; Schneier on trust models (already in the user's RSS); Annie Duke on resulting / decision quality independent of trust.
✓ closed byRunning Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — Willison provides the missing treatment of misplaced trust: do not trust agent code at all — contain it at an execution boundary and cut the exfiltration leg, since sandboxing (not trust) is the answer to prompt-injected or wrong-but-sanctioned actions.
Trust
incomplete
The 'trust factory' metaphor is endorsed without examining what it concedes. 'Factory' implies trust is manufactured by repeating standardized processes — but a major tradition (cultural, philosophical, theological) treats trust as precisely the kind of thing that cannot be manufactured. Beck does not engage this objection. The metaphor's industrial framing also obscures that XP practices may have worked because they instantiated values the practitioners already held — not because the practices produced the values.
Trust Factory — read next: Cutler on operating-system design vs cultural pre-conditions (TBM series); Diego Gambetta on trust as a non-manufacturable resource; the 'culture eats process for breakfast' literature.
Trust
incomplete
The four 'slow development' prescriptions are stated but not operationalized for the augmented case. How exactly do you 'slow development to encourage frequent person-to-person interaction' when the genie is so seductive? What are the equivalent of XP practices for the augmented era? Beck explicitly defers this ('not yet settled') but a reader looking for action is left with a principle, not a practice.
Trust Factory — read next: Beck's own subsequent essays (the 'XPAI' thread he's hinting at); Birgitta Böckeler on harness engineering; Garg on reducing friction in AI development (both already in the user's extracted corpus); Bockeler/Anthropic harness writing.
Agent HarnessThe Genie (Beck)Learning Culture & Slack
incomplete
The cultural specificity of trust is not addressed. XP emerged in a high-trust professional subculture (Chrysler C3, early agilists). Whether the trust factory works in low-trust organizational contexts — or only metabolizes pre-existing trust — is left untested. Same question applies to whether trust factories scale across cultural and national boundaries.
Trust Factory — read next: Printzell Halvorsen on Nordic high-trust societies enabling distributed teams (Heis ep 8); Fukuyama on high-trust vs low-trust societies; cross-cultural management literature.
Trust
incomplete
The implicit failure model is not made explicit. The patterns are pitched as a coherent system but the specific breakdowns they prevent are scattered: context loss after a 4-hour crash (Pattern 1), reasoning-context loss on HITL serialization (Pattern 2), behavioral drift and cross-workflow data leakage (Pattern 3), policy-version skew across an unsupervised fleet (Pattern 4), LLM-shortcutting orchestration logic and cascade-failure deploys (Pattern 5), and bespoke integration tax (A2A/MCP). A reader looking for 'which pattern solves which specific production breakdown' has to reverse-engineer the mapping.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Read Reed's 'Ironies of A²I²' (Bainbridge revisited) for the failure-modes-of-automation framing the post is implicitly using but never names; pair with Grab's multi-agent engineering-support post for a concrete incident catalog from an actual production deployment.
Human-in-the-LoopMulti-Agent Orchestration
incomplete
Human throughput is treated as a non-issue. The post mentions 'managing twenty concurrent long-running agents' and a 'unified inbox' but never confronts the reality that the human review queue itself is the binding constraint — the central thesis of Osmani's own 'Orchestration Tax is You' piece. Pattern 2's elegant in-place pause optimizes the agent side of the handoff while the human side scales linearly with agent count.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Read Osmani's 'Orchestration Tax is You' alongside this article — same author, opposite end of the telescope. Together they expose the gap: 'pause in place' is necessary but not sufficient; without batching, prioritization, and judgement-content sorting, the agent fleet just queues faster in front of the human bottleneck.
Agentic AIHuman as Orchestrator of AgentsHuman-in-the-LoopLong-Running & Managed Agents
incomplete
Memory drift is named but not operationally addressed. Governance prevents unauthorized writes; it does not detect agents whose accumulated long-term memory is subtly warping their reasoning. The post stops at IAM-for-memory; it does not propose drift detection, periodic memory audits, or rollback mechanisms — and gives no guidance on memory garbage collection over multi-week timescales.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Karpathy's 'LLM knowledge bases' extraction (in this library) and Karpathy's 'kb-tool built' piece for the orthogonal view that memory is a tool with editable artifacts, not a black-box store; revisit Bainbridge/Reed for why 'silently changing behavior' is the failure mode automation literature has studied for forty years.
Agent MemoryKnowledge Base
incomplete
Framework-neutrality is asserted in form (the patterns) but undermined in substance. The code uses google.adk; the only named runtime is Google Cloud Agent Runtime; the CTA is the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform; A2A is Google-backed; the seven-day persistence number is a vendor capability claim. A reader trying to implement these patterns on LangGraph, the Anthropic SDK, OpenAI Assistants, Mastra, or a homegrown stack has to translate every prescription — and several (notably 'zero-compute pause' and 'sub-second cold start') are runtime-dependent guarantees, not just design choices.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Read 'Anthropic's three-agent harness' and 'Anthropic harness design for long-running application development' for how the same problem space looks from the SDK-and-harness perspective rather than the managed-runtime perspective; read 'Block Goose' and 'OpenClaw setup' for OSS-stack equivalents.
Agent HarnessDORA & Engineering MetricsLong-Running & Managed AgentsOpenClaw
uninformed
The centralized governance layer is presented as an unalloyed good. No mention of the centralization risk: a policy engine in the hot path of every memory and tool access is both a high-availability requirement (downtime stalls the entire agent fleet) and a high-value attack surface (compromise = read/write to every memory bank). The post inherits microservices-governance vocabulary without inheriting microservices-governance failure modes.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Schneier and the supply-chain attack extractions in this library (CNCF Kusari, OpenClaw malicious skills) for the adversarial framing; classic site-reliability literature on policy-engine SPoFs.
Agent Governance (Internal)Agent MemoryHuman as Orchestrator of AgentsOpenClaw
incomplete
The diagnostic question ('what is the longest uninterrupted unit of work?') is only directional. It does not help a team decide whether a 6-hour workflow is better as one long-running agent or as four 90-minute agents handing off through a queue. The 'compose-or-decompose' question is the real architectural choice and is unanswered.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Kniberg's 'Multi-agent workflows' and 'Anthropic harness design' for the decomposition-vs-persistence tradeoff worked out in opposing directions.
Agentic AILong-Running & Managed AgentsMulti-Agent Orchestration
illogical
The competitive-advantage claim — 'companies building stateless agents today will be refactoring in twelve months' — is offered as if self-evident but rests on the very premise the article is selling: that multi-day workflows are the workloads that matter. For organizations whose agentic workloads are genuinely bounded (single-session research, short bursts of tool use), the refactoring prediction does not follow.
The Production Gap: 5 Patterns for Building Long-Running AI Agents — read next: Treat the closing prediction as marketing prose; the diagnostic question two paragraphs earlier is the honest version.
paywall truncation
Principles 3-5 (expert loops before administrative layers; organize around outcomes not handoffs; install evaluation/permissions/review from the start) are behind a paywall. The analysis is necessarily incomplete.
#3: How to Build an AI-Native Startup from Day One — read next: Upgrade to Turing Post premium or find coverage of the full article elsewhere.
AI-Native Company
missing evidence
The McKinsey 2025 survey is cited for the claim that workflow redesign drives EBIT impact, but no link, sample size, or effect size is given. The leap from enterprise survey data to startup prescriptions is not justified.
#3: How to Build an AI-Native Startup from Day One — read next: Look up the actual McKinsey 2025 generative AI survey to assess whether the findings support startup-specific conclusions.
AI Workflow Patterns
unexamined assumption
The article assumes the documentation overhead of machine-legibility is worth it for startups, but early-stage startups are notoriously resource-constrained. No cost-benefit framework is offered for deciding what to externalize and what to leave tacit.
#3: How to Build an AI-Native Startup from Day One — read next: Look for empirical work on documentation burden in small teams, or case studies of AI-native startups that quantify the overhead.
AI-Native CompanyOrganizational LegibilitySmall Teams & Compression
unexamined assumption
The article treats 'flatter organizational structures' as a natural and desirable consequence of cheaper intelligence, without examining whether this prediction holds or whether flattening carries risks (e.g., loss of mentorship, decision overload on fewer people).
#3: How to Build an AI-Native Startup from Day One — read next: Research on organizational delayering and its effects on decision quality and employee development.
Organizational Legibility
conceptual gap
The framing 'the organization itself becomes part of the product surface' is evocative but underdeveloped. It is unclear whether this means organizational design is visible to customers, or is a competitive moat, or something else.
#3: How to Build an AI-Native Startup from Day One — read next: Look for related work on organizational design as competitive advantage (e.g., Zenger on theory-based strategy, or Jacobides on architectural advantage).
incomplete
The article does not address how to diagnose which of the three needs applies in real time. The taxonomy is presented, but the recognition heuristics are thin — you would need to read context clues, but the article gives little guidance on what those clues are or what to do when the signals are ambiguous.
When an Executive Asks You an Unexpected Question — read next: Literature on executive communication patterns, or Chris Voss's 'Never Split the Difference' on reading implicit needs in conversation
Prompting as Communication
incomplete
The framework assumes a one-to-one mapping between questions and needs, but real executive questions often blend needs — an exec might want both reassurance and guidance simultaneously. The article does not address mixed or shifting needs.
When an Executive Asks You an Unexpected Question — read next: Research on conversational dynamics and need-shifting in organizational communication
Managing Up & InfluencePrompting as Communication
incomplete
The article places 100% of the communication burden on the person being questioned. It never considers whether executives should be trained to ask more direct questions, or whether organizational culture could reduce the indirectness.
When an Executive Asks You an Unexpected Question — read next: Kim Scott's 'Radical Candor' or organizational transparency literature for the complementary perspective
uninformed
No empirical evidence or research is cited for the three-category framework. It is presented as practitioner wisdom without grounding in communication research, psychology of questioning, or organizational behavior studies.
When an Executive Asks You an Unexpected Question — read next: Academic literature on question types and communication pragmatics, e.g., speech act theory (Searle) or the SPIN selling framework's question taxonomy for a parallel practitioner model
incomplete
This is a promotional newsletter, not an analytical piece. Only one Thinkie (Can't/Because) is demonstrated out of the claimed ~100. The definition of Thinkies is given but no taxonomy, no theory of why they work, and no evidence of effectiveness beyond one dialogue.
Unstick Your Stuck Thinking — read next: Kent Beck's book or course materials on Thinkies; TWC content if available; related work on lateral thinking (de Bono), reframing (Watzlawick), or cognitive flexibility
Meta-Skills & Contextual Judgment
incomplete
The piece does not distinguish Thinkies from existing frameworks for creative/lateral thinking (de Bono's lateral thinking hats, TRIZ contradiction analysis, Socratic questioning). It is unclear what is novel vs. repackaged.
Unstick Your Stuck Thinking — read next: Compare with de Bono's Six Thinking Hats, TRIZ inventive principles, and Socratic method literature
Meta-Skills & Contextual Judgment
incomplete
The Can't/Because example conflates correlation with causation without flagging it — 'can't X because Y' is treated as a causal claim to be solved, but no attention is given to whether Y actually causes not-X.
Unstick Your Stuck Thinking — read next: This may be intentional (the point is to generate ideas, not validate causation), but the piece does not acknowledge the distinction
uninformed
The load-bearing premise — that process/behavioural addiction is equivalent in kind to substance addiction — is asserted but never defended. She imports Modum Bad's substance-addiction six signs and a cocaine analogy onto a phone without noting that 'social media addiction' is not an established clinical diagnosis the way gambling disorder is (gambling disorder is in DSM-5/ICD-11; gaming disorder only recently and contestedly; social-media 'addiction' not at all). The category transfer that the whole argument rides on is treated as obvious.
Uten kontroll — read next: DSM-5 / ICD-11 status of behavioural/process addictions; the debate over pathologising heavy engagement (Billieux et al. on over-diagnosis; Andreassen's Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale as a proposed instrument and its critics); whether compulsive use is best modelled as addiction, habit, or coping.
incomplete
The entire argument rests on a single self-diagnosed case, yet she generalises explicitly to 'the young' and to a whole generation. She concedes she is unusually addiction-prone, which makes her a poor base-rate for the population she extrapolates to; the organised-activities anecdote is a second data point, not evidence of prevalence.
Uten kontroll — read next: Prevalence studies of problematic/compulsive social-media use among youth; base rates of behavioural addiction; whether her addiction-prone profile is representative or a tail case.
incomplete
The design-causes-the-compulsion thesis (and its corollary that structure beats willpower) is inferred from one reversal, not demonstrated. She names 'de korte videoene' and the phone's indispensability as the cause but gives no mechanism (variable-ratio reward, algorithmic ranking, autoplay, infinite scroll) and no evidence that design rather than her disposition is doing the work.
Uten kontroll — read next: Attention-economy and persuasive-design literature — Hari 'Stolen Focus', Chris Bailey on attention as a managed resource, Tristan Harris / Center for Humane Technology, and especially Natasha Dow Schull 'Addiction by Design' on variable-ratio machine design as the closest analogue to feed mechanics.
Attention as a Hijackable Resource
incomplete
The durability of the fix is unexamined. She concedes she is not cured and relapse is close, but the two-phone protocol is validated only by a short, self-reported window; there is no evidence it holds over time, generalises to people without her motivation or income, or survives the temptation to uninstall the limiter.
Uten kontroll — read next: Relapse/maintenance literature for behavioural addictions; longitudinal evaluations of digital self-control tools (do app-limiters durably change behaviour or get bypassed?); the second-phone tactic as a privileged workaround vs. a scalable one.
Attention as a Hijackable Resource
incomplete
The proposed better research question — 'voluntary or an addiction?' — comes with no operationalisation. She does not say how the voluntary/addicted distinction would be measured at population scale, and reframing a null result post hoc around a new, unmeasured construct risks unfalsifiability (any null becomes 'you measured the wrong thing').
Uten kontroll — read next: Construct-validity methodology; validated behavioural-addiction screening instruments and their psychometrics; whether 'loss of control' can be measured without circularity at scale.
incomplete
Locus-of-remedy mismatch: she diagnoses engineered design as the cause but places the entire remedy on individuals (her own hack) and on parents, never on platform design, defaults, or regulation. If capture is engineered, individual friction is treating a systemic cause with a personal workaround — and one that presumes you can afford and coordinate a second device.
Uten kontroll — read next: Regulatory and design-level approaches: EU Digital Services Act provisions on addictive design, age-verification and 'addictive feed' bans (New York, California), and the live Norwegian debate over an aldersgrense for social media; systems-thinking framing of where leverage actually sits when the cause is structural.
incomplete
Ontology maintenance and curation cost is entirely unaddressed. The post treats the ontology as a layer that 'supplies' semantic memory, but says nothing about who authors the shared formal vocabulary, how it is governed as many notes link into it, or that this is scarce knowledge-engineering labour. If maintaining the ontology is the hard part, the bottleneck moves from markdown convenience to specialist curation — undercutting the linked 'simplest thing that could possibly work' framing.
Tony Seale's Post — read next: Read the historical adoption record of formal ontologies and the Semantic Web (OWL/RDF, schema.org governance) for why ontology upkeep, not authoring notes, is the recurring failure point. Compare with this repo's own knowledge/ layer, which runs the same episodic+semantic split but deliberately diverges from Vault-LD: it keeps the ontology in an external, human-curated registry (concepts.yaml) rather than inline YAML-LD, and uses positional refs (slug#p3) rather than linked-data URIs. That a working instance of the pattern chose an external registry is direct evidence that inline 'one file, each layer optional' is not the only viable architecture — and that someone still has to own the semantic layer by hand.
Agent MemoryOrganizational Legibility
incomplete
The inference payoff is asserted but never demonstrated. 'Project into a graph, where inference, validation, query and integration are already waiting' names four benefits without a single worked example of what an agent can now answer, or what a validation constraint would catch, that a plain markdown wiki could not. The most consequential claim in the piece — that the ontology overhead buys real capability — is left as a promise.
Tony Seale's Post — read next: Seek the linked Vault-LD spec for a concrete example, then pressure-test it against the counter-claim in the corpus (Karpathy: an LLM-maintained markdown index is sufficient for complex Q&A at ~100-article scale without any graph). Ask specifically: what query, inference or validation justifies the ontology, and at what vault size does flat markdown actually break?
Decision-Making PracticesKnowledge BaseOrganizational Legibility
incomplete
The net-positive assumption skips the sync/drift ledger. Keeping typed fields and ontology links in agreement with prose as the prose evolves is ongoing cost, and 'each layer optional until you need it' in practice tends to mean 'skipped' — which leaves the graph sparse exactly where semantics were supposed to add value, and leaves the annotated minority prone to silent divergence from the text it describes.
Tony Seale's Post — read next: Read on documentation drift / doc-rot and on the divergence between described and enforced structure over time (cf. the static-sensors debate on markdown guides vs enforcement rules). Consider whether YAML-LD frontmatter has the same rot dynamics as any duplicated metadata, and what would keep it honest.
Organizational Legibility
incomplete
The JSON-LD-on-HTML analogy imports an inevitability it has not earned. The web's structured-data adoption was driven by a concrete incentive — search engines reward JSON-LD with ranking — and is itself uneven and gamed. The post identifies no equivalent forcing function for private or team vaults, yet treats 'the same move' as a settled outcome rather than a hopeful one.
Tony Seale's Post — read next: Examine schema.org adoption data and its SEO dynamics — how much of the web's 'knowledge graph' is genuine semantics versus ranking-driven boilerplate — and ask what a vault-world analogue of search-ranking pressure would even be. If none exists, the adoption story needs a different engine than the analogy provides.
Knowledge Base
incomplete
No treatment of the trustworthiness of the memory itself. Agent memory is largely LLM-written; a hallucinated or drifting note, once given typed fields and ontology links, inherits machine-queryable false authority. The semantic layer is presented purely as validation upside, with no acknowledgement that it propagates bad facts as readily as it structures good ones, or that memory poisoning becomes more consequential once memory is formally queried.
Tony Seale's Post — read next: Read on agent-memory poisoning and knowledge-base trust (how errors propagate through an LLM-maintained base over time). Distinguish validation that catches contradictions from structure that merely launders unverified claims into confident graph facts.
Agent MemoryAgent SecurityOrganizational LegibilityTrust
incomplete
The article presents curl's verification infrastructure as the exemplar but does not address the resource asymmetry: curl is a well-funded, high-profile project with a dedicated lead developer. Most open-source projects lack the resources to implement 200+ CI jobs, continuous fuzzing, and dedicated security tooling. The transferability of this model to the long tail of open-source dependencies is unexamined.
Leading Open Source Author Calls for Verification over Trust in Software Supply Chains — read next: Look for work on scalable verification for under-resourced projects — e.g., OpenSSF Scorecard, Sigstore for automated signing, or community-funded security auditing.
incomplete
Stenberg's 'few independent verifiers' argument rests on an unexamined incentive problem: who are these verifiers, what motivates them, and how do they sustain the practice? The article acknowledges Stenberg 'cannot know who those users are, or whether they currently exist' but does not explore the fragility of relying on unpaid, uncoordinated volunteers.
Leading Open Source Author Calls for Verification over Trust in Software Supply Chains — read next: Look for research on verification-as-a-service models, paid auditing, or how the reproducible builds movement addresses the volunteer-verifier problem.
incomplete
The article synthesises Stenberg's argument with Petersson's SBOM advocacy and the GitHub Actions incident, but does not address the coherence gap between these approaches. SBOMs describe what is in the software; Stenberg's verification checks that the source matches the release; CI pipeline security prevents build-time tampering. These are complementary but the article does not articulate how they compose into a unified verification strategy.
Leading Open Source Author Calls for Verification over Trust in Software Supply Chains — read next: Look for end-to-end supply chain security frameworks (e.g., SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) that integrate source, build, and distribution verification.
Software Supply-Chain Security
absent
The article does not discuss the cost-benefit tradeoff of verification. For organisations consuming hundreds or thousands of dependencies, verifying each one to curl's standard may be impractical. There is no discussion of risk-based prioritisation — which dependencies warrant full verification and which can be handled with lighter checks.
Leading Open Source Author Calls for Verification over Trust in Software Supply Chains — read next: Look for risk-based dependency management frameworks and dependency criticality scoring (e.g., OpenSSF Criticality Score).
Verifiability (as the Quality Bar)
incomplete
The newsletter never names the specific retirement rule it references in the headline. This is the central claim of the entire email — 'this rule could change that' — but the reader must click through to Sheffield's article to learn what the rule actually is. The newsletter is structurally unable to deliver on its own promise.
The Week in Ideas: Countless Americans face a scary retirement. This rule could change that. — read next: Read the full Carrie Sheffield article on the retirement rule — likely about the DOL fiduciary rule, automatic 401(k) enrollment under SECURE 2.0, or fee transparency requirements
incomplete
The editorial curation implicitly argues that American systems are failing, but the newsletter format prevents any engagement with counterarguments or the question of whether these are isolated failures or symptoms of a deeper institutional crisis.
The Week in Ideas: Countless Americans face a scary retirement. This rule could change that. — read next: Read each linked article individually for the actual arguments; consider whether the editorial selection represents a coherent worldview or just aggregation
incomplete
The overdose-deaths-plummeting piece is presented alongside the failures, but it's actually a success story. The newsletter doesn't explore the tension: if one system (overdose response) improved, what made it different from the ones that haven't?
The Week in Ideas: Countless Americans face a scary retirement. This rule could change that. — read next: Read Sam Quinones's piece on what worked in overdose reduction — the mechanisms of success may illuminate what's missing in the failing systems
incomplete
The whole cross-retreat comparison rests on an acknowledged seam: the author read only the public summary of the earlier retreat, so he cannot separate 'the conversation genuinely grew' from 'the earlier summary simply compressed this out.' The central 'what changed' claim is offered on face value with that limitation showing.
What Survived Contact — read next: Obtain the fuller record (not just the public key-takeaways summary) of the earlier Thoughtworks retreat and re-run the comparison against like-for-like source material.
incomplete
The optimistic evidence is uncontrolled, single-team, self-reported metrics (a sample of one), which the author admits carries no conclusions; the piece names the asymmetry but does not offer a path to controlled, comparable evidence for the upside.
What Survived Contact — read next: Look for controlled or quasi-experimental studies of agentic-coding productivity and quality, and for methods that make single-team metrics comparable across teams.
Measuring AI Impact
incompleteclosed
The referenced-oracle technique is demonstrated only where a running reference implementation already existed (rebuilding Rails behavior in Roundhouse). The piece does not address how to construct a self-generating oracle for genuinely new behavior, where the intent lives only in a human's head and there is nothing to reference.
What Survived Contact — read next: Read on property-based and metamorphic testing, and on deriving executable oracles for green-field behavior where no reference system exists.
✓ closed byPodcast: Formal Methods for Every Engineer in an AI-Powered Future — g3 asks how to build a self-generating oracle for genuinely NEW behaviour, where no running reference implementation exists to reference an expected answer from live. Formal methods supply exactly that: instead of computing the expected answer from a pre-existing running system, the engineer authors an abstract formal model of the intended behaviour and uses model-based testing to replay the model's behaviours against the implementation — the authored model itself becomes the oracle, constructed from understanding before any reference implementation exists. (Note the mechanism difference from the referenced-oracle case: the answer is authored, not referenced live.)
Evolutionary Architecture & Fitness FunctionsSpec-Driven Development
illogical
The claim that the model's missing stake 'lives in the objective' assumes the checkable oracle fully captures what the human cared about. It doesn't examine the Goodhart case where a passable oracle diverges from the true, uncheckable intent — the model satisfies the letter of the objective while missing its point.
What Survived Contact — read next: Read on specification gaming, Goodhart's law, and reward hacking in agentic systems, and on how to detect an oracle that has drifted from intent.
EvalsGuardrails
incomplete
The 'small teams that never merge into one' scaling hypothesis rests on a three-person anecdote and does not address where plain issues-and-PRs coordination breaks down as the number of subsystems, their coupling, or team count grows.
What Survived Contact — read next: Read on coordination cost, Conway's law limits, and the failure modes of loosely federated subsystems at larger scale.
Coordination CostSmall Teams & Compression
incomplete
The episode focuses on Western/Anglophone naming traditions. No stories explore how names function across more radically different cultural or linguistic contexts, despite the host's opening mention of names shifting across language and distance.
What's in a Name: The Moth Podcast — read next: Comparative cultural studies on naming practices, or Moth episodes featuring naming stories from non-Western traditions.
incomplete
The philosophical question of whether naming shapes the essence of the named (raised in the introduction) is illustrated but not explored at depth. The stories show names shaping emotional experience and cultural trends, but the ontological question remains open.
What's in a Name: The Moth Podcast — read next: Philosophy of language — Kripke's 'Naming and Necessity,' Wittgenstein on language games, or Sapir-Whorf hypothesis on linguistic relativity.
incomplete
Elise's story of estrangement is presented entirely from her perspective. The father's side, the nature of the conflict, and any alternative interpretation of the family dynamics are absent — necessarily so for a personal storytelling format, but it limits the argument's universality.
What's in a Name: The Moth Podcast — read next: Not a flaw per se — The Moth's format is first-person narrative, not balanced journalism — but a reader should note the single-perspective framing.
incomplete
Selection and survivorship bias in the evidence: all three cases are famous winners who eventually lost, so the pattern is fitted to confirming instances. The essay never examines high performers whose harsh behavior was checked WHILE they were still winning, or abusers who failed early and were never prosecuted — so the claim that results are effectively the only variable is illustrated, not tested against disconfirming cases or base rates.
When Nothing Happens — read next: Comparative and base-rate evidence on when abusive-but-successful leaders are and are not sanctioned; cases of accountability arriving during a winning streak.
Hierarchy & Org ChartsTrust
incomplete
The causal relationship between harsh standards and good results is asserted away rather than examined. The whole argument depends on treating behavior as constant while results vary independently ('the behavior stayed the same'), but it never engages the possibility it raises and drops — the 'master motivator' reading — that in some cases the harshness partly produces the performance, which would move the normative verdict from 'unadmitted bet' toward 'defensible trade-off.'
When Nothing Happens — read next: Empirical literature on abusive supervision and performance — whether harsh leadership causally improves or degrades team outcomes, and under what conditions.
Performance PsychologyPsychological Safety
incomplete
The prescription stops at self-diagnosis. It tells the reader to write down a name and ask the harder question, but never says what to DO with the answer: whether and how to act on a tolerated high performer before the numbers force it, how to weigh their irreplaceable contribution against the harm, or how to break normalization of deviance without simply waiting for results to fade.
When Nothing Happens — read next: Practical methods for holding high-performing-but-harmful people accountable while retaining their value; interventions that reset an eroded behavioral standard.
Decision-Making PracticesPsychological Safety
incomplete
The account is written entirely from the observer's chair. The people harmed — the tearful 1:1s, the disrespected legal team, the choked player — appear only as instruments of the argument, with no attention to their voice, exit, or redress, and no account of remedy for the harm the machine normalizes.
When Nothing Happens — read next: Literature on organizational voice, whistleblowing, and abusive supervision from the target's perspective; how harmed parties surface or escape the normalized standard.
Burnout & RecoveryPsychological Safety
incomplete
The article prescribes self-awareness and reframing as the path to change but does not address how leaders actually develop the capacity for this — whether therapy, coaching, peer support, or structured practice is needed. The 'how to change' is significantly thinner than the 'what to notice'.
When You Start to Find Employee Requests Irritating — read next: Literature on adult attachment change, therapeutic mechanisms of change, and leadership development interventions (e.g., Kegan & Lahey's 'Immunity to Change')
incompleteclosed
The four-need taxonomy (safety, love, belonging, meaning) is presented as comprehensive but the article does not address how these map onto or differ from established frameworks like Deci & Ryan's self-determination theory (autonomy, competence, relatedness) or Maslow's hierarchy. The relationship is asserted but not argued.
When You Start to Find Employee Requests Irritating — read next: Compare with Deci & Ryan's Basic Psychological Needs Theory and assess whether 'love' and 'belonging' are genuinely distinct constructs or facets of 'relatedness'
✓ closed by5 Things High-Performing Teams Do Differently — That gap flags a four-need taxonomy (safety, love, belonging, meaning) left unmapped to established frameworks like Deci & Ryan. This article grounds workplace belonging squarely in Deci & Ryan's Self-Determination Theory — autonomy, competence, and especially relatedness — showing that 'belonging' corresponds to the relatedness need and connecting the folk taxonomy to the canonical SDT triad.
Hierarchy & Org Charts
uninformed
The article does not consider organizational or structural causes of irritation — e.g., that a leader may be genuinely under-resourced, poorly supported by their own leadership, or operating in a dysfunctional system. All irritation is attributed to psychological origin stories, which risks individualizing systemic problems.
When You Start to Find Employee Requests Irritating — read next: Organizational design literature, structural causes of manager burnout, systemic vs. individual attribution in leadership research
Burnout & Recovery
incomplete
The research citations (4,000-person survey, 3,381 interviews) are from the authors' forthcoming books and are not yet peer-reviewed or publicly available. The statistical claims (2.6x, 3.18x) cannot be independently evaluated.
When You Start to Find Employee Requests Irritating — read next: Await publication of the cited forthcoming books; seek peer-reviewed replications
illogical
The article moves from correlational survey findings ('those whose desires were supported were 3.18x more likely to report stronger mental health') to causal-prescriptive claims ('stabilize that need in yourself first') without acknowledging the inferential gap. Correlation between childhood experience and adult outcomes does not directly validate the proposed intervention.
When You Start to Find Employee Requests Irritating — read next: Intervention studies testing whether leader self-regulation of attachment-related triggers actually improves leadership effectiveness
evidentiary
The 100x token economics claim is asserted but not rigorously derived. Anthropic's revenue growth could reflect pricing strategy, API volume, or contract structure rather than inherent per-user value differences.
Who Cares About Consumer AI?
AI Unit Economics
analytical
The ads math assumes Google-level ad revenue per user is achievable for AI chat interfaces, but AI conversations are structurally different from search (fewer commercial-intent queries, different attention patterns). No evidence is given that AI ad inventory can match search ad inventory.
Who Cares About Consumer AI?
analytical
Chesky's consumer renaissance prediction is presented without interrogation. The structural barriers he identifies (no business model, design gap, trend-following culture) are not shown to have a clear resolution mechanism within 12-24 months.
Who Cares About Consumer AI?
scope
No discussion of non-US consumer AI markets, where ad economics differ radically and subscription willingness may be lower.
Who Cares About Consumer AI?
analytical
The newsletter does not consider whether consumer AI could generate value through data/behavioral signals rather than direct monetization, which is Meta's actual historical model.
Who Cares About Consumer AI?
incomplete
The four-source taxonomy is presented as exhaustive but no argument is made for completeness. Interpersonal dynamics (e.g., genuine personality clashes, political conflicts, or power struggles) are not addressed as a distinct category and do not fit neatly into the four sources.
Why Effective Leaders Get Branded as Problems
incomplete
The article provides no guidance for the common case where multiple friction sources are simultaneously primary. The diagnostic protocol treats them as distinguishable, but the simple heuristic at the end (consistent/recent = skill gap, outdated = drift, strong performance + friction = overextension, environment blocks change = system) does not address compound cases.
Why Effective Leaders Get Branded as Problems
uninformed
The claim that true skill deficits are the 'minority of situations at the senior level' rests entirely on the author's coaching experience. No data, frequency estimates, or external research is cited to support this base-rate claim, which is load-bearing for the entire argument.
Why Effective Leaders Get Branded as Problems
illogical
There is a circularity risk in the diagnostic protocol: the article argues that organizations default to blaming the individual, then provides a framework where three of four sources exonerate the individual. The framework may encode the author's coaching bias (clients are usually right, systems are usually wrong) as a structural feature rather than testing it.
Why Effective Leaders Get Branded as Problems
incomplete
The claim that AI-built systems are 'genuinely viable' for transactional backends and COBOL rewrites is asserted from others' reported experience without stating the criteria that mark a domain as suitable for full automation versus one that needs a human-maintained, legible codebase.
Why did Martin Fowler invite 60 engineers to a ski resort? — read next: Look for case studies and taxonomies of where 100%-AI-maintained systems succeed or fail by domain, coupling, and change-rate.
Coding AgentsLegacy & Organizational Forgetting
illogical
The argument that reduced engineer hiring reflects democratization (optimistic read) rather than declining demand (pessimistic read) rests on Robert C. Martin's developer-doubling extrapolation, which is rhetorical and does not actually adjudicate between the two readings — the same hiring data is consistent with both.
Why did Martin Fowler invite 60 engineers to a ski resort? — read next: Read labor-market analyses that separate 'demand for software' from 'demand for professional engineers' with evidence rather than extrapolation.
AI Labor DisruptionNon-Engineers Building
incomplete
The citizen-developer risks (security exposure and complacency) are vividly named but left entirely without proposed mitigation — the author declares the tension 'a genuinely open question' and offers no governance, guardrail, or review mechanism.
Why did Martin Fowler invite 60 engineers to a ski resort? — read next: Look for governance patterns for non-expert AI builders: access controls, human-in-the-loop checks, and complacency countermeasures.
Agent SecurityNon-Engineers Building
uninformed
The 'AIs have a short attention span' premise carries much of the weight of the good-for-AI-equals-good-for-humans argument but is asserted as intuition rather than substantiated, and no case is examined where AI-usable code might diverge from human-readable code.
Why did Martin Fowler invite 60 engineers to a ski resort? — read next: Seek empirical work on how context length, code structure, and readability affect agent task success, and on whether agent-optimal code ever differs from human-optimal code.
Code QualityContext Rot & Context Limits
incomplete
The article asserts that the agent layer is where coding competition now happens, but doesn't decompose what makes one harness materially better than another (context management, tool-use design, model-harness coupling, evals, ecosystem). For someone evaluating Claude Code vs. Antigravity vs. Codex on engineering merits, this is the question that matters most.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Addy Osmani on Claude Code agent patterns; Alex Wang on Claude Code setup quality; Anthropic engineering blog on Claude Code internals; any direct head-to-head harness eval (e.g. SWE-bench Verified with different harnesses on the same base model).
Agent HarnessClaude CodeCoding AgentsContext Engineering
incomplete
No serious treatment of how enterprise adoption of coding agents (procurement, security review, on-prem options, audit logging) might break the consumer-distribution argument — enterprise developer tools are a distinct moat from consumer MAU.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Pragmatic Engineer on enterprise AI adoption; DORA 2025 AI findings; pieces on agent governance in regulated industries.
Agent Governance (Internal)Agentic AICoding AgentsDORA & Engineering Metrics
incomplete
The 'distribution moat beats quality' implicit premise is not argued. History has counter-examples (Google+ failed despite distribution; iPhone won despite Microsoft's distribution). The conditions under which distribution wins vs. loses are not specified.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Ben Thompson / Stratechery on aggregation theory and distribution limits; case studies of distribution failures (Bing vs. Google, Google+ vs. Facebook).
Moats & Defensibility
incomplete
The article treats 'recursive self-improvement' as a coherent strategic bet without examining whether Anthropic's use of Claude to accelerate pretraining research is qualitatively different from what every lab is doing internally (Google uses Gemini, OpenAI uses GPT). The Karpathy-framed bet may be more rhetorical than substantive.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Anthropic's published work on using Claude in research; comparable disclosures from OpenAI/Google DeepMind on internal model use; Gary Marcus or other skeptics on recursive self-improvement claims.
Recursive Self-Improvement
incomplete
No analysis of the cost-economics shift the article gestures at. If token cost is becoming the dominant axis, what does the economic landscape look like — TPU/GPU constraints, inference architecture (Baseten-style serving), open-weights pressure (DeepSeek)? Treating cost as 'the dominant concern' without unpacking it leaves the central economic claim under-evidenced.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Baseten/Together inference economics; Aaron Levie on enterprise token costs; DeepSeek pricing analysis; Simon Willison on per-token economics across labs.
AI Unit Economics
incomplete
Demis's 'different game' is asserted but not connected to any actionable framework for evaluating Google. If world-models / AGI is the real bet, what would success look like in 18 months? Without success criteria, the 'different game' framing is unfalsifiable and conveniently insulates Google from any near-term scoreboard.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: DeepMind publications on world models (Genie series); critical takes on AGI timelines (Marcus, Mitchell); comparisons of Anthropic's safety/RSP framework vs. DeepMind's published commitments.
The Genie (Beck)
misinformed
The article calls coining 'vibe coding' Karpathy's most prominent public contribution. This understates his substantive influence (nanoGPT, neural network educational content, Tesla autopilot architecture). Suggests the author is writing for an audience that knows the meme but not the work — and may carry that same shallowness into other technical claims.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Karpathy's own writing (Software 2.0 essay, nanoGPT, his lecture series). Useful to calibrate when this newsletter's technical depth is reliable.
Vibe Coding
uninformed
No examination of where Google's coding-agent investment is actually flowing inside the company (Jules, Gemini Code Assist, Antigravity, Android CLI all coexist). Sprawl could be a sign of internal competition rather than absence of priority — the article treats sprawl as monocausal incoherence.
Why Google Isn't Chasing Claude Code — read next: Internal Google AI org coverage (The Information, Business Insider); the Sergey 'strike team' reporting referenced; Gemini Code Assist roadmap.
incomplete
The remedy is almost entirely individual (introspect, de-identify, prioritize, perform calm) while the diagnosed causes are largely structural (a field with no gold standard, an over-productive theory/benchmark industry, AI-era layoff anxiety). She never connects the two — if the culture reliably manufactures identity-fusion and 'burnout that feels like success', individual mindset shifts treat symptoms while the generating system persists.
Why product feels so hard right now - Charity Ibhadon (WPP) — read next: Read against extract-burnout-across-org-chart (burnout as a systemic design failure requiring level-specific structural fixes) and extract-overcome-burnout-philosophy (the same structural-diagnosis-with-individual-remedy tension named explicitly via Byung-Chul Han's 'achievement society').
Burnout & RecoveryProfessional Identity
incomplete
The 'happy high status' prescription assumes performed composure the leader does not feel is sustainable and cost-free, but suppression of one's actual state is itself a recognised driver of depletion — so the composure remedy may quietly reproduce the 'looks fine, feels terrible' dynamic she identifies as burnout-that-feels-like-success. The cost side of leaderly calm is never examined.
Why product feels so hard right now - Charity Ibhadon (WPP) — read next: Emotional-labour and surface-acting research (Hochschild) on the depletion cost of performed emotion; recovery/detachment models (Sonnentag) on why suppression blocks the recovery she elsewhere prescribes.
Burnout & RecoveryPsychological Safety
incomplete
Recovery is credited to a highly privileged intervention (a three-month break, horse riding in Ecuador) that she herself flags as unavailable to most; the scaled-down substitutes she offers (a quieter Friday, an introspective Saturday, a monthly gallery visit) are asserted to work but never argued to be sufficient against a structurally over-demanding job.
Why product feels so hard right now - Charity Ibhadon (WPP) — read next: Evidence on micro-recovery / daily detachment versus extended leave; whether small non-instrumental breaks meaningfully offset chronic overload or merely make it tolerable.
Burnout & Recovery
incomplete
She prescribes 'be more human, be commercially curious, be nice to work with' as the durable AI-era edge but offers no account of which of these are developable skills versus fixed dispositions, nor how someone would train them — leaving the advice motivational rather than actionable, and vulnerable to the same 'no clear starting point' complaint she levels at the framework literature.
Why product feels so hard right now - Charity Ibhadon (WPP) — read next: extract-ai-worker-identity-archetypes and the meta-skills / contextual-judgment literature on which durable capabilities are teachable; commercial-acumen development pathways for product roles.
Meta-Skills & Contextual JudgmentProfessional Identity
incomplete
No explicit threat model. The article enumerates sandbox capabilities but never names the adversary: is it a confused agent, a malicious plugin author, a prompt-injection chain, or a curious user? Each implies different controls and different acceptable risk.
Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — read next: Anthropic's 'How we contain Claude' (linked in the same newsletter) provides an explicit threat-model decomposition; pair with Willison's earlier Lethal Trifecta essay for an integrated view.
Lethal TrifectaSandboxing
incomplete
Sandboxing solves blast-radius questions but not authority questions. The Meta example (linked in the same issue) shows that the catastrophic failure is granting agents excessive *sanctioned* power — which a perfectly secure sandbox does nothing to prevent. The article never connects these.
Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — read next: Read the Meta item against Anthropic containment essay against this piece syntopically; ask 'what authority does the code inside the sandbox have, and who decided?'
Sandboxing
incomplete
Pyodide is dismissed for server-side use on the basis of a 2024 statement; no engagement with running Pyodide via Node embeddings, with PyPy-WASM, RustPython, Starlark, or restricted-Python variants. The 'survey of alternatives' is thin given the strength of the resulting claim.
Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — read next: Recent Pyodide release notes, RustPython status, the Bytecode Alliance's component-model work for cross-language sandboxing.
Sandboxing
incomplete
No discussion of side channels or supply-chain risk in the distributed 362KB WASM blob. A prebuilt opaque artifact in a PyPI package is itself a trust delegation, and reproducible builds are not addressed.
Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — read next: WebAssembly component-model security analyses; reproducible-build practices for prebuilt native artifacts on PyPI.
Trust
incomplete
The cost/discipline thread (Whitacre, Wilson, Majors, Uber caps) is present in the newsletter but disconnected from the lead piece. Willison's sandbox lowers the cost of spinning up agent-executed code; the roundup says that's already the bottleneck. The unity does not acknowledge that making sandboxed execution cheaper might make the discipline problem worse.
Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM — read next: Majors's enthusiasts/skeptics piece; David Wilson's 'cancelling my AI subscription'; treat them as a counterweight when evaluating sandbox-as-enabler.
Sandboxing
incomplete
Willison identifies that the SDLC must restructure but does not address the downstream bottlenecks — if you produce 10x more code, testing, review, deployment, monitoring, and incident response must also scale, and it is unclear whether they can. The essay focuses on code production and design but says nothing about ops, security review, or organizational change management.
Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like — read next: Charity Majors on observability-driven development; Google's DORA research on deployment frequency vs. stability; Will Larson on engineering org design under scaling pressure.
DORA & Engineering MetricsSRE & Incident Response
incomplete
The 'usage as trust signal' argument assumes that usage reveals bugs, but many serious defects (security vulnerabilities, data races, subtle data corruption) can lurk for months or years without being triggered by normal usage. Usage is a better signal than artifact appearance, but it is not a sufficient signal for safety-critical properties.
Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like — read next: Literature on latent defects in software; Nancy Leveson's work on system safety; the Therac-25 case.
Trust
incomplete
The essay treats the accountability gap between human teams and AI agents as an acknowledged discomfort but does not explore solutions — contractual liability, organizational accountability structures, or the possibility that AI vendors could offer guarantees or insurance for agent-produced code.
Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like — read next: Legal scholarship on AI liability; Anthropic's own responsible scaling policy; discussions of AI insurance markets.
Agentic AI
incomplete
Willison's 'amplifier of expertise' framing implies that junior engineers benefit less from AI tools, but the essay does not address the apprenticeship problem: if AI handles the straightforward tasks that juniors traditionally learn on, how do new engineers develop the expertise that makes AI an amplifier?
Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like — read next: Matt Rickard and Addy Osmani on the junior engineer pipeline; apprenticeship models in medicine and law for comparison.
scope limitation
The lecture focuses entirely on in-person, live speaking. It does not address video presentations, remote/virtual talks, podcasts, or any mediated format — despite these being the dominant communication modes in many professional settings.
How to Speak — read next: Compare with resources on virtual presentation (e.g., Nancy Duarte, Garr Reynolds) for the digital equivalent.
evidence gap
The f(K,P,T) formula and the 20% fog-out rate are presented as facts but without citation. The empathetic mirroring / mirror neuron hypothesis is self-labeled 'lunatic fringe' but treated as explanatory. Winston's Star is derived from a single self-referential case study.
How to Speak — read next: Look into research on cognitive load theory (Sweller), dual-coding theory (Paivio), and the replication status of mirror neuron claims for more rigorous grounding.
Cognitive Load
cultural bias
The advice is deeply situated in MIT / American academic culture. The 'don't say thank you' advice, the preference for humor, the informality of anecdotes — these may not transfer to other cultures, industries, or power dynamics (e.g., a junior person presenting to senior executives in Japan).
How to Speak — read next: Cross-reference with Erin Meyer's 'The Culture Map' for how communication norms vary across cultures.
omission
Winston claims speaking > writing > ideas in determining success but never argues for this ordering or addresses the obvious counterexamples (great writers who are poor speakers, ideas that succeed on merit despite poor presentation).
How to Speak — read next: Examine whether this is a rhetorical exaggeration designed to motivate the audience or a genuine empirical claim.
implicit premise
The entire lecture assumes that the goal of speaking is persuasion and memorability — that you want your ideas to 'win.' It does not consider speaking as a tool for collective inquiry, dialogue, or epistemic humility. The framework is fundamentally adversarial/competitive.
How to Speak — read next: Compare with Bohm's 'On Dialogue' or Scharmer's 'Theory U' for communication frameworks oriented toward shared understanding rather than persuasion.
Multiplayer vs Single-Player AI
omission
No discussion of Flippa's role or responsibility. The marketplace that facilitated the ownership transfer is named but never examined as a potential point of intervention. Should digital marketplaces require security reviews or cooling-off periods for plugin/package sales?
Attacker Bought 30 WordPress Plugins on Flippa and Backdoored All of Them
underdeveloped
The article states the purchase was 'six figures' but does not explore the economics of the attack. What is the expected ROI of SEO spam injection at 400K-site scale? Understanding the economics would clarify why this pattern will intensify.
Attacker Bought 30 WordPress Plugins on Flippa and Backdoored All of Them
omission
No examination of why WordPress.org has not adopted the countermeasures that npm and PyPI implemented. Is it governance structure, technical debt, community resistance, or something else? The comparison is presented as self-evidently damning without exploring root causes.
Attacker Bought 30 WordPress Plugins on Flippa and Backdoored All of Them
Technical Debt
omission
The article does not address whether the 400,000 affected site owners were notified directly, or what the actual remediation rate was. The forced update fixed the phone-home but not the injected code — how many sites remain compromised months later?
Attacker Bought 30 WordPress Plugins on Flippa and Backdoored All of Them
underdeveloped
The blockchain C2 technique is mentioned but its broader implications are not explored. If attackers routinely move C2 to smart contracts, what does this mean for the entire domain-takedown model of incident response?
Attacker Bought 30 WordPress Plugins on Flippa and Backdoored All of Them
SRE & Incident Response
incomplete
The article asserts that ecosystem maturity/standardization makes XML 'superior' but never defines the criteria of superiority or weighs them against JSON's decisive real-world advantages — network effects, JavaScript-ecosystem gravity, and cognitive simplicity — which are the actual reasons formats win adoption. 'Superiority' is treated as a property of the format in isolation rather than of the format-in-its-ecosystem.
Worse is better: JSON versus XML — read next: Read Richard Gabriel's original 'The Rise of Worse Is Better' — it argues that the 'worse' option's simplicity of interface is itself the deeper virtue, directly challenging the 'why reinvent the wheel' framing — alongside the network-effects and standards-adoption literature.
Worse Is Better
illogical
The recurring 'why reinvent the wheel?' treats JSON's re-implementation of XML capabilities as pure waste, but the very fact that the ecosystem chose to reinvent a SIMPLER wheel is evidence that XML's richer wheel was mis-sized for the common case. The argument thus assumes what it needs to prove — that XML's heavier standards are worth their cost — and this directly contradicts the Gabriel 'worse is better' thesis Seemann himself invokes as his framing device (a tension the article never acknowledges).
Worse is better: JSON versus XML — read next: Engage Gabriel's actual claim that simplicity of the common case can rightly dominate completeness, and read the design-fundamentals argument that unused capability is a cost, not a latent asset.
Worse Is Better
incomplete
The size argument is dismissed too quickly — 'when performance really is a consideration, JSON may be too big, too' waves away rather than engages the large middle band of high-throughput text APIs (aggregate bandwidth, parse cost, streaming) and the schema-driven binary formats that are actually chosen when neither text format fits.
Worse is better: JSON versus XML — read next: Read the schema-driven binary serialization literature — Protocol Buffers, Apache Avro, Cap'n Proto, MessagePack — to see where the real interchange-format frontier lies beyond the XML-vs-JSON text axis.
incomplete
The Hyrum's-law/implicit-contract point is used only against JSON, but it cuts both ways: XML with an XSD still couples consumers to undocumented behaviors, and a schema is not a free lunch — XSD complexity, schema evolution, and versioning cost are never weighed against the benefit claimed.
Worse is better: JSON versus XML — read next: Read on schema evolution and compatibility (e.g. Avro/Protobuf compatibility rules, and API-versioning practice) to see the ongoing cost side of the schema ledger the article omits.
incomplete
The configuration-file case rests almost entirely on JSON's lack of standard comments, yet never mentions YAML, TOML, or HCL — the formats that actually displaced both XML and JSON for configuration — which makes the 'XML for config' recommendation weaker and more dated than presented.
Worse is better: JSON versus XML — read next: Compare TOML and YAML directly on the same criteria (comments, readability, schema tooling) to see why the config-format question was largely settled without either XML or JSON winning.
incomplete
The article never operationalizes what a Skill IS at the runtime level. Persistent across conversations could mean (a) system-prompt fragment retrieved by trigger, (b) tool entry in the model's available toolset, (c) per-account fine-tuned adapter, (d) execution graph stored against the account. Each option has very different governance, observability, and failure-mode profiles.
xAI Releases Grok Skills and Updates Tool Calling Responses API — read next: Pair with the Google Genkit middleware extract — Genkit's middleware metaphor and Skills-as-runtime-artifact are unrelated abstractions that both claim to wrap agent execution. Worth a syntopical pass: what is the actual unit of customization across these vendors?
Agent Skills
incomplete
The Grok-vs-OpenAI-vs-Claude-vs-Vercel comparison is asserted but not analyzed structurally. The article uses workflow/agent/developer-extension as distinguishing terms without defining them. The user's recent extractions (Anthropic dev newsletter, Console.dev, Genkit) provide the comparative scaffolding the article lacks.
xAI Releases Grok Skills and Updates Tool Calling Responses API — read next: Direct syntopical reading: extract-anthropic-developer-newsletter-2026-05.json + extract-google-genkit-middleware.json + extract-console-dev-best-tools-and-betas.json + this piece. Central question: 'What is a Skill?' across vendors.
Agent HarnessAgent SkillsAgent Tool ManagementAI Workflow Patterns
incomplete
Tight X-platform integration is offered as the moat, but the article doesn't engage what happens to Skills tied to X if a user multi-homes across LLM vendors (the norm for power users). The implicit premise — that X integration is sticky enough to outweigh model-quality and tool-ecosystem competition — is undefended.
xAI Releases Grok Skills and Updates Tool Calling Responses API — read next: AI Daily Brief on Google v Claude Code (already extracted) — same question of platform moats vs. model quality; xAI is making the inverse bet.
Agent SkillsClaude CodeMoats & Defensibility
incomplete
Governance is absent. AWS MCP extraction surfaced 'IAM-bounded ≠ governed' as the load-bearing over-claim; xAI's piece doesn't even attempt the IAM analogy. Account-level Skills with cross-platform persistence and tool access (William Wallace's GitHub-connected example) raise auditability and credential-isolation questions the article doesn't acknowledge.
xAI Releases Grok Skills and Updates Tool Calling Responses API — read next: Pair with the AWS MCP GA extract — same agent-tooling category, very different governance surface.
Agent Skills
incomplete
He asserts taste cannot be tested but never addresses how taste is developed or transmitted. He brings pre-existing taste to every judgment (the population-map problem, the anglophone-Wikipedia bias, the suspect Reykjavik=10) yet treats it as a given human endowment — silent on how a novice, a team, or the next generation would acquire or inherit it, especially once AI removes the on-ramp tasks that used to build it.
You can't unit test for taste — read next: Read Polanyi on tacit knowledge and Dreyfus on skill acquisition (novice-to-expert); pair with the corpus's junior-apprenticeship thread (heis-09, heis-12, ki-utvikling-og-folelser) and Beck 'Tidy First?' / Ousterhout on design judgment as cultivated rather than innate. Ask: does taste transmit at all when AI removes the practice that used to form it?
Organizational LegibilityTaste & Judgment
incomplete
He confines 'you can't unit test for taste' to messy data and treats code as the safely 'verifiable domain,' but never turns the lens onto AI-generated code itself, where tests pass yet design taste — simplicity, deep modules, restraint — has no red/green check. As agents generate more test-passing code, the untestable-taste problem migrates into the codebase, not away from it.
You can't unit test for taste — read next: Read Ousterhout 'A Philosophy of Software Design,' Beck 'Tidy First?,' and Hickey 'Simple Made Easy' on design quality as unassertable; cross with corpus docs agents-dont-know-what-good-looks-like (behavioral vs capability verification) and cognitive-surrender (surface-correctness signals). Test the claim: is a green build ever evidence of good design?
Code QualitySoftware Design FundamentalsTaste & Judgment
incomplete
He delegates the 'subjective' rating to the LLM's latent weights and finds it useful, but never examines whether that latent 'taste' is genuine judgment or averaged internet popularity — i.e., whether outsourcing scoring to a model launders the very anglophone-Wikipedia bias he diagnosed a section earlier into a 'subjective score' he can no longer see or audit.
You can't unit test for taste — read next: Read on models as reflections of their training distribution (Bender et al.) and on popularity-as-proxy-for-quality critiques; probe the LLM ratings against non-anglophone sources to see whether the 'taste' is judgment or a re-encoding of who edits Wikipedia.
Code QualityTaste & Judgment
incomplete
Between the red/green unit test and pure manual taste sits a graded-evaluation middle path — evals, rubric grading, LLM-as-judge — which he names ('I'm sure there are frameworks and evals') but does not develop or attempt. The essay's verification story therefore skips the very tooling that partially addresses its central problem.
You can't unit test for taste — read next: Read the evals literature (LLM-as-judge, rubric-based and reference-free evaluation) and the corpus's evals concept; ask how far graded, probabilistic evaluation can substitute for taste before genuine human judgment is still required — and precisely where it cannot.
EvalsTaste & Judgment
incomplete
The 'you don't get to create anything' extension to AI is never made explicit
You Don't Get to Create Anything (Still Burning: Beck + Randy Shoup on Jevons paradox and distributed systems under AI) — read next: Beck's own Substack on 'starving genies' and the human's relationship to the genie's outputs; Cal Newport's deep-work tradition on how the substitution of tool-mediated production changes the nature of the work; Pressfield on Resistance; and — the persona-relevant cross-thread — the 'fragility of ideas' hidden layer in Kjetil's reading: if the maker-compulsion is the durable substrate, what does it look like when the agent satisfies it without you having moved? This is the productive question the episode leaves on the table.
AI Employee vs Tool FramingThe Genie (Beck)Intrinsic Motivation & Making
incomplete
The Jevons-on-cognition forecast is asserted, not argued through to its qualitative differences
You Don't Get to Create Anything (Still Burning: Beck + Randy Shoup on Jevons paradox and distributed systems under AI) — read next: Carl Benedikt Frey 'The Technology Trap' on the political economy of transitional displacement; Brad DeLong on what 'creating new categories of work' has actually looked like historically; Anton Korinek and Daron Acemoglu on AI-and-labor specifically. For the contrarian side: Erik Brynjolfsson on the J-curve (gains that show up late and unevenly); the Robin Hanson 'Age of Em' tradition for the qualitative-difference-of-cognition objection. Persona-relevant: DORA 2025 on AI's actual code-quality effects.

incomplete
The playbook claim under-specifies the AI playbook's structure

This is exactly where 'craft transmission for the next era' lives — and where the conversation is most incomplete.

Shoup's 'make it easy, don't shame' principle is stated as climate policy and not applied to software craft
